clause.title: Copyright
clause.p: Even if electronic versions are available free of charge on the website of one or more of the JCGM member organizations, economic and moral copyrights related to all JCGM publications are internationally protected. The JCGM does not, without its written authorisation, permit third parties to rewrite or re-brand issues, to sell copies to the public, or to broadcast or use on-line its publications. Equally, the JCGM also objects to distortion, augmentation or mutilation of its publications, including its titles, slogans and logos, and those of its member organizations.

clause.title: Official versions and translations
clause.p: The only official versions of documents are those published by the JCGM, in their original languages.

The JCGM’s publications may be translated into languages other than those in which the documents were originally published by the JCGM. Permission must be obtained from the JCGM before a translation can be made. All translations should respect the original and official format of the formulae and units (without any conversion to other formulae or units), and contain the following statement (to be translated into the chosen language):

quote.p: All JCGM’s products are internationally protected by copyright. This translation of the original JCGM document has been produced with the permission of the JCGM. The JCGM retains full internationally protected copyright on the design and content of this document and on the JCGM’s titles, slogan and logos. The member organizations of the JCGM also retain full internationally protected right on their titles, slogans and logos included in the JCGM’s publications. The only official version is the document published by the JCGM, in the original languages.

clause.p: The JCGM does not accept any liability for the relevance, accuracy, completeness or quality of the information and materials offered in any translation. A copy of the translation shall be provided to the JCGM at the time of publication.

clause.title: Reproduction
clause.p: The JCGM’s publications may be reproduced, provided written permission has been granted by the JCGM. A sample of any reproduced document shall be provided to the JCGM at the time of reproduction and contain the following statement:A

The member organizations of the JCGM also retain full internationally protected right on their titles, slogans and logos included in the JCGM’s publications. The only official versions are the original versions of the documents published by the JCGM.

clause.title: Disclaimer
clause.p: The JCGM and its member organizations have published this document to enhance access to information about metrology. They endeavor to update it on a regular basis, but cannot guarantee the accuracy at all times and shall not be responsible for any direct or indirect damage that may result from its use. Any reference to commercial products of any kind (including but not restricted to any software, data or hardware) or links to websites, over which the JCGM and its member organizations have no control and for which they assume no responsibility, does not imply any approval, endorsement or recommendation by the JCGM and its member organizations.

foreword.title: Foreword
foreword.p: In 1977, recognizing the lack of international consensus on the expression of uncertainty in measurement, the world’s highest authority in metrology, the Comité International des Poids et Mesures (CIPM), requested the Bureau International des Poids et Mesures (BIPM) to address the problem in conjunction with the national standards laboratories and to make a recommendation.

The BIPMBIPM prepared a detailed questionnaire covering the issues involved and distributed it to 32 national metrology laboratories known to have an interest in the subject (and, for information, to five international organizations). By early 1979 responses were received from 21 laboratories
See the .
Footnote to the 2008 version:
In producing this 2008 version of the GUM, necessary corrections only to the printed 1995 version have been introduced by JCGM/WG 1. These corrections occur in subclauses 4.2.2, 4.2.4, 5.1.2, B.2.17, C.3.2, C.3.4, E.4.3, H.4.3, H.5.2.5 and H.6.2. .

Almost all believed that it was important to arrive at an internationally accepted procedure for expressing measurement uncertainty and for combining individual uncertainty components into a single total uncertainty. However, a consensus was not apparent on the method to be used. The BIPM then convened a meeting for the purpose of arriving at a uniform and generally acceptable procedure for the specification of uncertainty; it was attended by experts from 11 national standards laboratories. This Working Group on the Statement of UncertaintiesWorking Group on the Statement of Uncertainties developed Recommendation INC-1 (1980Recommendation INC-1 (1980), Expression of Experimental Uncertainties . The CIPMCIPM approved the Recommendation in 1981 and reaffirmed it in 1986 .

The task of developing a detailed guide based on the Working Group Recommendation (which is a brief outline rather than a detailed prescription) was referred by the CIPM to the International Organization for Standardization (ISO), since ISO could better reflect the needs arising from the broad interests of industry and commerce.

Responsibility was assigned to the ISO Technical Advisory Group on Metrology (TAG 4) because one of its tasks is to coordinate the development of guidelines on measurement topics that are of common interest to ISO and the six organizations that participate with ISO in the work of TAG 4: the International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC), the partner of ISO in worldwide standardization; the CIPM and the International Organization of Legal Metrology (OIML), the two worldwide metrology organizations; the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC) and the International Union of Pure and Applied Physics (IUPAP), the two international unions that represent chemistry and physics; and the International Federation of Clinical Chemistry (IFCC).IUPACIUPAPIECIFCCISOISO/TAG 4OIML

TAG 4 in turn established Working Group 3 (ISO/TAG 4/WG 3Working Group 3 (ISO/TAG 4/WG 3) composed of experts nominated by the BIPM, IEC, ISO, and OIML and appointed by the Chairman of TAG 4. It was assigned the following terms of reference:ISO/TAG 4/WG 3ISO/TAG 4/WG 3terms of reference ofISO Technical Advisory Group on Metrology (ISO/TAG 4)

To develop a guidance document based upon the recommendation of the BIPM Working Group on the Statement of Uncertainties which provides rules on the expression of measurement uncertainty for use within standardization, calibration, laboratory accreditation, and metrology services;laboratoriesnational metrology or standards
The purpose of such guidance is

li.p: to promote full information on how uncertainty statements are arrived at;
to provide a basis for the international comparison of measurement results.

foreword.p: This corrected version of JCGM 100:2008 incorporates the following corrections:

li.p: on page v, Footnote ** has been corrected;

in the first lines of B.2 and C.2, Clause 0 has been corrected to Clause 2;
in G.3.2, (G,1c) has been changed to (G.1c);
in H.1.3.4, the formatting of the first equation has been improved.

clause.p: When reporting the result of a measurement of a physical quantity, it is obligatory that some quantitativeindication of the quality of the result be given so that those who use it can assess its reliability. Without such an indication, measurement results cannot be compared, either among themselves or with reference values given in a specification or standard. It is therefore necessary that there be a readily implemented, easily understood, and generally accepted procedure for characterizing the quality of a result of a measurement, that is, for evaluating and expressing its uncertainty.uncertainty of measurement

The concept of uncertainty as a quantifiable attribute is relatively new in the history of measurement, although errorerror of measurement and error analysiserror analysis have long been a part of the practice of measurement science or metrology. It is now widely recognized that, when all of the known or suspected components of error have been evaluated and the appropriate corrections have been applied, there still remains an uncertainty about the correctness of the stated result, that is, a doubt about how well the result of the measurement represents the value of the quantity being measured.uncertainty of measurement

Just as the nearly universal use of the International System of Units (SIInternational System of Units (SI) has brought coherence to all scientific and technological measurements, a worldwide consensus on the evaluation and expression of uncertainty in measurement would permit the significance of a vast spectrum of measurement results in science, engineering, commerce, industry, and regulation to be readily understood and properly interpreted. In this era of the global marketplace, it is imperative that the method for evaluating and expressing uncertainty be uniform throughout the world so that measurements performed in different countries can be easily compared.

The ideal method for evaluating and expressing the uncertainty of the result of a measurement should be:uncertaintyideal method for evaluating and expressinguncertaintyinternally consistent quantity for expressinguncertaintytransferable quantity for expressinguncertaintyuniversal method for evaluating and expressing

li.p: universal: the method should be applicable to all kinds of measurements and to all types of input dataused in measurements.

clause.p: The actual quantity used to express uncertainty should be:

li.p: internally consistent: it should be directly derivable from the components that contribute to it, as well asindependent of how these components are grouped and of the decomposition of the components into subcomponents;
transferable: it should be possible to use directly the uncertainty evaluated for one result as a componentin evaluating the uncertainty of another measurement in which the first result is used.

clause.p: Further, in many industrial and commercial applications, as well as in the areas of health and safety, it is often necessary to provide an interval about the measurement result that may be expected to encompass a large fraction of the distribution of values that could reasonably be attributed to the quantity subject to measurement. Thus the ideal method for evaluating and expressing uncertainty in measurement should be capable of readily providing such an interval, in particular, one with a coverage probabilitycoverage probability or level of confidencelevel of confidence that corresponds in a realistic way with that required.

The approach upon which this guidance document is based is that outlined in Recommendation INC-1 (1980Recommendation INC-1 (1980) of the Working Group on the Statement of UncertaintiesWorking Group on the Statement of Uncertainties, which was convened by the BIPMBIPM in response to a request of the CIPM (see ). This approach, the justification of which is discussed in , meets all of the requirements outlined above. This is not the case for most other methods in current use. Recommendation INC-1 (1980) was approved and reaffirmed by the CIPM in its own Recommendations 1 (CI-1981)Recommendation 1 (CI-1981), CIPM and 1 (CI-1986)Recommendation 1 (CI-1986), CIPM ; the English translations of these CIPMCIPM Recommendations are reproduced in (see and , respectively). Because Recommendation INC-1 (1980) is the foundation upon which this document rests, the English translation is reproduced in and the French text, which is authoritative, is reproduced in .

A succinct summary of the procedure specified in this guidance document for evaluating andex pressing uncertainty in measurement is given in and a number of examples are presented in detail in . Other annexes deal with general terms in metrology (); basic statistical terms and concepts (); “true” value, error, and uncertainty (); practical suggestions for evaluating uncertainty components (); degrees of freedom and levels of confidence (); the principal mathematical symbols used throughout the document (); and bibliographical references (). An alphabetical index concludes the document.
Recommendation INC-1 (1980Recommendation INC-1 (1980) Expression of experimental uncertainties

li.p: The uncertainty in the result of a measurement generally consists of several components which may be grouped into two categories according to the way in which their numerical value is estimated:
those which are evaluated by statistical methods,
those which are evaluated by other means.

There is not always a simple correspondence between the classification into categories A or B and the previously used classification into “random” and “systematic” uncertainties. The term “systematic uncertainty” can be misleading and should be avoided.
Any detailed report of the uncertainty should consist of a complete list of the components, specifying for each the method used to obtain its numerical value.

The components in category A are characterized by the estimated variances s i 2 s_i^2, (or the estimated “standard deviations” s i s_i) and the number of degrees of freedom ν i ii(nu)_i. Where appropriate, the covariances should be given.

The components in category B should be characterized by quantities u j 2 u_j^2, which may be considered as approximations to the corresponding variances, the existence of which is assumed. The quantities u j 2 u_j^2 may be treated like variances and the quantities u j u_j like standard deviations. Where appropriate, the covariances should be treated in a similar way.

The combined uncertainty should be characterized by the numerical value obtained by applying the usual method for the combination of variances. The combined uncertainty and its components should be expressed in the form of “standard deviations”.
If, for particular applications, it is necessary to multiply the combined uncertainty by a factor to obtain an overall uncertainty, the multiplying factor used must always be stated.

clause.title: Preliminary content
clause.p: This Guide establishes general rules for evaluating and expressing uncertainty in measurement that are intended to be applicable to a broad spectrum of measurements. The basis of the Guide is Recommendation 1 (CI-1981)Recommendation 1 (CI-1981), CIPM of the Comité International des Poids et Mesures (CIPM) and Recommendation INC-1 (1980Recommendation INC-1 (1980) of the Working Group on the Statement of UncertaintiesWorking Group on the Statement of Uncertainties. The Working Group was convened by the Bureau International des Poids et Mesures (BIPM) in response to a request of the CIPMCIPM. The CIPM Recommendation is the only recommendation concerning the expression of uncertainty in measurement adopted by an intergovernmental organization.

This Guide was prepared by a joint working group consisting of experts nominated by the BIPMBIPM, the International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC), the International Organization for Standardization (ISO), and the International Organization of Legal Metrology (OIML).

The following seven organizations
Footnote to the 2008 version:
In 2005, the International Laboratory Accreditation Cooperation (ILAC) officially joined the seven founding international organizations. supported the development of this Guide, which is published in their name:

dd.p: Bureau International des Poids et Mesures
International Electrotechnical Commission

International Federation of Clinical Chemistry
Footnote to the 2008 version:
The name of this organization has changed since 1995. It is now:
IFCC: International Federation of Clinical Chemistry and Laboratory Medicine
International Organization for Standardization
International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry
International Union of Pure and Applied Physics
International Organization of Legal Metrology

clause.p:
Users of this Guide are invited to send their comments and requests for clarification to any of the seven supporting organizations, the mailing addresses of which are given on the inside front cover
Footnote to the 2008 version:
Links to the addresses of the eight organizations presently involved in the JCGM (Joint Committee for Guides in Metrology) are given on . .

This Guide establishes general rules for evaluating and expressing uncertainty in measurement thatcan be followed at various levels of accuracy and in many fields — from the shop floor to fundamental research. Therefore, the principles of this Guide are intended to be applicable to a broad spectrum of measurements, including those required for:measurementsspectrum ofto which the principles of the Guide applyuncertainty of measurement

li.p: maintaining quality control and quality assurance in production; complying with and enforcing laws and regulations;
conducting basic research, and applied research and development, in science and engineering;
calibrating standards and instruments and performing tests throughout a national measurement system in order to achieve traceability to national standards;
developing, maintaining, and comparing international and national physical reference standards, including reference materials.

clause.p: This Guide is primarily concerned with the expression of uncertainty in the measurement of a well-defined physical quantity — the measurandmeasurand — that can be characterized by an essentially unique value. If the phenomenon of interest can be represented only as a distribution of values or is dependent on one or more parameters, such as time, then the measurands required for its description are the set of quantities describing that distribution or that dependence.

This Guide is also applicable to evaluating and expressing the uncertainty associated with the conceptual design and theoretical analysis of experiments, methods of measurement, and complex components and systems. Because a measurement result and its uncertainty may be conceptual and based entirely on hypothetical data, the term “result of a measurement”result of a measurement as used in this Guide should be interpreted in this broader context.

This Guide provides general rules for evaluating and expressing uncertainty in measurement rather than detailed, technology-specific instructions. Further, it does not discuss how the uncertainty of a particular measurement result, once evaluated, may be used for different purposes, for example, to draw conclusions about the compatibility of that result with other similar results, to establish tolerance limits in a manufacturing process, or to decide if a certain course of action may be safely undertaken. It may therefore be necessary to develop particular standards based on this Guide that deal with the problems peculiar to specific fields of measurement or with the various uses of quantitative expressions of uncertainty.
Footnote to the 2008 version:
Since the initial publication of this Guide, several general and specific applications documents derived from this document have been published. For information purposes, nonexhaustive compilations of these documents can be found on . . These standards may be simplified versions of this Guide but should include the detail that is appropriate to the level of accuracy and complexity of the measurements and uses addressed.
There may be situations in which the concept of uncertainty of measurement is believed not to be fully applicable, such as when the precision of a test method is determined (see Reference for example).

clause.title: General metrological terms
clause.p:
The definition of a number of general metrological terms relevant to this Guide, such as “measurable quantity”, “measurand”, and “error of measurement”, are given in . These definitions are taken from the International vocabulary of basic and general terms in metrology (abbreviated VIM)
Footnote to the 2008 version:
The third edition of the vocabulary was published in 2008, under the title JCGM 200:2008, International vocabulary of metrology — Basic and general concepts and associated terms (VIM). . In addition, gives the definitions of a number of basic statistical terms taken mainly from International Standard ISO 3534-1ISO 3534-1 . When one of these metrological or statistical terms (or a closely related term) is first used in the text, starting with , it is printed in boldface and the number of the subclause in which it is defined is given in parentheses.

Because of its importance to this Guide, the definition of the general metrological term “uncertainty of measurement” is given both in and . The definitions of the most important terms specific to this Guide are given in to . In all of these subclauses and in and , the use of parentheses around certain words of some terms means that these words may be omitted if this is unlikely to cause confusion.VIM

clause.title: The term “uncertainty”
clause.p: The concept of uncertainty is discussed further in and .uncertainty of measurement

The word “uncertainty” means doubt, and thus in its broadest sense “uncertainty of measurement”uncertainty of measurement means doubt about the validity of the result of a measurement. Because of the lack of different words for this general concept of uncertainty and the specific quantities that provide quantitative measures of the concept, for example, the standard deviation, it is necessary to use the word “uncertainty” in these two different senses.

In this Guide, the word “uncertainty” without adjectives refers both to the general concept of uncertainty and to any or all quantitative measures of that concept. When a specific measure is intended, appropriate adjectives are used.uncertainty of measurement
The formal definition of the term “uncertainty of measurement”uncertainty of measurement developed for use in this Guide and in the VIMVIM (VIM:1993, definition 3.9) is as follows:

uncertainty (of measurement)
parameter, associated with the result of a measurement, that characterizes the dispersion of the values that could reasonably be attributed to the measurand
The parameter may be, for example, a standard deviation (or a given multiple of it), or the half-width of an interval having a stated level of confidencelevel of confidence.
Uncertainty of measurement comprises, in general, many components. Some of these components may be evaluated from the statistical distribution of the results of series of measurements and can be characterized by experimental standard deviations. The other components, which also can be characterized by standard deviations, are evaluated from assumed probability distributions based on experience or other information.
It is understood that the result of the measurement is the best estimate of the value of the measurand, and that all components of uncertainty, including those arising from systematic effects, such as components associated with corrections and reference standards, contribute to the dispersion.

The definition of uncertainty of measurementuncertainty of measurement given in is an operational one that focuses on the measurement result and its evaluated uncertainty. However, it is not inconsistent with other concepts of uncertainty of measurementuncertainty of measurement, such as

li.p: a measure of the possible error in the estimated value of the measurand as provided by the result of a measurement;
an estimate characterizing the range of values within which the true valuetrue value of a quantity of a measurand lies (VIM:1984, definition 3.09).VIM

clause.p: Although these two traditional concepts are valid as ideals, they focus on unknowable quantities: the “error” of the result of a measurement and the “true value” of the measurand (in contrast to its estimated value), respectively. Nevertheless, whichever concept of uncertainty is adopted, an uncertainty component is always evaluated using the same data and related information. (See also .)error of measurement

terms.title: Terms specific to this Guide
terms.p: For the purposes of this document, the following terms and definitions apply.
In general, terms that are specific to this Guide are defined in the text when first introduced. However, the definitions of the most important of these terms are given here for easy reference.

note.p: Further discussion related to these terms may be found as follows: for , see and ; for , see and ; for , see and and ; and for and , see .

verbal-definition.p: uncertainty of the result of a measurement expressed as a standard deviation

expression.name: Type A evaluation (of uncertainty)Type A evaluation of uncertainty

verbal-definition.p: method of evaluation of uncertainty by the statistical analysis of series of observations

expression.name: Type B evaluation (of uncertainty)Type B evaluation of uncertainty

verbal-definition.p: method of evaluation of uncertainty by means other than the statistical analysis of series of observations

standard uncertainty of the result of a measurement when that result is obtained from the values of a number of other quantities, equal to the positive square root of a sum of terms, the terms being the variances or covariances of these other quantities weighted according to how the measurement result varies with changes in these quantities
quantity defining an interval about the result of a measurement that may be expected to encompass a large fraction of the distribution of values that could reasonably be attributed to the measurand

termnote.p: The fraction may be viewed as the coverage probabilitycoverage probability or level of confidencelevel of confidence of the interval.

To associate a specific level of confidencelevel of confidence with the interval defined by the expanded uncertainty requires explicit or implicit assumptions regarding the probability distribution characterized by the measurement result and its combined standard uncertainty. The level of confidencelevel of confidence that may be attributed to this interval can be known only to the extent to which such assumptions may be justified.
Expanded uncertainty is termed overall uncertaintyuncertaintyoverall in paragraph 5 of Recommendation INC-1 (1980).

verbal-definition.p: numerical factor used as a multiplier of the combined standard uncertainty in order to obtain an expanded uncertainty

termnote.p: A coverage factor, k k, is typically in the range 2 to 3.

clause.title: Basic concepts
clause.p: Additional discussion of basic concepts may be found in , which focuses on the ideas of “true” value, error and uncertainty and includes graphical illustrations of these concepts; and in , which explores the motivation and statistical basis for Recommendation INC-1 (1980) upon which this Guide rests. is a glossary of the principal mathematical symbols used throughout the Guide.

The objective of a measurementmeasurement () is to determine the value () of the measurandmeasurand (), that is, the value of the particular quantityparticular quantity () to be measured. A measurement therefore begins with an appropriate specification of the measurand, the method of measurementmethod of measurement (), and the measurement proceduremeasurement procedure ().
The term “true value”true value of a quantity (see ) is not used in this Guide for the reasons given in ; the terms “value of a measurand” (or of a quantity) and “true value of a measurand”measurandvalue of the (or of a quantity) are viewed as equivalent.value of a quantity

In general, the result of a measurementresult of a measurement () is only an approximation or estimateestimate () of the value of the measurandmeasurandvalue of the and thus is complete only when accompanied by a statement of the uncertainty () of that estimate.

In practice, the required specification or definition of the measurandmeasurand is dictated by the required accuracy of measurementaccuracy of measurement (). The measurand should be defined with sufficient completeness withrespect to the required accuracy so that for all practical purposes associated with the measurement its value is unique. It is in this sense that the expression “value of the measurand”measurandvalue of the is used in this Guide.

example.p:
If the length of a nominally one-metre long steel bar is to be determined to micrometre accuracy, its specification should include the temperature and pressure at which the length is defined. Thus the measurand should be specified as, for example, the length of the bar at
25.00 ⁢
°C
25.00 "unitsml(degC)"
Footnote to the 2008 version:
According to Resolution 10 of the 22nd CGPM (2003) “…​ the symbol for the decimal marker shall be either the point on the line or the comma on the line…​”. The JCGM has decided to adopt, in its documents in English, the point on the line. However, in this document, the decimal comma has been retained for consistency with the 1995 printed version. and
101325 ⁢
Pa
101325 "unitsml(Pa)" (plus any other defining parameters deemed necessary, such as the way the bar is to be supported). However, if the length is to be determined to only millimetre accuracy, its specification would not require a defining temperature or pressure or a value for any other defining parameter.

note.p: Incomplete definition of the measurand can give rise to a component of uncertainty sufficiently large that it must be included in the evaluation of the uncertainty of the measurement result (see , , and ).uncertainty due to incomplete definition of the measurand

clause.p: In many cases, the result of a measurement is determined on the basis of series of observationsobservationsrepeated obtained under repeatability conditionsrepeatability conditions ().
Variations in repeated observationsobservationsrepeated are assumed to arise because influence quantitiesinfluence quantity () that can affect the measurement result are not held completely constant.

The mathematical model of the measurementmeasurementmathematical model of the that transforms the set of repeated observationsobservationsrepeated into the measurement result is of critical importance because, in addition to the observations, it generally includes various influence quantitiesinfluence quantity that are inexactly known. This lack of knowledge contributes to the uncertainty of the measurement result, as do the variations of the repeated observations and any uncertainty associated with the mathematical model itself.

This Guide treats the measurand as a scalar (a single quantity). Extension to a set of related measurands determined simultaneously in the same measurement requires replacing the scalar measurand and its variancevariance (,,) by a vector measurand and covariance matrixcovariance matrix (). Such a replacement is considered in this Guide only in the examples (see , , and ).correlated output estimates or quantities

clause.title: Errors, effects, and correctionscorrectionerror of measurement

clause.p:
In general, a measurement has imperfections that give rise to an error () in the measurement result. Traditionally, an error is viewed as having two components, namely, a randomrandom error () component and a systematicsystematic error () component.
Error is an idealized concept and errors cannot be known exactly.error of measurement

Random error presumably arises from unpredictable or stochastic temporal and spatial variations of influence quantities. The effects of such variations, hereafter termed random effectrandom effects, give rise to variations in repeated observationsobservationsrepeated of the measurand. Although it is not possible to compensate for the random errorrandom error of a measurement result, it can usually be reduced by increasing the number of observations; its expectation or expected valueexpectation (or expected value) (, ) is zero.
The experimental standard deviation of the arithmetic mean or average of a series of observations (see ) is not the random error of the mean, although it is so designated in some publications. It is instead a measure of the uncertainty of the mean due to random effects. The exact value of the error in the mean arising from these effects cannotbe known.
In this Guide, great care is taken to distinguish between the terms “error” and “uncertainty”error and uncertaintyconfusion between. They are not synonyms, but represent completely different concepts; they should not be confused with one another or misused.error of measurement

Systematic error, like random errorrandom error, cannot be eliminated but it too can often be reduced. If a systematic errorsystematic error arises from a recognized effect of an influence quantityinfluence quantity on a measurement result, hereafter termed a systematic effectsystematic effect, the effect can be quantified and, if it is significant in size relative to the required accuracy of the measurement, a correctioncorrection () or correction factorcorrection factor () can be applied to compensate for the effect. It is assumed that, after correction, the expectation or expected valueexpectation (or expected value) of the error arising from a systematic effect is zero.
The uncertainty of a correction applied to a measurement result to compensate for a systematic effect is not the systematic error, often termed biasbias, in the measurement result due to the effect as it is sometimes called. It is instead a measure of the uncertainty of the result due to incomplete knowledge of the required value of the correction. The error arising from imperfect compensation of a systematic effect cannot be exactly known. The terms “error” and “uncertainty”error and uncertaintyconfusion between should be used properly and care taken to distinguish between them.error of measurementuncertainty of a correction
It is assumed that the result of a measurement has been corrected for all recognized significant systematic effectsystematic effects and that every effort has been made to identify such effects.

example.p: A correction due to the finite impedance of a voltmeter used to determine the potential difference (the measurand) across a high-impedance resistor is applied to reduce the systematic effect on the result of the measurement arising from the loading effect of the voltmeter. However, the values of the impedances of the voltmeter and resistor, which are used to estimate the value of the correction and which are obtained from other measurements, are themselves uncertain. These uncertainties are used to evaluate the component of the uncertainty of the potential difference determination arising from the correction and thus from the systematic effect due to the finite impedance of the voltmeter.

note.p: Often, measuring instruments and systems are adjusted or calibrated using measurement standards and reference materials to eliminate systematic effects; however, the uncertainties associated with these standards and materials must still be taken into account.
The case where a correctioncorrection for a known significant systematic effect is not applied is discussed in the and in .correctionignoring a

clause.p:
The uncertainty of the result of a measurement reflects the lack of exact knowledge of the value of the measurand (see ). The result of a measurement after correction for recognized systematic effectsystematic effects is still only an estimate of the value of the measurand because of the uncertainty arising from random effectrandom effects and from imperfect correction of the result for systematic effects.uncertainty of a correctionuncertainty of measurement
The result of a measurement (after correction) can unknowably be very close to the value of the measurand (and hence have a negligible error) even though it may have a large uncertainty. Thus the uncertainty of the result of a measurement should not be confused with the remaining unknown error.error of measurement
In practice, there are many possible sources of uncertainty in a measurement, including:error of measurementuncertainty of measurementuncertaintysources of

li.p: incomplete definition of the measurand;
imperfect reaIization of the definition of the measurand;
nonrepresentative sampling — the sample measured may not represent the defined measurand;
inadequate knowledge of the effects of environmental conditions on the measurement or imperfect measurement of environmental conditions;
personal bias in reading analogue instruments;
finite instrument resolution or discrimination threshold;
inexact values of measurement standards and reference materials;
inexact values of constants and other parameters obtained from external sources and used in the data-reduction algorithm;
approximations and assumptions incorporated in the measurement method and procedure;
variations in repeated observations of the measurand under apparently identical conditions.

clause.p: These sources are not necessarily independent, and some of sources to may contribute to source . Of course, an unrecognized systematic effectsystematic effect cannot be taken into account in the evaluation of the uncertainty of the result of a measurement but contributes to its error.

Recommendation INC-1 (1980) of the Working Group on the Statement of UncertaintiesWorking Group on the Statement of Uncertainties groups uncertainty components into two categories based on their method of evaluation, “A” and “B” (see ,, and ). These categories apply to uncertainty and are not substitutes for the words “random”random and “systematic”.systematic The uncertainty of a correction for a known systematic effectsystematic effect may in some cases be obtained by a Type A evaluationType A evaluation of uncertainty while in other cases by a Type B evaluationType B evaluation of uncertainty, as may the uncertainty characterizing a random effectrandom effect.uncertaintycategorizing or classifying components ofuncertainty of a correction
In some publications, uncertainty components are categorized as “random” and “systematic” and are associated with errors arising from random effects and known systematic effects, respectively. Such categorization of components of uncertainty can be ambiguous when generally applied. For example, a “random” component of uncertainty in one measurement may become a “systematic” component of uncertainty in another measurement in which the result of the first measurement is used as an input datum. Categorizing the methods of evaluating uncertainty components rather than the components themselves avoids such ambiguity. At the same time, it does not preclude collecting individual components that have been evaluated by the two different methods into designated groups to be used for a particular purpose (see ).uncertaintygrouping components of

The purpose of the Type AType A evaluation of uncertainty and Type BType B evaluation of uncertainty classification is to indicate the two different ways of evaluating uncertainty components and is for convenience of discussion only; the classification is not meant to indicate that there is any difference in the nature of the components resulting from the two types of evaluation. Both types of evaluation are based on probability distributionprobability distributions (), and the uncertainty components resulting from either type are quantified by variances or standard deviations.uncertaintycategorizing or classifying components of

The estimated variance u 2 u^2 characterizing an uncertainty component obtained from a Type A evaluationType A evaluation of uncertainty is calculated from series of repeated observationsobservationsrepeated and is the familiar statistically estimated variance s 2 s^2 (see ). The estimated standard deviationstandard deviation (,,) u u, the positive square root of u 2 u^2, is thus u = s u = s and for convenience is sometimes called a Type A standard uncertaintystandard uncertaintyType A standard uncertainty. For an uncertainty component obtained from a Type B evaluationType B evaluation of uncertainty, the estimated variance u 2 u^2 is evaluated using available knowledge (see ), and the estimated standard deviation u is sometimes called a Type B standard uncertaintyType B standard uncertainty.

Thus a Type A standard uncertainty is obtained from a probability density functionprobability density function () derived from an observed frequency distributionfrequency distribution (), while a Type B standard uncertainty is obtained from an assumed probability density function based on the degree of beliefdegree of belief that an event will occur [often called subjective probabilityprobabilityprobabilitysubjective ()].Both approaches employ recognized interpretations of probability.
A Type B evaluation of an uncertainty component is usually based on a pool of comparatively reliable information (see ).informationpool offor a Type B evaluation

The standard uncertaintystandard uncertainty of the result of a measurement, when that result is obtained from the values of a number of other quantities, is termed combined standard uncertaintycombined standard uncertainty and denoted by u c u_{"c"}. It is the estimated standard deviation associated with the result and is equal to the positive square root of the combined variancevariancecombined obtained from all variance and covariancecovariance () components, however evaluated, using what is termed in this Guide the law of propagation of uncertainty (see ).uncertaintylaw of propagation of

To meet the needs of some industrial and commercial applications, as well as requirements in the areas of health and safety, an expanded uncertaintyexpanded uncertainty
U
ii(U) is obtained by multiplying the combined standard uncertainty
u c
u_{"c"} by a coverage factorcoverage factor
k
k. The intended purpose of
U
ii(U) is to provide an interval about the result of a measurement that may be expected to encompass a large fraction of the distribution of values that could reasonably be attributed to the measurand. The choice of the factor
k
k, which is usually in the range 2 to 3, is based on the coverage probabilitycoverage probability or level of confidencelevel of confidence required of the interval (see ).
The coverage factor k k is always to be stated, so that the standard uncertainty of the measured quantity can be recovered for use in calculating the combined standard uncertainty of other measurement results that may depend on that quantity.

If all of the quantities on which the result of a measurement depends are varied, its uncertainty can be evaluated by statistical means. However, because this is rarely possible in practice due to limited time and resources, the uncertainty of a measurement result is usually evaluated using a mathematical model of the measurementmeasurementmathematical model of the and the law of propagation of uncertainty. Thus implicit in this Guide is the assumption that a measurement can be modelled mathematically to the degree imposed by the required accuracy of the measurementaccuracy of measurement.uncertaintylaw of propagation ofuncertaintystatistical evaluation ofby varying input quantities

Because the mathematical modelmeasurementmathematical model of the may be incomplete, all relevant quantities should be varied to the fullest practicable extent so that the evaluation of uncertainty can be based as much as possible on observed data. Whenever feasible, the use of empirical models of the measurement founded on long-term quantitative data, and the use of check standards and control charts that can indicate if a measurement is under statistical controlstatistical control, should be part of the effort to obtain reliable evaluations of uncertainty. The mathematical model should always be revised when the observed data, including the result of independent determinations of the same measurand, demonstrate that the model is incomplete. A well-designed experiment can greatly facilitate reliable evaluations of uncertainty and is an important part of the art of measurement.uncertaintystatistical evaluation ofby varying input quantities

In order to decide if a measurement system is functioning properly, the experimentally observed variability of its output values, as measured by their observed standard deviation, is often compared with the predicted standard deviation obtained by combining the various uncertainty components that characterize the measurement. In such cases, only those components (whether obtained from Type A or Type B evaluations) that could contribute to the experimentally observed variability of these output values should be considered.uncertaintygrouping components of
Such an analysis may be facilitated by gathering those components that contribute to the variability and those that do not into two separate and appropriately labelled groups.

In some cases, the uncertainty of a correction for a systematic effect need not be included in the evaluation of the uncertainty of a measurement result. Although the uncertainty has been evaluated, it may be ignored if its contribution to the combined standard uncertainty of the measurement result is insignificant. If the value of the correction itself is insignificant relative to the combined standard uncertainty, it too may be ignored.uncertainty ignoring a component ofuncertainty when a correction is not applied

It often occurs in practice, especially in the domain of legal metrology, that a device is tested through a comparison with a measurement standard and the uncertainties associated with the standard and the comparison procedure are negligible relative to the required accuracy of the test. An example is the use of a set of well-calibrated standards of mass to test the accuracy of a commercial scale. In such cases, because the components of uncertainty are small enough to be ignored, the measurement may be viewed as determining the errorerrordetermining of the device under test. (See also .)

The estimate of the value of a measurand provided by the result of a measurement is sometimes expressed in terms of the adopted value of a measurement standard rather than in terms of the relevant unit of the International System of Units (SIInternational System of Units (SI). In such cases, the magnitude of the uncertainty ascribable to the measurement result may be significantly smaller than when that result is expressed in the relevant SI unit. (In effect, the measurand has been redefined to be the ratio of the value of the quantity to be measured to the adopted value of the standard.)unituse of an adopted value of a measurement standard as a

example.p: A high-quality Zener voltage standard is calibrated by comparison with a Josephson effect voltage reference based on the conventional value of the Josephson constant recommended for international use by the CIPM. The relative combined standard uncertainty u c ( V S ) / V S u_{"c"}(ii(V)_{"S"})//ii(V)_{"S"} (see ) of the calibrated potential difference V S ii(V)_{"S"} of the Zener standard is 2 × 10 − 8 2 xx 10^{-8} when V S ii(V)_{"S"} is reported in terms of the conventional value, but u c ( V S ) / V S u_{"c"}(ii(V)_{"S"})//ii(V)_{"S"} is 4 × 10 − 7 4 xx 10^{-7} when V S ii(V)_{"S"} is reported in terms of the SI unit of potential difference, the volt (V), because of the additional uncertainty associated with the SI value of the Josephson constant.

clause.p: Blunders in recording or analysing data can introduce a significant unknown error in the result of ameasurement. Large blundersblunders can usually be identified by a proper review of the data; small ones could be masked by, or even appear as, random variations. Measures of uncertainty are not intended to account for such mistakes.

Although this Guide provides a framework for assessing uncertainty, it cannot substitute for critical thinking, intellectual honesty and professional skill. The evaluation of uncertainty is neither a routine task nor a purely mathematical one; it depends on detailed knowledge of the nature of the measurand and of the measurement. The quality and utility of the uncertainty quoted for the result of a measurement therefore ultimately depend on the understanding, critical analysis, and integrity of those who contribute to the assignment of its value.uncertaintyquality and utility of the quoted

clause.title: Evaluating standard uncertainty
clause.p: Additional guidance on evaluating uncertainty components, mainly of a practical nature, may be found in .

clause.title: Modelling the measurementmeasurementmathematical model of the

clause.p: In most cases, a measurand Y ii(Y) is not measured directly, but is determined from N ii(N) other quantities X 1 , X 2 , … , X N ii(X)_1, ii(X)_2,..., ii(X)_N through a functional relationshipfunctional relationship f f:

note.p: For economy of notation, in this Guide the same symbol is used for the physical quantity (the measurand) and for the random variablerandom variable (see ) that represents the possible outcome of an observation of that quantity. When it is stated that X i ii(X)_i has a particular probability distributionprobability distribution, the symbol is used in the latter sense; it is assumed that the physical quantity itself can be characterized by an essentially unique value (see and ).

In a series of observations, the k kth observed value of X i ii(X)_i is denoted by X i , k ii(X)_{i,k}; hence if R ii(R) denotes the resistance of a resistor, the k kth observed value of the resistance is denoted by R k ii(R)_k.
The estimate of X i ii(X)_i (strictly speaking, of its expectation)expectation (or expected value) is denoted by x i x_i.

example.p: If a potential difference V ii(V) is applied to the terminals of a temperature-dependent resistor that has a resistance R 0 ii(R)_0 at the defined temperature t 0 t_0 and a linear temperature coefficient of resistance α ii(alpha), the power P P (the measurand) dissipated by the resistor at the temperature t t depends on V ii(V), R 0 ii(R)_0, α ii(alpha), and t t according to

note.p: Other methods of measuring P ii(P) would be modelled by different mathematical expressions.measurementmathematical model of the

clause.p: The input quantities X 1 , X 2 , … , X N ii(X)_1,ii(X)_2,...,ii(X)_N upon which the output quantityoutput quantity Y ii(Y) depends may themselves beviewed as measurands and may themselves depend on other quantities, including corrections and correction factors for systematic effects, thereby leading to a complicated functional relationshipfunctional relationship f f that may never be written down explicitly. Further, f f may be determined experimentally (see ) or exist only as an algorithm that must be evaluated numerically. The function f f as it appears in this Guide is to be interpreted in this broader context, in particular as that function which contains every quantity, including all corrections and correction factors, that can contribute a significant component of uncertainty to the measurement result.measurementmathematical model of the

Thus, if data indicate that
f
f does not model the measurement to the degree imposed by the required accuracy of the measurement result, additional input quantities must be included in
f
f to eliminate the inadequacy (see ). This may require introducing an input quantityinput quantity to reflect incomplete knowledge of a phenomenon thataffects the measurand. In the example of , additional input quantities might be needed to account for a known nonuniform temperature distribution across the resistor, a possible nonlinear temperature coefficient of resistance, or a possible dependence of resistance on barometric pressure.
Nonetheless, may be as elementary as Y = X 1 − X 2 ii(Y) = ii(X)_1 - ii(X)_2. This expression models, for example, the comparison of two determinations of the same quantity X ii(X).
The set of input quantitiesinput quantitiescategorization of X 1 , X 2 , … , X N ii(X)_1, ii(X)_2,..., ii(X)_N may be categorized as:

li.p: quantities whose values and uncertainties are directly determined in the current measurement. These values and uncertainties may be obtained from, for example, a single observation, repeated observations, or judgement based on experience, and may involve the determination of corrections to instrument readings and corrections for influence quantities, such as ambient temperature, barometric pressure, and humidity;

quantities whose values and uncertainties are brought into the measurement from external sources, such as quantities associated with calibrated measurement standards, certified reference materials, and reference data obtained from handbooks.

clause.p: An estimate of the measurand Y ii(Y), denoted by y y, is obtained from using input estimateinput estimates x 1 , x 2 , … , x N x1,x_2,..., x_N for the values of the N ii(N) quantities X 1 , X 2 , … , X N ii(X)_1, ii(X)_2,..., ii(X)_N. Thus the output estimateoutput estimate y y, which is the result of the measurement, is given by

note.p: In some cases, the estimate y y may be obtained from

stem.asciimath: y = bar(ii(Y)) = 1/n sum_{k=1}^n ii(Y)_k = 1/n sum_{k=1}^n f(ii(X)_{1,k}, ii(X)_{2,k},..., ii(X)_{N,k})

note.p: That is, y y is taken as the arithmetic meanarithmetic mean or average (see ) of n n independent determinations Y k ii(Y)_k of Y ii(Y), each determination having the same uncertainty and each being based on a complet e set of observed values of the N ii(N) input quantities X i ii(X)_i obtained at the same time. This way of averaging, rather than y = f ( X ¯ 1 , X ¯ 2 , … , X ¯ N ) y = f(bar(ii(X))_1, bar(ii(X))_2,..., bar(ii(X))_N), wherefunctional relationshipnonlinear

is the arithmetic mean of the individual observations X i , k ii(X)_{i,k}, may be preferable when f f is a nonlinear function of the input quantities X 1 , X 2 , … , X N ii(X)_1, ii(X)_2,..., ii(X)_N, but the two approaches are identical if f f is a linear function of the X i ii(X)_i (see and ).

clause.p: The estimated standard deviation associated with the output estimateoutput estimate or measurement result y y, termed combined standard uncertaintystandard uncertainty and denoted by u c ( y ) u_{"c"}(y), is determined from the estimated standard deviation associated with each input estimate x i x_i, termed standard uncertaintystandard uncertainty and denoted by u ( x i ) u(x_i) (see and ).

Each input estimateinput estimate x i x_i and its associated standard uncertaintystandard uncertainty u ( x i ) u(x_i) are obtained from a distribution ofpossible values of the input quantity X i ii(X)_i. This probability distributionprobability distribution may be frequency based, that is, based on a series of observations X i , k ii(X)_{i,k} of X i ii(X)_i, or it may be an a priori distribution. Type A evaluations of standard uncertaintyType A evaluation of uncertainty components are founded on frequency distributionfrequency distributions while Type B evaluationsType B evaluation of uncertainty are founded on a priori distributionsdistributiona priori. It must be recognized that in both cases the distributions are models that are used torepresent the state of our knowledge.

clause.title: Type A evaluation of standard uncertaintyType A evaluation of uncertainty

clause.p: In most cases, the best available estimate of the expectation or expected valueexpectation (or expected value) μ q ii(mu)_q of a quantity q q that varies randomly [a random variablerandom variable ()], and for which n n independent observations q k q_k have been obtained under the same conditions of measurement (see ), is the arithmetic meanarithmetic mean or average q ¯ bar(q) () of the n n observations:

Thus, for an input quantity X i ii(X)_i estimated from n n independent repeated observationsobservationsrepeated X i , k ii(X)_{i,k}, the arithmetic mean X ¯ i bar(ii(X))_i obtained from isused as the input estimateinput estimate x i x_i in to determine the measurement result y y; that is, x i = X ¯ i x_i = bar(ii(X))_i. Those input estimates not evaluated from repeated observations must be obtained by other methods, such as those indicated in the second category of .Type A evaluation of uncertainty

The individual observations q k q_k differ in value because of random variations in the influence quantitiesinfluence quantity, or random effectrandom effects (see ). The experimental variance of the observationsstandard deviationexperimental, which estimates the variance of the observations, which estimates the variance σ 2 ii(sigma)^2 of the probability distribution of q q, is given by

This estimate of variancevariance and its positive square root s ( q k ) s(q_k), termed the experimental standard deviation (), characterize the variability of the observed values q k q_k, or more specifically, their dispersion about their mean q ¯ bar(q).Type A evaluation of uncertaintyvarianceexperimental (or estimate of)
The best estimate of σ 2 ( q ¯ ) = σ 2 / n ii(sigma)^2 (bar(q)) = ii(sigma)^2//n, the variancevariance of the mean, is given by

The experimental variance of the mean s 2 ( q ¯ ) s^2(bar(q)) and the experimental standard deviation of the mean s ( q ¯ ) s(bar(q)) (), equal to the positive square root of s 2 ( q ¯ ) s^2(bar(q)), quantify how well q ¯ bar(q) estimates the expectation μ q ii(mu)_q of q q, and either may be used as a measure of the uncertainty of q ¯ bar(q).standard deviation of the meanexperimentalType A evaluation of uncertaintyType A standard uncertaintyvariance of the meanvariance of the meanexperimental

u(x_i) = s(bar(ii(X))_i) are sometimes called a Type A varianceType A variance and a Type A standard uncertainty, respectively.
The number of observations n n should be large enough to ensure that q ¯ bar(q) provides a reliable estimate of the expectation μ q ii(mu)_q of the random variablerandom variable q q and that s 2 ( q ¯ ) s^2(bar(q)) provides a reliable estimate of the variance σ 2 ( q ¯ ) = σ 2 / n ii(sigma)^2(bar(q)) = ii(sigma)^2//n (see ). The difference between s 2 ( q ¯ ) s^2(bar(q)) and σ 2 ( q ¯ ) ii(sigma)^2(bar(q)) must be considered when one constructs confidence intervalconfidence intervals (see ). In this case, if the probability distributionprobability distribution of q q is a normal distributionnormal distribution (see ), the difference is taken into account through the t t-distribution t t-distribution (see ).
Although the variance s 2 ( q ¯ ) s^2(bar(q)) is the more fundamental quantity, the standard deviation s ( q ¯ ) s(bar(q)) is more convenient in practice because it has the same dimension as q q and a more easily comprehended value than that of the variance.

For a well-characterized measurement under statistical controlstatistical control, a combined or pooled estimate of variance s p 2 s_p^2 (or a pooled experimental standard deviation s p s_p) that characterizes the measurement may be available. In such cases, when the value of a measurand q q is determined from n n independent observations, the experimental variance of the arithmetic mean q ¯ bar(q) of the observations is estimated better by s p 2 / n s_p^2//n than by s 2 ( q k ) / n s^2(q_k)//n and the standard uncertainty is u = s p / n u = s_{"p"}//sqrt(n). (See also the .)Type A evaluation of uncertaintyvariancepooled estimate of (or pooled experimental standard deviation)

Often an estimate x i x_i of an input quantity X i ii(X)_i is obtained from a curve that has been fitted to experimental data by the method of least squaresleast squaresmethod of. The estimated variances and resulting standard uncertainties of the fitted parameters characterizing the curve and of any predicted points can usually be calculated by well-known statistical procedures (see and Reference ).Type A evaluation of uncertainty

The degrees of freedomdegrees of freedom () ν i ii(nu)_i of u ( x i ) u(x_i) (see ), equal to n − 1 n - 1 in the simple case where x i = X ¯ i x_i = bar(ii(X))_i and u ( x i ) = s ( X ¯ i ) u(x_i) = s(bar(ii(X))_i) are calculated from n n independent observations as in and , should always be given when Type A evaluations of uncertainty components are documented.Type A evaluation of uncertainty

If the random variationscorrelated random variations in the observations of an input quantity are correlated, for example, in time, the mean and experimental standard deviation of the mean as given in and may be inappropriate estimatorestimators () of the desired statisticstatistics (). In such cases, the observations should be analysed bystatistical methods specially designed to treat a series of correlated, randomly-varying measurements.Type A evaluation of uncertainty
Such specialized methods are used to treat measurements of frequency standards. However, it is possible that as one goes from short-term measurements to long-term measurements of other metrological quantities, the assumption of uncorrelated random variations may no longer be valid and the specialized methods could be used to treat these measurements as well. (See Reference , for example, for a detailed discussion of the Allan variancevarianceAllan.)

The discussion of Type A evaluation of standard uncertainty in to is not meant to be exhaustive; there are many situations, some rather complex, that can be treated by statistical methods. An important example is the use of calibration designs, often based on the method of least squares, to evaluate the uncertainties arising from both short- and long-term random variations in the results of comparisons of material artefacts of unknown values, such as gauge blocks and standards of mass, with reference standards of known values. In such comparatively simple measurement situations, components of uncertainty can frequently be evaluated by the statistical analysis of data obtained from designs consisting of nested sequences of measurements of the measurand for a number of different values of the quantities upon which it depends — a so-called analysis of variance (see ).ANOVAType A evaluation of uncertaintyuncertaintystatistical evaluation ofby varying input quantities
At lower levels of the calibration chaincalibration chain, where reference standards are often assumed to be exactly known because they have been calibrated by a national or primary standards laboratory, the uncertainty of a calibration result may be a single Type A standard uncertainty evaluated from the pooled experimental standard deviation that characterizes the measurement.unituse of an adopted value of a measurement standard as avariancepooled estimate of (or pooled experimental standard deviation)

clause.title: Type B evaluation of standard uncertaintyType B evaluation of uncertainty

clause.p: For an estimate x i x_i of an input quantity X i ii(X)_i that has not been obtained from repeated observationsobservationsrepeated, the associated estimated variancevariance u 2 ( x i ) u^2(x_i) or the standard uncertainty u ( x i ) u(x_i) is evaluated by scientific judgement based on all of the available information on the possible variability of X i ii(X)_i. The pool of informationinformationpool offor a Type B evaluationType B evaluation of uncertaintyType B standard uncertainty may include

li.p: previous measurement data;
experience with or general knowledge of the behaviour and properties of relevant materials and instruments; manufacturer’s specifications;
data provided in calibration and other certificates;
uncertainties assigned to reference data taken from handbooks.

clause.p:
For convenience,

When x i x_i is obtained from an a priori distributiondistributiona priori, the associated variance is appropriately written as u 2 ( X i ) u^2(ii(X)_i), but for simplicity, u 2 ( x i ) u^2(x_i) and u ( x ) u(x) are used throughout this Guide.

The proper use of the poolinformationpool offor a Type B evaluation of available information for a Type B evaluationType B evaluation of uncertainty of standard uncertainty calls for insight based on experience and general knowledge, and is a skill that can be learned with practice. It should be recognized that a Type B evaluation of standard uncertainty can be as reliable as a Type A evaluation, especially in a measurement situation where a Type A evaluation is based on a comparatively small number of statistically independent observations.normal distributionType A evaluation of uncertainty
If the probability distribution of q q in is normal, then σ [ s ( q ¯ ) ] / σ ( q ¯ ) ii(sigma)[s(bar(q))]//ii(sigma)(bar(q)), the standard deviation of s ( q ¯ ) s(bar(q)) relative to σ ( q ¯ ) ii(sigma)(bar(q)), is approximately [ 2 ( n − 1 ) ] − 1 / 2 [2(n - 1)]^{-1//2}. Thus, taking σ [ s ( q ¯ ) ] ii(sigma)[s(bar(q))] as the uncertainty of s ( q ¯ ) s(bar(q)), for n = 10 n = 10 observations, the relative uncertainty in s ( q ¯ ) s(bar(q)) is 24 percent, while for n = 50 n = 50 observations it is 10 percent. (Additional values are given in in .)uncertainty due to limited samplinguncertainty of the experimental standard deviation of the mean

If the estimate x i x_i is taken from a manufacturer’s specification, calibration certificate, handbook, orother source and its quoted uncertainty is stated to be a particular multiple of a standard deviation, the standard uncertainty u ( x i ) u(x_i) is simply the quoted value divided by the multiplier, and the estimated variance u 2 ( x i ) u^2(x_i) is the square of that quotient.Type B evaluation of uncertainty

example.p: A calibration certificate states that the mass of a stainless steel mass standard m S m_{"S"} of nominal value one kilogram is 1000.000325 ⁢ g 1000.000325 "unitsml(g)" and that “the uncertainty of this value is 240 ⁢ µg 240 "unitsml(ug)" at the three standard deviation level”. The standard uncertainty of the mass standard is then simply u ( m S ) = ( 240 ⁢ µg ) / 3 = 80 ⁢ µg u(m_{"S"}) = (240 "unitsml(ug)")//3 = 80 "unitsml(ug)". This corresponds to a relative standard uncertainty u ( m S ) / m S u(m_{"S"})//m_{"S"} of 80 × 10 − 9 80 xx 10^{-9} (see ). The estimated variance is u 2 ( m S ) = ( 80 ⁢ µg ) 2 = 6.4 × 10 − 9 ⁢ g 2 u^2(m_{"S"}) = (80 "unitsml(ug)")^2 = 6.4 xx 10^{-9} "unitsml(g^2)".

note.p: In many cases, little or no information is provided about the individual components from which the quoted uncertainty has been obtained. This is generally unimportant for expressing uncertainty according to the practices of this Guide since all standard uncertainties are treated in the same way when the combined standard uncertainty of ameasurement result is calculated (see ).

clause.p:
The quoted uncertainty of
x i
x_i is not necessarily given as a multiple of a standard deviation as in . Instead, one may find it stated that the quoted uncertainty defines an interval having a 90, 95, or 99 percent level of confidencelevel of confidence (see ). Unless otherwise indicated, one may assume that a normal distributionnormal distribution () was used to calculate the quoted uncertainty, and recover the standard uncertainty of
x i
x_i by dividing the quoted uncertainty by the appropriate factor for the normal distribution. The factors corresponding to the above three levels of confidence are 1.641.64; 1.961.96; and 2.582.58 (see also in ).Type B evaluation of uncertainty
There would be no need for such an assumption if the uncertainty had been given in accordance with the recommendations of this Guide regarding the reporting of uncertainty, which stress that the coverage factorcoverage factor used is always to be given (see ).

example.p: A calibration certificate states that the resistance of a standard resistor R S ii(R)_{"S"} of nominal value ten ohms is 10.000742 ⁢ Ω ± 129 ⁢ µΩ 10.000742 "unitsml(Ohm)" pm 129 "unitsml(uOhm)" at 23 ⁢ °C 23 "unitsml(degC)" and that “the quoted uncertainty of 129 ⁢ µΩ 129 "unitsml(uOhm)" defines an interval having a level of confidence of 99 percent”. The standard uncertainty of the resistor may be taken as u ( R S ) = ( 129 ⁢ µΩ ) / 2.58 = 50 ⁢ µΩ u(ii(R)_{"S"}) = (129 "unitsml(uOhm)")//2.58 = 50 "unitsml(uOhm)", which corresponds to a relative standard uncertainty u ( R S ) / R S u(ii(R)_{"S"})//ii(R)_{"S"} of 5.0 × 10 − 6 5.0 xx 10^{-6} (see ). The estimated variance is u 2 ( R S ) = ( 50 ⁢ µΩ ) 2 = 2.5 × 10 − 9 ⁢ Ω 2 u^2(ii(R)_{"S"}) = (50 "unitsml(uOhm)")^2 = 2.5 xx 10^{-9} "unitsml(Ohm^2)".

clause.p: Consider the case where, based on the available information, one can state that “there is a fifty-fifty chance that the value of the input quantity X i ii(X)_i lies in the interval a − a_"−" to a + a_+” (in other words, the probability that X i ii(X)_i lies within this interval is 0.50.5 or 50 percent). If it can be assumed that the distribution of possible values of X i ii(X)_i is approximately normal, then the best estimate x i x_i of X i ii(X)_i can be taken to be the midpoint of the interval. Further, if the half-width of the interval is denoted by a = ( a + − a − ) / 2 a = (a_+ - a_"−")//2, one can take u ( x i ) = 1.48 a u(x_i) = 1.48 a, because for a normal distributionnormal distribution with expectation μ ii(mu) and standard deviation σ ii(sigma) the interval μ ± σ / 1.48 ii(mu) pm ii(sigma)//1.48 encompasses approximately 50 percent of the distribution.Type B evaluation of uncertainty

example.p: A machinist determining the dimensions of a part estimates that its length lies, with probability 0.50.5, in the interval 10.07 ⁢ mm 10.07 "unitsml(mm)" to 10.15 ⁢ mm 10.15 "unitsml(mm)", and reports that l = ( 10.11 ± 0.04 ) ⁢ mm l = (10.11 pm 0.04) "unitsml(mm)", meaning that ± 0.04 ⁢ mm pm 0.04 "unitsml(mm)" defines an interval having a level of confidence of 50 percent. Then a = 0.04 ⁢ mm a = 0.04 "unitsml(mm)", and if one assumes a normal distribution for the possible values of l l, the standard uncertainty of the length is u ( l ) = 1.48 × 0.04 ⁢ mm ≈ 0.06 ⁢ mm u(l) = 1.48 xx 0.04 "unitsml(mm)" ~~ 0.06 "unitsml(mm)" and the estimated variance is u 2 ( l ) = ( 1.48 × 0.04 ⁢ mm ) 2 = 3.5 × 10 − 3 ⁢ mm 2 u^2(l) = (1.48 xx 0.04 "unitsml(mm)")^2 = 3.5 xx 10^{-3} "unitsml(mm^2)".
