It would give the value of u ( x i ) u(x_i) considerably more significance than is obviously warranted if one were to use the actual normal deviate 0.967420.96742 corresponding to probability p = 2 / 3 p = 2//3, that is, if one were to write u ( x i ) = a / 0.96742 = 1.033 a u(x_i) = a//0.96742 = 1.033a.

In other cases, it may be possible to estimate only bounds (upper and lower limits) for X i ii(X)_i, in particular, to state that “the probabilityprobability that the value of X i ii(X)_i lies within the interval a − a_"−" to a + a_+ for all practical purposes is equal to one and the probability that X i ii(X)_i lies outside this interval is essentially zero”. If there is no specific knowledge about the possible values of X i ii(X)_i within the interval, one can only assume that it is equally probablefor X i ii(X)_i to lie anywhere within it (a uniform or rectangular distribution of possible values — see and ). Then x i x_i, the expectation or expected valueexpectation (or expected value) of X i ii(X)_i, is the midpoint of the interval, x i = ( a − + a + ) / 2 x_i = (a_"−" + a_+)//2, with associated varianceType B evaluation of uncertainty
If the difference between the boundsbounds on an input quantity, a + − a − a_+ - a_"−", is denoted by 2 a 2a, then becomes

note.p: When a component of uncertainty determined in this manner contributes significantly to the uncertainty of a measurement result, it is prudent to obtain additional data for its further evaluation.

example.p: A handbook gives the value of the coefficient of linear thermal expansion of pure copper at 20 ⁢ °C 20 "unitsml(degC)", α 20 ( Cu ) ii(alpha)_{20}("Cu"), as 16.52 × 10 − 6 ⁢ °C − 1 16.52 xx 10^{-6} "unitsml(degC^(-1))" and simply states that “the error in this value should not exceed 0.40 × 10 − 6 ⁢ °C − 1 0.40 xx 10^{-6} "unitsml(degC^(-1))"“. Based on this limited information, it is not unreasonable to assume that the value of α 20 ( Cu ) ii(alpha)_{20}("Cu") lies with equal probability in the interval 16.12 × 10 − 6 ⁢ °C − 1 16.12 xx 10^{-6} "unitsml(degC^(-1))" to 16.92 × 10 − 6 ⁢ °C − 1 16.92 xx 10^{-6} "unitsml(degC^(-1))", and that it is very unlikely that α 20 ( Cu ) ii(alpha)_{20}("Cu") lies outside this interval. The variance of this symmetric rectangular distributiondistributionrectangular of possible values of α 20 ( Cu ) ii(alpha)_{20}("Cu") of half-width a = 0.40 × 10 − 6 ⁢ °C − 1 a = 0.40 xx 10^{-6} "unitsml(degC^(-1))" is then, from , u 2 ( α 20 ) = ( 0.40 × 10 − 6 ⁢ °C − 1 ) 2 / 3 = 53.3 × 10 − 15 ⁢ °C − 2 u^2(ii(alpha)_{20}) = (0.40 xx 10^{-6} "unitsml(degC^(-1))")^2//3 = 53.3 xx 10^{-15} "unitsml(degC^(-2))", and the standard uncertainty is u ( α 20 ) = ( 0.40 × 10 − 6 ⁢ °C − 1 ) / 3 = 0.23 × 10 − 6 ⁢ °C − 1 u(ii(alpha)_{20})=(0.40 xx 10^{-6} "unitsml(degC^(-1))")//sqrt(3) = 0.23 xx 10^{-6} "unitsml(degC^(-1))".

A manufacturer’s specifications for a digital voltmeter state that “between one and two years after the instrument is calibrated, its accuracy on the 1 ⁢ V 1 "unitsml(V)" range is 14 × 10 − 6 14 xx 10^{-6} times the reading plus 2 × 10 − 6 2 xx 10^{-6} times the range”. Consider that the instrument is used 20 months after calibration to measure on its 1 ⁢ V 1 "unitsml(V)" range a potential difference V ii(V), and the arithmetic mean of a number of independent repeated observations of V ii(V) is found to be V ¯ = 0.928571 ⁢ V bar(ii(V)) = 0.928571 "unitsml(V)" with a Type A standard uncertainty u ( V ¯ ) = 12 ⁢ µV u(bar(ii(V))) = 12 "unitsml(uV)". One can obtain the standard uncertainty associated with the manufacturer’s specifications from a Type B evaluation by assuming that the stated accuracy provides symmetric bounds to an additive correction to V ¯ bar(ii(V)), Δ V ¯ Delta bar(ii(V)), of expectation equal to zero and with equal probability of lying anywhere within the bounds. The half-width a a of the symmetric rectangular distribution of possible values of Δ V ¯ Delta bar(ii(V)) is then a = ( 14 × 10 − 6 ) × ( 0.928571 ⁢ V ) + ( 2 × 10 − 6 ) × ( 1 ⁢ V ) = 15 ⁢ µV a = (14 xx 10^{-6}) xx (0.928571 "unitsml(V)") + (2 xx 10^{-6}) xx (1 "unitsml(V)") = 15 "unitsml(uV)", and from , u 2 ( Δ V ¯ ) = 75 ⁢ µV 2 u^2(Delta bar(ii(V))) = 75 "unitsml(uV^2)" and u ( Δ V ¯ ) = 8.7 ⁢ µV u(Delta bar(ii(V))) = 8.7 "unitsml(uV)". The estimate of the value of the measurand V ii(V), for simplicity denoted by the same symbol V ii(V), is given by V = V ¯ + Δ V ¯ = 0.928571 ⁢ V ii(V) = bar(ii(V)) + Delta bar(ii(V)) = 0.928571 "unitsml(V)". One can obtain the combined standard uncertainty of this estimate by combining the 12 ⁢ µV 12 "unitsml(uV)" Type A standard uncertainty of V ¯ bar(ii(V)) with the 8.7 ⁢ µV 8.7 "unitsml(uV)" Type B standard uncertainty of Δ V ¯ Delta bar(ii(V)). The general method for combining standard uncertainty components is given in , with this particular example treated in .

clause.p: In , the upper and lower bounds a + a_+ and a − a_"−" for the input quantity X i ii(X)_i may not be symmetric with respect to its best estimate x i x_i; more specifically, if the lower bound is written as a − = x i − b − a_"−" = x_i - b_"−" and the upper boundbounds on an input quantity as a + = x i − b + a_+ = x_i - b_+, then b − ≠ b + b_"−" != b_+. Since in this case x i x_i (assumed to be the expectationexpectation (or expected value) of X i ii(X)_i) is not at the centre of the interval a − a_"−" to a + a_+, the probabilityprobability distribution of X i ii(X)_i cannot be uniform throughout the interval. However, there may not be enough information available to choose an appropriate distribution; different models will lead to different expressions for the variance. In the absence of such information, the simplest approximation isType B evaluation of uncertainty
which is the variance of a rectangular distribution with full width b + + b − b_+ + b_"−". (Asymmetric distributions are also discussed in and .)

example.p: If in the value of the coefficient is given in the handbook as α 20 ( Cu ) = 16.52 × 10 − 6 ⁢ °C − 1 ii(alpha)_{20}("Cu") = 16.52 xx 10^{-6} "unitsml(degC^(-1))" and it is stated that “The smallest possible value is 16.40 × 10 − 6 ⁢ °C − 1 16.40 xx 10^{-6} "unitsml(degC^(-1))"” then b − = 0.12 × 10 − 6 ⁢ °C − 1 b_"−" = 0.12 xx 10^{-6} "unitsml(degC^(-1))", b + = 0.40 × 10 − 6 ⁢ °C − 1 b_+ = 0.40 xx 10^{-6} "unitsml(degC^(-1))", and, from , u ( α 20 ) = 0.15 × 10 − 6 ⁢ °C − 1 u(ii(alpha)_{20}) = 0.15 xx 10^{-6} "unitsml(degC^(-1))".

note.p: In many practical measurement situations where the bounds are asymmetricdistributionasymmetric, it may be appropriate to apply a correction to the estimate x i x_i of magnitude ( b + − b − ) / 2 (b_+ - b_"−")//2 so that the new estimate x ′ i x'_i of X i ii(X)_i is at the midpoint of the bounds: x ′ i = ( a − + a + ) / 2 x'_i = (a_"−" + a_+)//2. This reduces the situation to the case of , with new values b ′ + = b ′ − = ( b + + b − ) / 2 = ( a + − a − ) / 2 = a b'_+ = b'_{-} = (b_+ + b_"−")//2 = (a_+ - a_"−")//2 = a.

Based on the principle of maximum entropymaximum entropyprinciple of, the probability density functionprobability density function in the asymmetric case may be shown to be p ( X i ) = A exp [ − λ ( X i − x i ) p(ii(X)_i) = ii(A) exp[- ii(lambda) (ii(X)_i - x_i), with A = [ b − exp ( λ b − ) + b + exp ( − λ b + ) ] − 1 ii(A) = [b_"−" exp(ii(lambda) b_"−") + b_+ exp(- ii(lambda) b_+)]^{-1} and λ = { exp [ λ ( b − + b + ) ] − 1 } / { b − exp [ λ ( b − + b + ) ii(lambda) = {exp[ii(lambda) (b_"−" + b_+)]-1}//{b_"−" exp[ii(lambda)(b_"−" + b_+) + b_+}]. This leads to the variance u 2 ( x i ) = b + b − − ( b + + − b − ) / λ u^2(x_i) = b_+ b_"−" - (b_+ + -b_"−")//ii(lambda); for b + > b − b_+ > b_"−", λ > 0 ii(lambda) > 0 and for b + < b − b_+ < b_"−", λ < 0 ii(lambda) < 0.

clause.p: In , because there was no specific knowledge about the possible values of X i ii(X)_i within its estimated boundsbounds on an input quantity a − a_"−" to a + a_+, one could only assume that it was equally probable for X i ii(X)_i to take any value within those bounds, with zero probabilityprobability of being outside them. Such step function discontinuities in a probability distribution are often unphysical. In many cases, it is more realistic to expect that values near the bounds are less likely than those near the midpoint. It is then reasonable to replace the symmetric rectangular distributiondistributionrectangular with a symmetric trapezoidal distributiondistributiontrapezoidal having equal sloping sides (an isosceles trapezoid), a base of width a + − a − = 2 a a_+ - a_"−" = 2a, and a top of width 2 a β 2a ii(beta), where 0 ≤ β ≤ 1 0 <= ii(beta) <= 1. As β → 1 ii(beta) -> 1, this trapezoidal distribution approaches therectangular distribution of , while for β = 0 ii(beta) = 0, it is a triangular distributiondistributiontriangular [see and )]. Assuming such a trapezoidal distribution for X i ii(X)_i, one finds that the expectationexpectation (or expected value) of X i ii(X)_i is x i = ( a − + a + ) / 2 x_i = (a_"−" + a_+)//2 and its associated variance isType B evaluation of uncertainty
which becomes for the triangular distribution, β = 0 ii(beta) = 0,

note.p: For a normal distributionnormal distribution with expectation μ ii(mu) and standard deviation σ ii(sigma), the interval μ ± 3 σ ii(mu) pm 3 ii(sigma) encompasses approximately 99.7399.73 percent of the distribution. Thus, if the upper and lower bounds a + a_+ and a − a_"−" define 99.7399.73 percent limits rather than 100 percent limits, and X i ii(X)_i can be assumed to be approximately normally distributed rather than there being no specific knowledge about X i ii(X)_i between the bounds as in , then u 2 ( x i ) = a 2 / 9 u^2(x_i) = a^2//9. By comparison, the variance of a symmetric rectangular distribution of half-width a a is a 2 / 3 a^2//3 [] and that of a symmetric triangular distribution of half-width a a is a 2 / 6 a^2//6 []. The magnitudes of the variances of the three distributions are surprisingly similar in view of the large differences in the amount of information required to justify them.

The trapezoidal distribution is equivalent to the convolutionprobability distributions, convolving of two rectangular distributions , one with a half-width a 1 a_1 equal to the mean half-width of the trapezoid, a 1 = a ( 1 + β ) / 2 a_1 = a (1 + ii(beta))//2, the other with a half-width a 2 a_2 equal to the mean width of one of the triangular portions of the trapezoid, a 2 = a ( 1 − β ) / 2 a_2 = a (1 - ii(beta))//2. The variance of the distribution is u 2 = a 1 2 / 3 + a 2 2 / 3 u^2 = a_1^2//3 + a_2^2//3. The convolved distribution can be interpreted as a rectangular distribution whose width 2 a 1 2 a_1 has itself an uncertainty represented by a rectangular distribution of width 2 a 2 2 a_2 and models the fact that the bounds on an input quantitybounds on an input quantity are not exactly known. But even if a 2 a_2 is as large as 30 percent of a 1 a_1, u u exceeds a 1 / 3 a_1//sqrt(3) by less than 5 percent.

clause.p: It is important not to “double-count” uncertainty componentsuncertaintydouble-counting components of. If a component of uncertainty arising from a particular effect is obtained from a Type B evaluation, it should be included as an independent component of uncertainty in the calculation of the combined standard uncertainty of the measurement result only to the extent that the effect does not contribute to the observed variability of the observations. This is because the uncertainty due to that portion of the effect that contributes to the observed variability is already included in the component of uncertainty obtained from the statistical analysis of the observations.Type B evaluation of uncertainty

The discussion of Type B evaluation of standard uncertainty in to is meant only to be indicative. Further, evaluations of uncertainty should be based on quantitative data to the maximum extent possible, as emphasized in and .Type B evaluation of uncertainty

clause.p: representsthe estimation of the value of an input quantity X i ii(X)_i and the evaluation of the uncertainty of that estimate from an a priori distributiondistributiona priori of possible values of X i ii(X)_i, or probability distributionprobability distribution of X i ii(X)_i, based on all of the available information. For both cases shown, the input quantity is again assumed to be a temperature t t.Type B evaluation of uncertainty

For the case illustrated in ), it is assumed that little information is available about the input quantity t t and that all one can do is suppose that t t is described by a symmetric, rectangulardistributionrectangular a priori probability distribution of lower bound a − = 96 ⁢ °C a_"−" = 96 "unitsml(degC)", upper boundbounds on an input quantity a + = 104 ⁢ °C a_+ = 104 "unitsml(degC)", and thus half-width a = ( a + − a − ) / 2 = 4 ⁢ °C a = (a_+ - a_"−")//2 = 4 "unitsml(degC)" (see ). The probability density functionprobability density function of t t is then

As indicated in , the best estimate of t t is its expectation μ t = ( a + + a − ) / 2 = 100 ⁢ °C ii(mu)_t = (a_+ + a_"−")//2 = 100 "unitsml(degC)", which follows from . The standard uncertainty of this estimate is u ( μ t ) = a / 3 ≈ 2.3 ⁢ °C u(ii(mu)_t) = a//sqrt(3) ~~ 2.3 "unitsml(degC)", which follows from . [see ].Type B evaluation of uncertainty

For the case illustrated in ), it is assumed that the available information concerning t t is less limited and that t t can be described by a symmetric, triangulardistributiontriangular a priori probability distribution of the same lower bound a − = 96 ⁢ °C a_"−" = 96 "unitsml(degC)", the same upper boundbounds on an input quantity a + = 104 ⁢ °C a_+ = 104 "unitsml(degC)", and thus the same half-width a = ( a + − a − ) / 2 = 4 ⁢ °C a = (a_+ - a_"−")//2 = 4 "unitsml(degC)" as in (see ). The probability density functionprobability density function of t t is thenType B evaluation of uncertainty

As indicated in , the expectation of t t is μ t = ( a + + a − ) / 2 = 100 ⁢ °C ii(mu)_t = (a_+ + a_"−")//2 = 100 "unitsml(degC)", which follows from . The standard uncertainty of this estimate is u ( μ t ) = a / 6 ≈ 1.6 ⁢ °C u(ii(mu)_t) = a//sqrt(6) ~~ 1.6 "unitsml(degC)", which follows from [see ].

The above value, u ( μ t ) = 1.6 ⁢ °C u(ii(mu)_t) = 1.6 "unitsml(degC)", may be compared with u ( μ t ) = 2.3 ⁢ °C u(ii(mu)_t) = 2.3 "unitsml(degC)" obtained in from a rectangular distribution of the same 8 ⁢ °C 8 "unitsml(degC)" width; with σ = 1.5 ⁢ °C ii(sigma) = 1.5 "unitsml(degC)" of the normal distributionnormal distribution of ) whose − 2.58 σ -2.58 ii(sigma) to + 2.58 σ +2.58 ii(sigma) width, which encompasses 99 percent of the distribution, is nearly 8 ⁢ °C 8 "unitsml(degC)"; and with u ( t ¯ ) = 0.33 ⁢ °C u(bar(t)) = 0.33 "unitsml(degC)" obtained in from 20 observations assumed to have been taken randomly from the same normal distribution.

clause.title: Determining combined standard uncertaintycombined standard uncertainty
Uncorrelated input quantities
clause.p: This subclause treats the case where all input quantities are independent (). The case where two or more input quantities are related, that is, are interdependent or correlated (), is discussed in .correlation

The combined standard uncertainty u c ( y ) u_{"c"}(y) is the positive square root of the combined variance u c 2 ( y ) u_{"c"}^2(y), which is given by

where f f is the function given in . Each u ( x i ) u(x_i) is a standard uncertainty evaluated as described in (Type A evaluation) or as in (Type B evaluation). The combined standard uncertainty u c ( y ) u_{"c"}(y) is an estimated standard deviation and characterizes the dispersion of the values that could reasonably be attributed to the measurand Y ii(Y) (see ).combined standard uncertainty

See for an example of a situation where the contribution of higher-order terms to u c 2 ( y ) u_{"c"}^2(y) needs to be considered.

The partial derivativespartial derivatives ∂ f / ∂ x i del f//del x_i are equal to ∂ f / ∂ X i del f//del ii(X)_i evaluated at X i = x i ii(X)_i = x_i (see below). These derivatives, often called sensitivity coefficientssensitivity coefficients, describe how the output estimate y y varies with changes in the values of the input estimates x 1 , x 2 , … , x N x_1, x_2,..., x_N. In particular, the change in y y produced by a small change Δ x i Delta x_i in input estimate x i x_i is given by ( Δ y ) i = ( ∂ f / ∂ x i ) ( Δ x i ) (Delta y)_i = (del f//del x_i)(Delta x_i). If this change is generated by the standard uncertainty of the estimate x i x_i, the corresponding variation in y y is ( ∂ f / ∂ x i ) u ( x i ) (del f//del x_i) u(x_i). The combined variance u c 2 ( y ) u_{"c"}^2(y) can therefore be viewed as a sum of terms, each of which represents the estimated variance associated with the output estimate y y generated by the estimated variance associated with each input estimate x i x_i. This suggests writing ascombined standard uncertainty
where

note.p: Strictly speaking, the partial derivatives are ∂ f / ∂ x i = ∂ f / ∂ X i del f//del x_i = del f//del ii(X)_i evaluated at the expectations of the X i ii(X)_i. However, in practice, the partial derivatives are estimated by
The combined standard uncertainty u c ( y ) u_{"c"}(y) may be calculated numerically by replacing c i u ( x i ) c_i u(x_i) in withcombined standard uncertaintynumerical calculation of

That is, u i ( y ) u_i(y) is evaluated numerically by calculating the change in y y due to a change in x i x_i of + u ( x i ) +u(x_i) and of − u ( x i ) -u(x_i). The value of u i ( y ) u_i(y) may then be taken as | Z i | |ii(Z)_i| and the value of the corresponding sensitivity coefficient c i c_i as Z i / u ( x i ) ii(Z)_i//u(x_i).

clause.p: Instead of being calculated from the function f f, sensitivity coefficientssensitivity coefficients ∂ f / ∂ x i del f//del x_i are sometimes determined experimentally: one measures the change in Y ii(Y) produced by a change in a particular X i ii(X)_i while holding the remaining input quantities constant. In this case, the knowledge of the function f f (or a portion of it when only several sensitivity coefficientssensitivity coefficients are so determined) is accordingly reduced to an empirical first-order Taylor series expansion based on the measured sensitivity coefficientssensitivity coefficientssensitivity coefficientsexperimental determination of.

If for the measurand Y ii(Y) is expanded about nominal values X i , 0 ii(X)_{i,0} of the input quantities X i ii(X)_i, then, to first order (which is usually an adequate approximation), Y = Y 0 + c 1 δ 1 + c 2 δ 2 + … + c N δ N ii(Y) = ii(Y)_0 + c_1 ii(delta)_1 + c_2 ii(delta)_2 + ... + c_N ii(delta)_N, where Y 0 = f ( X 1 , 0 , X 2 , 0 , … , X N , 0 ) ii(Y)_0 = f(ii(X)_{1,0}, ii(X)_{2,0},..., ii(X)_{N,0}), c i = ( ∂ f / ∂ X i ) c_i = (del f//del ii(X)_i) evaluated at X i = X i , 0 ii(X)_i = ii(X)_{i,0}, and δ i = X i − X i , 0 ii(delta)_i = ii(X)_i - ii(X)_{i,0}. Thus, for the purposes of ananalysis of uncertainty, a measurand is usually approximated by a linear function of its variables by transforming its input quantities from X i ii(X)_i to δ i ii(delta)_i (see ).functional relationshiplinearization of a

example.p: From of , the estimate of the value of the measurand V ii(V) is V = V ¯ + Δ V ¯ ii(V) = bar(ii(V)) + Delta bar(ii(V)), where V ¯ = 0.928571 ⁢ V bar(ii(V)) = 0.928571 "unitsml(V)", u ( V ¯ ) = 12 ⁢ µV u(bar(ii(V)))=12 "unitsml(uV)", the additive correction Δ V ¯ = 0 Delta bar(ii(V)) = 0, and u ( Δ V ¯ ) = 8.7 ⁢ µV u (Delta bar(ii(V))) = 8.7 "unitsml(uV)". Since ∂ V / ∂ V ¯ = 1 del ii(V)//del bar(ii(V)) = 1, and ∂ V / ∂ ( Δ V ¯ ) = 1 del ii(V)//del (Delta bar(ii(V))) = 1, the combined variance associated with V ii(V) is given by

and the combined standard uncertainty is u c ( V ) = 15 ⁢ µV u_{"c"}(V) = 15 "unitsml(uV)", which corresponds to a relative combined standard uncertainty u c ( V ) / V u_{"c"}(ii(V))//ii(V) of 16 × 10 − 6 16 xx 10^{-6} (see ). This is an example of the case where the measurand is already a linear function of the quantities on which it depends, with coefficients c i = + 1 c_i = +1. It follows from that if Y = c 1 X 1 + c 2 X 2 + … + c N X N ii(Y) = c_1ii(X)_1 + c_2ii(X)_2 +... + c_Nii(X)_N and if the constants c i = + 1 o r − 1 c_i = +1 or -1, then u c 2 ( y ) = ∑ i = 1 N u 2 ( x i ) u_{"c"}^2 (y) = sum_{i=1}^{ii(N)} u^2 (x_i).

If each p i p_i is either +1 or -1, becomes [ u c ( y ) / y ] 2 = ∑ i = 1 N [ u ( x i ) / x i ] [u_{"c"}(y)//y]^2 = sum_{i=1}^ii(N) [u(x_i)//x_i], which shows that, for this special case, the relative combined variance associated with the estimate y y is simply equal to the sum of the estimated relative variances associated with the input estimates x i x_i.

and those derived from it such as and are valid only if the input quantities X i ii(X)_i are independentindependence or uncorrelated (the random variables, not the physical quantities that are assumed to be invariants — see ). If some of the X i ii(X)_i are significantly correlated, the correlations must be taken into account.
When the input quantities are correlated, the appropriate expression for the combined variance u c 2 ( y ) u_{"c"}^2(y) associated with the result of a measurement is

stem.asciimath: u_{"c"}^2(y) = sum_{i=1}^ii(N) sum_{j=1}^ii(N) {del f}/{del x_i} {del f}/{del x_j} u(x_i,x_j) = sum_{i=1}^ii(N) ({del f}/{del x_i})^2 u^2(x_i) + 2 sum_{i=1}^{ii(N)-1} sum_{j=i+1}^ii(N) {del f}/{del x_j} {del f}/{del x_j} u(x_i,x_j)

clause.p: where x i x_i and x j x_j are the estimates of X i ii(X)_i and X j ii(X)_j and u ( x i , x j ) = u ( x j , x i ) u(x_i,x_j) = u(x_j,x_i) is the estimated covariancecovariance associated with x i x_i and x j x_j. The degree of correlation between x i x_i and x j x_j is characterized by the estimated correlation coefficientcorrelation coefficient ()combined standard uncertainty

where r ( x i , x j ) = r ( x j , x i ) r (x_i,x_j) = r (x_j, x_i), and − 1 ≤ r ( x i , x j ) ≤ + 1 -1 <= r(x_i,x_j) <= +1. If the estimates x i x_i and x j x_j are independent, r ( x i , x j ) = 0 r(x_i,x_j) = 0, and a change in one does not imply an expected change in the other. (See , , and for further discussion.)
In terms of correlation coefficientcorrelation coefficients, which are more readily interpreted than covariances, the covariance term of may be written as

stem.asciimath: 2 sum_{i=1}^{ii(N)-1} sum_{j=i+1}^ii(N) {del f}/{del x_i} {del f}/{del x_j} u(x_i) u(x_j) r(x_i,x_j)

clause.p: then becomes, with the aid of ,

stem.asciimath: u_{"c"}^2(y) = sum_{i=1}^ii(N) c_i^2 u^2(x_i) + 2 sum_{i=1}^{ii(N)-1} sum_{j=i+1}^ii(N) c_i c_j u(x_i) u(x_j) r(x_i,x_j)

note.p: For the very special case where all of the input estimates are correlated with correlation coefficientcorrelation coefficients r ( x i , x j ) = + 1 r(x_i,x_j)= +1, reduces to

stem.asciimath: u_{"c"}^2(y) = [sum_{i=1}^ii(N) c_i u(x_i)]^2 = [sum_{i=1}^ii(N) {del f}/{del x_i} u(x_i)]^2

note.p: The combined standard uncertainty u c ( y ) u_{"c"}(y) is thus simply a linear sum of terms representing the variation of the output estimate y y generated by the standard uncertainty of each input estimate x i x_i (see ). [This linear sum should not be confused with the general law of error propagationerror propagationgeneral law of although it has a similar form; standard uncertainties are not errors (see ).]

example.p: Ten resistors, each of nominal resistance R i = 1000 ⁢ Ω ii(R)_i = 1000 "unitsml(Ohm)", are calibrated with a negligible uncertainty of comparison in terms of the same 1000 ⁢ Ω 1000 "unitsml(Ohm)" standard resistor R s ii(R)_s characterized by a standard uncertainty u ( R s ) = 100 ⁢ mΩ u(ii(R)_s) = 100 "unitsml(mOhm)" as given in its calibration certificate. The resistors are connected in series with wires having negligible resistance in order to obtain a reference resistance R ref ii(R)_{"ref"} of nominal value 10 ⁢ kΩ 10 "unitsml(kOhm)". Thus R ref = f ( R i ) = ∑ i = 1 10 R i ii(R)_{"ref"} = f (ii(R)_i) = sum_{i=1}^10 ii(R)_i. Since r ( x i , x j ) = r ( R i , R j ) = + 1 r(x_i,x_j) = r(ii(R)_i,ii(R)_j) = +1 for each resistor pair (see , ), the equation of this note applies. Since for each resistor ∂ f / ∂ x i = ∂ R ref / ∂ R i = 1 del f // del x_i = del ii(R)_{"ref"}// del ii(R)_i = 1, and u ( x i ) = u ( R i ) = u ( R s ) u(x_i) = u(ii(R)_i) = u(ii(R)_s) (see , ), that equation yields for the combined standard uncertainty of R ref ii(R)_{"ref"}, u c ( R ref ) = ∑ i = 1 10 u ( R s ) = 10 × ( 100 ⁢ mΩ ) = 1 ⁢ Ω u_{"c"}(ii(R)_{"ref"}) = sum_{i=1}^10 u (ii(R)_s) = 10 xx (100 "unitsml(mOhm)") = 1 "unitsml(Ohm)". The result u c ( R ref ) = [ ∑ i = 1 10 u 2 ( R s ) ] 1 / 2 = 0.32 ⁢ Ω u_{"c"}(ii(R)_{"ref"}) = [sum_{i=1}^10 u^2 (ii(R)_s)]^{1//2} = 0.32 "unitsml(Ohm)" obtained from is incorrect because it does not take into account that all of the calibrated values of the ten resistors are correlated.

note.p: The estimated variances u 2 ( x i ) u^2(x_i) and estimated covariances u ( x i , x j ) u(x_i, x_j) may be considered as the elements of a covariance matrixcovariance matrix with elements u i j u_{ij}. The diagonal elements u i i u_{i i} of the matrix are the variances u 2 ( x i ) u^2(x_i), while the off-diagonal elements u i j ( i ≠ j ) u_{ij} (i != j) are the covariances u ( x i , x j ) = u ( x j , x i ) u(x_i,x_j) = u(x_j,x_i). If two input estimates are uncorrelated, their associated covariance and the corresponding elements u i j u_{ij} and u j i u_{ji} of the covariance matrixcovariance matrix are 0. If the input estimates are all uncorrelated, all of the off-diagonal elements are zero and the covariance matrixcovariance matrix is diagonal. (See also .)
For the purposes of numerical evaluationcombined standard uncertaintynumerical calculation of, may be written as

Examples where it is necessary to use covariances as calculated from are given in and .

There may be significant correlation between two input quantities if the same measuring instrument, physical measurement standard, or reference datum having a significant standard uncertainty is used in their determination. For example, if a certain thermometer is used to determine a temperature correction required in the estimation of the value of input quantity X i ii(X)_i, and the same thermometer is used to determine a similar temperature correction required in the estimation of input quantity X j ii(X)_j, the two input quantities could be significantly correlated. However, if X i ii(X)_i and X j ii(X)_j in this example are redefined to be the uncorrected quantities and the quantities that define the calibration curve for the thermometer are included as additional input quantities with independent standard uncertainties, the correlation between X i ii(X)_i and X j ii(X)_j is removed. (See and for further discussion.)correlationelimination of

Correlations between input quantities cannot be ignored if present and significant. The associated covariancescovarianceexperimental evaluation of should be evaluated experimentally if feasible by varying the correlated input quantities (see ), or by using the pool of available information on the correlated variability of the quantities inquestion (Type B evaluation of covariance). Insight based on experience and general knowledge (see and ) is especially required when estimating the degree of correlation between input quantities arising from the effects of common influences, such as ambient temperature, barometric pressure, and humidity. Fortunately, in many cases, the effects of such influences have negligible interdependence and the affected input quantities can be assumed to be uncorrelated. However, if they cannot be assumed to be uncorrelated, the correlations themselves can be avoided if the common influences are introduced as additional independent input quantities as indicated in .correlationelimination of

Recommendation INC-1 (1980Recommendation INC-1 (1980) of the Working Group on the Statement of UncertaintiesWorking Group on the Statement of Uncertainties on which this Guide is based (see the ), and Recommendations 1 (CI-1981)Recommendation 1 (CI-1981), CIPM and 1 (CI-1986)Recommendation 1 (CI-1986), CIPM of the CIPMCIPM approving and reaffirming INC-1 (1980) (see and ), advocate the use of the combined standard uncertainty u c ( y ) u_{"c"}(y) as the parameter for expressing quantitatively the uncertainty of the result of a measurement. Indeed, in the second of its recommendations, the CIPM has requested that what is now termed combined standard uncertaintycombined standard uncertaintycombined standard uncertainty and international comparisons u c ( y ) u_{"c"}(y) be used “by all participants in giving the results of all international comparisons or other work done under the auspices of the CIPM and Comités Consultatifs”.

Although u c ( y ) u_{"c"}(y) can be universally used to express the uncertainty of a measurement result, in some commercial, industrial, and regulatory applications, and when health and safety are concerned, it is often necessary to give a measure of uncertainty that defines an interval about the measurement result that may be expected to encompass a large fraction of the distribution of values that could reasonably be attributed to the measurand. The existence of this requirement was recognized by the Working GroupWorking Group on the Statement of Uncertainties and led to paragraph 5 of Recommendation INC-1 (1980Recommendation INC-1 (1980). It is also reflected in Recommendation 1 (CI-1986)Recommendation 1 (CI-1986), CIPM of the CIPMCIPM.

The additional measure of uncertainty that meets the requirement of providing an interval of the kind indicated in is termed expanded uncertaintyexpanded uncertainty and is denoted by U ii(U). The expanded uncertainty U ii(U) is obtained by multiplying the combined standard uncertainty u c ( y ) u_{"c"}(y) by a coverage factorcoverage factor k k:

The result of a measurement is then conveniently expressed as Y = y ± U ii(Y) = y pm ii(U), which is interpreted to mean that the best estimate of the value attributable to the measurand Y ii(Y) is y y, and that y − U y - ii(U) to y + U y + ii(U) is an interval that may be expected to encompass a large fraction of the distribution of values that could reasonably be attributed to Y ii(Y). Such an interval is also expressed as y − U ≤ Y ≤ y + U y - ii(U) <= ii(Y) <= y + ii(U).

The terms confidence intervalconfidence interval (, ) and confidence levelconfidence level () have specific definitions in statistics and are only applicable to the interval defined by U ii(U) when certain conditions are met, including that all components of uncertainty that contribute to u c ( y ) u_{"c"}(y) be obtained from Type A evaluations. Thus, in this Guide, the word “confidence” is not used to modify the word “interval” when referring to the interval defined by U ii(U); and the term “confidence level” is not used in connection with that interval but rather the term “level of confidence”.level of confidence More specifically, U ii(U) is interpreted as defining an interval about the measurement result that encompasses a large fraction p p of the probability distribution characterized by that result and its combined standard uncertainty, and p p is the coverage probabilitycoverage probability or level of confidencelevel of confidence of the interval.expanded uncertainty

For preferred ways of stating the result of a measurement when the measure of uncertainty is u c ( y ) u_{"c"}(y) and when it is U ii(U), see and , respectively.

The value of the coverage factor
k

Occasionally, one may find that a known correction b b for a systematic effect has not been applied to the reported result of a measurement, but instead an attempt is made to take the effect into account by enlarging the “uncertainty” assigned to the result. This should be avoided; only in very special circumstances should corrections for known significant systematic effects not be applied to the result of a measurement (see for a specific case and how to treat it). Evaluating the uncertainty of a measurement result should not be confused with assigning a safety limitsafety limit to some quantity.correctionignoring auncertainty when a correction is not applied

Ideally, one would like to be able to choose a specific value of the coverage factor k k that would provide an interval Y = y ± U = y ± k u c ( y ) ii(Y) = y pm ii(U) = y pm k u_{"c"}(y) corresponding to a particular level of confidencelevel of confidence p p, such as 95 or 99 percent; equivalently, for a given value of k k, one would like to be able to state unequivocally the level of confidencelevel of confidence associated with that interval. However, this is not easy to do in practice because it requires extensive knowledge of the probability distribution characterized by the measurement result y y and its combined standard uncertainty u c ( y ) u_{"c"}(y). Although these parameters are of critical importance, they are by themselves insufficient for the purpose of establishing intervals having exactly known levels of confidence.

k = 2

k = 3
