A method for estimating the effective degrees of freedom of u c ( y ) u_{"c"}(y) is given in . of can then be used to help decide if this solution is appropriate for a particular measurement (see ).

In general, as one moves up the measurement hierarchymeasurement hierarchy, more details are required on how a measurement result and its uncertainty were obtained. Nevertheless, at any level of this hierarchy, including commercial and regulatory activities in the marketplace, engineering work in industry, lower-echelon calibration facilities, industrial research and development, academic research, industrial primary standards and calibration laboratories, and the national standards laboratories and the BIPMBIPM, all of the information necessary for the re-evaluation of the measurement should be available to others who may have need of it. The primary difference is that at the lower levels of the hierarchical chain, more of the necessary information may be made available in the form of published calibration and test system reports, test specifications, calibration and test certificates, instruction manuals, international standards, national standards, and local regulations.measurement result and its uncertaintyavailability of information describing a

When the details of a measurement, including how the uncertainty of the result was evaluated, are provided by referring to published documents, as is often the case when calibration results are reported on a certificate, it is imperative that these publications be kept up-to-date so that they are consistent with the measurement proceduremeasurement procedure actually in use.

Numerous measurements are made every day in industry and commerce without any explicit report of uncertainty. However, many are performed with instruments subject to periodic calibration or legal inspection. If the instruments are known to be in conformance with their specifications or with the existing normative documents that apply, the uncertainties of their indications may be inferred from these specifications or from these normative documents.measurement result and its uncertaintyavailability of information describing auncertaintylack of an explicit report of

Although in practice the amount of information necessary to document a measurement result depends on its intended use, the basic principle of what is required remains unchanged: when reporting the result of a measurement and its uncertainty, it is preferable to err on the side of providing too much information rather than too little. For example, one shouldmeasurement result and its uncertaintyreporting in detail a

li.p: describe clearly the methods used to calculate the measurement result and its uncertainty from the experimental observations and input data;
list all uncertainty components and document fully how they were evaluated;
present the data analysis in such a way that each of its important steps can be readily followed and the calculation of the reported result can be independently repeated if necessary;
give all corrections and constants used in the analysis and their sources.

clause.p: A test of the foregoing list is to ask oneself “Have I provided enough information in a sufficiently clear manner that my result can be updated in the future if new information or data become available?”
When reporting the result of a measurement, and when the measure of uncertainty is the combined standard uncertainty u c ( y ) u_{"c"}(y), one should

li.p: give a full description of how the measurand Y ii(Y) is defined;
give the estimate y y of the measurand Y ii(Y) and its combined standard uncertainty u c ( y ) u_{"c"}(y); the units of y y and u c ( y ) u_{"c"}(y) should always be given;
include the relative combined standard uncertainty u c ( y ) / | y | u_{"c"}(y)//|y|, | y | ≠ 0 |y| != 0, when appropriate;
give the information outlined in or refer to a published document that contains it.

clause.p: If it is deemed useful for the intended users of the measurement result, for example, to aid in future calculations of coverage factors or to assist in understanding the measurement, one may indicate

li.p: the estimated effective degrees of freedom ν eff ii(nu)_{"eff"} (see );

the Type Acombined standard uncertainty from Type A components alonedegrees of freedomeffectiveof Type A components aloneType A combined standard uncertainty and Type Bcombined standard uncertainty from Type B components alonedegrees of freedomeffectiveof Type B components aloneType B combined standard uncertainty combined standard uncertainties u cA ( y ) u_{"cA"}(y) and u cB ( y ) u_{"cB"}(y) and their estimated effective degrees of freedom ν effA ii(nu)_{"effA"} and ν effB ii(nu)_{"effB"} (see ).combined standard uncertaintyrelativecombined standard uncertaintyreporting

clause.p: When the measure of uncertainty is u c ( y ) u_{"c"}(y), it is preferable to state the numerical result of the measurement in one of the following four ways in order to prevent misunderstanding. (The quantity whose value is being reported is assumed to be a nominally 100 ⁢ g 100 "unitsml(g)" standard of mass m S m_{"S"}; the words in parentheses may be omitted for brevity if u c u_{"c"} is defined elsewhere in the document reporting the result.)measurement result and its uncertaintyformats for reporting a

li.p: “ m S = 100.02147 ⁢ g m_{"S"} = 100.02147 "unitsml(g)" with (a combined standard uncertainty) u c = 0.35 ⁢ mg u_{"c"} = 0.35 "unitsml(mg)".”

“ m S = 100.02147 ( 35 ) ⁢ g m_{"S"}= 100.02147(35) "unitsml(g)", where the number in parentheses is the numerical value of (the combined standard uncertainty) u c u_{"c"} referred to the corresponding last digits of the quoted result.”

“ m S = 100.02147 ( 0.00035 ) ⁢ g m_{"S"} = 100.02147(0.00035) "unitsml(g)", where the number in parentheses is the numerical value of (the combined standard uncertainty) u c u_{"c"} expressed in the unit of the quoted result.”

“ m S = ( 100.02147 ± 0.00035 ) ⁢ g m_{"S"} = (100.02147 pm 0.00035) "unitsml(g)", where the number following the symbol ± pm is the numerical value of (the combined standard uncertainty) u c u_{"c"} and not a confidence interval.”

note.p: The ± pm format should be avoided whenever possible because it has traditionally been used to indicate an interval corresponding to a high level of confidence and thus may be confused with expanded uncertainty (see ). Further, although the purpose of the caveat in is to prevent such confusion, writing Y = y ± u c ( y ) ii(Y) = y pm u_{"c"}(y) might still be misunderstood to imply, especially if the caveat is accidentally omitted, that an expanded uncertainty with k = 1 k = 1 is intended and that the interval y − u c ( y ) ≤ Y ≤ y + u c ( y ) y - u_{"c"}(y) <= ii(Y) <= y + u_{"c"}(y) has a specified level of confidence p p, namely, that associated with the normal distribution (see ). As indicated in and , interpreting u c ( y ) u_{"c"}(y) in this way is usually difficult to justify.combined standard uncertaintyreporting

clause.p: When reporting the result of a measurement, and when the measure of uncertainty is the expanded uncertainty U = k u c ( y ) ii(U) = k u_{"c"}(y), one should

li.p: give a full description of how the measurand Y ii(Y) is defined;
state the result of the measurement as Y = y ± U ii(Y) = y pm ii(U) and give the units of y y and U ii(U);
include the relative expanded uncertaintyexpanded uncertaintyrelative U / | y | ii(U)//|y|, | y | ≠ 0 |y| != 0, when appropriate;
give the value of k k used to obtain U ii(U) [or, for the convenience of the user of the result, give both k k and u c ( y ) u_{"c"}(y)];
give the approximate level of confidence associated with the interval y ± U y pm ii(U) and state how it was determined;
give the information outlined in or refer to a published document that contains it.expanded uncertaintyreporting

clause.p: When the measure of uncertainty is U ii(U), it is preferable, for maximum clarity, to state the numerical result of the measurement as in the following example. (The words in parentheses may be omitted for brevity if U ii(U), u c u_{"c"}, and k k are defined elsewhere in the document reporting the result.)measurement result and its uncertaintyformats for reporting a

“ m S = ( 100.02147 ± 0.00079 ) ⁢ g m_{"S"} = (100.02147 pm 0.00079) "unitsml(g)", where the number following the symbol ± pm is the numerical value of (an expanded uncertainty) U = k u c ii(U) = k u_{"c"}, with U U determined from (a combined standard uncertainty) u c = 0.35 ⁢ mg u_{"c"} = 0.35 "unitsml(mg)" and (a coverage factor) k = 2 k = 2, 26 based on the t t-distribution for ν = 9 ii(nu) = 9 degrees of freedom, and defines an interval estimated to have a level of confidence of 95 percent.”

If a measurement determines simultaneously more than one measurand, that is, if it provides two or more output estimates y i y_i (see ,, and ), then, in addition to giving y i y_i and u c ( y i ) u_{"c"}(y_i), give the covariance matrix elements u ( y i , y j ) u(y_i,y_j) or the elements r ( y i , y j ) r(y_i,y_j) of the correlation coefficient matrix () (and preferably both).correlated output estimates or quantitiescorrelation coefficient matrixcovariance matrix

The numerical values of the estimate y y and its standard uncertainty u c ( y ) u_{"c"}(y) or expanded uncertainty U ii(U) should not be given with an excessive number of digits. It usually suffices to quote u c ( y ) u_{"c"}(y) and U ii(U) [as well as the standard uncertainties u ( x i ) u(x_i) of the input estimates x i x_i] to at most two significant digits, although in some cases it may be necessary to retain additional digits to avoid round-off errors in subsequent calculations.

In reporting final results, it may sometimes be appropriate to round uncertaintiesuncertaintiesrounding of up rather than to the nearest digit. For example, u c ( y ) = 10.47 ⁢ mΩ u_{"c"}(y) = 10.47 "unitsml(mOhm)" might be rounded up to 11 ⁢ mΩ 11 "unitsml(mOhm)". However, common sense should prevail and a value such as u ( x i ) = 28.05 ⁢ kHz u(x_i) = 28.05 "unitsml(kHz)" should be rounded down to 28 ⁢ kHz 28 "unitsml(kHz)". Output and input estimates should be rounded to be consistent with their uncertainties; for example, if y = 10.05762 ⁢ Ω y = 10.05762 "unitsml(Ohm)" with u c ( y ) = 27 ⁢ mΩ u_{"c"}(y) = 27 "unitsml(mOhm)", y y should be rounded to 10.058 ⁢ Ω 10.058 "unitsml(Ohm)". Correlation coefficients should be given with three-digit accuracy if theirabsolute values are near unity.correlation coefficientsignificant digits for auncertaintiessignificant digits for

In the detailed report that describes how the result of a measurement and its uncertainty were obtained, one should follow the recommendations of and thusmeasurement result and its uncertaintyreporting in detail a

li.p: give the value of each input estimate x i x_i and its standard uncertainty u ( x i ) u(x_i) together with a description of how they were obtained;
give the estimated covariances or estimated correlation coefficients (preferably both) associated with all input estimates that are correlated, and the methods used to obtain them;
give the degrees of freedom for the standard uncertainty of each input estimate and how it was obtained;

give the functional relationship Y = f ( X 1 , X 2 , … , X N ) ii(Y) = f(ii(X)_1, ii(X)_2,..., ii(X)_N) and, when they are deemed useful, the partial derivatives or sensitivity coefficients ∂ f / ∂ x i del f//del x_i. However, any such coefficients determined experimentally should be given.

note.p: Since the functional relationship f f may be extremely complex or may not exist explicitly but only as a computer program, it may not always be possible to give f f and its derivatives. The function f f may then be described in general terms or the program used may be cited by an appropriate reference. In such cases, it is important that it be clear how the estimate y y of the measurand Y ii(Y) and its combined standard uncertainty u c ( y ) u_{"c"}(y) were obtained.

clause.title: Summary of procedure for evaluating and expressing uncertainty
clause.p: The steps to be followed for evaluating and expressing the uncertainty of the result of a measurement as presented in this Guide may be summarized as follows:

li.p: Express mathematically the relationship between the measurand Y ii(Y) and the input quantities X i ii(X)_i on which Y ii(Y) depends: Y = f ( X 1 , X 2 , … , X N ) ii(Y) = f(ii(X)_1, ii(X)_2,..., ii(X)_N). The function f f should contain every quantity, including all corrections andcorrection factors, that can contribute a significant component of uncertainty to the result of the measurement (see and ).
Determine x i x_i, the estimated value of input quantity X i ii(X)_i, either on the basis of the statistical analysis of series of observations or by other means (see ).

Evaluate the standard uncertainty u ( x i ) u(x_i) of each input estimate x i x_i. For an input estimate obtained from the statistical analysis of series of observations, the standard uncertainty is evaluated as described in (Type A evaluation of standard uncertainty). For an input estimate obtained by other means, the standard uncertainty u ( x i ) u(x_i) is evaluated as described in (Type B evaluation of standard uncertainty).
Evaluate the covariances associated with any input estimates that are correlated (see ).

Calculate the result of the measurement, that is, the estimate y y of the measurand Y ii(Y), from the functional relationship f f using for the input quantities X i ii(X)_i the estimates x i x_i obtained in step 2 (see ).

Determine the combined standard uncertainty u c ( y ) u_{"c"}(y) of the measurement result y y from the standard uncertainties and covariances associated with the input estimates, as described in . If the measurement determines simultaneously more than one output quantity, calculate their covariances (see ,, , and ).

If it is necessary to give an expanded uncertainty U ii(U), whose purpose is to provide an interval y − U y - ii(U) to y + U y + ii(U) that may be expected to encompass a large fraction of the distribution of values that couldreasonably be attributed to the measurand Y ii(Y), multiply the combined standard uncertainty u c ( y ) u_{"c"}(y) by a coverage factor k k, typically in the range 2 to 3, to obtain U = k u c ( y ) ii(U) = k u_{"c"}(y). Select k k on the basis of the level of confidence required of the interval (see ,, and especially , which discusses the selection of a value of k k, that produces an interval having a level of confidence close to a specified value).

Report the result of the measurement y y together with its combined standard uncertainty u c ( y ) u_{"c"}(y) or expanded uncertainty U ii(U) as discussed in and ; use one of the formats recommended in and . Describe, as outlined also in , how y y and u c ( y ) u_{"c"}(y) or U ii(U) were obtained.

annex.title: Recommendations of Working Group and CIPM

clause.title: Recommendation INC-1 (1980Recommendation INC-1 (1980)
clause.p: The Working Group on the Statement of UncertaintiesWorking Group on the Statement of Uncertainties (see ) was convened in October 1980 by the Bureau International des Poids et Mesures (BIPM) in response to a request of the Comité International des Poids et Mesures (CIPM). It prepared a detailed report for consideration by the CIPM that concluded with Recommendation INC-1 (1980) . The English translation of this Recommendation is given in of this Guide and the French text, which is authoritative, is as follows :BIPMCIPM
Expression des incertitudes expérimentales

li.p: L’incertitude d’un résultat de mesure comprend généralement plusieurs composantes qui peuvent être groupées en deux catégories d’après la méthode utilisée pour estimer leur valeur numérique:
celles qui sont évaluées à l’aide de méthodes statistiques,
celles qui sont évaluées par d’autres moyens.

Il n’y a pas toujours une correspondance simple entre le classement dans les catégories A ou B et le caractère «aléatoire» ou «systématique» utilisé antérieurement pour classer les incertitudes. L’expression «incertitude systématique» est susceptible de conduire à des erreurs d’interprétation; elle doit être évitée.
Toute description détaillée de l’incertitude devrait comprendre une liste complète de ses composantes et indiquer pour chacune la méthode utilisée pour lui attribuer une valeur numérique.

Les composantes de la catégorie A sont caractérisées par les variances estimées s i 2 s_i^2 (ou les «écart-types» estimés s i s_i) et les nombres ν i nu_i de degrés de liberté. Le cas échéant, les covariances estimées doivent être données.

Les composantes de la catégorie B devraient être caractérisées par des termes u j 2 u_j^2 qui puissent être considérés comme des approximations des variances correspondantes dont on admet l’existence. Les termes u j 2 u_j^2 peuvent être traités comme des variances et les termes u j u_j comme des écarts-types. Le cas échéant, les covariances doivent être traitées de façon analogue.

L’incertitude composée devrait être caractérisée par la valeur obtenue en appliquant la méthode usuelle de combinaison des variances. L’incertitude composée ainsi que ses composantes devraient être exprimées sous la forme d’«écart-types».

Si pour des utilisations particulières on est amené à multiplier par un facteur l’incertitude composée afin d’obtenir une incertitude globale, la valeur numérique de ce facteur doit toujours être donnée.

clause.title: Recommendation 1 (CI-1981)Recommendation 1 (CI-1981), CIPM
clause.p: The CIPMCIPM reviewed the report submitted to it by the Working Group on the Statement of UncertaintiesWorking Group on the Statement of Uncertainties and adopted the following recommendation at its 70th meeting held in October 1981 :
Expression of experimental uncertainties
The Comité International des Poids et Mesures

li.p: the need to find an agreed way of expressing measurement uncertainty in metrology,
the effort that has been devoted to this by many organizations over many years,
the encouraging progress made in finding an acceptable solution, which has resulted from the discussions of the Working Group on the Expression of Uncertainties which met at BIPMBIPM in 1980,
that the proposals of the Working Group might form the basis of an eventual agreement on the expression of uncertainties,
that the proposals of the Working Group be diffused widely;
that BIPMBIPM attempt to apply the principles therein to international comparisons carried out under its auspices in the years to come;
that other interested organizations be encouraged to examine and test these proposals and let their comments be known to BIPM;
that after two or three years BIPM report back on the application of these proposals.

clause.title: Recommendation 1 (CI-1986)Recommendation 1 (CI-1986), CIPM
clause.p: The CIPMCIPM further considered the matter of the expression of uncertainties at its 75th meeting held in October 1986 and adopted the following recommendation :combined standard uncertainty and Comités Consultatifscombined standard uncertainty and international comparisons
Expression of uncertainties in work carried out under the auspices of the CIPM
The Comité International des Poids et Mesures,

considering the adoption by the Working Group on the Statement of UncertaintiesWorking Group on the Statement of Uncertainties of Recommendation INC-1 (1980Recommendation INC-1 (1980) and the adoption by the CIPM of Recommendation 1 (CI-1981)Recommendation 1 (CI-1981), CIPM,

considering that certain members of Comités Consultatifs may want clarification of this Recommendationfor the purposes of work that falls under their purview, especially for international comparisons,

recognizes that paragraph 5 of Recommendation INC-1 (1980) relating to particular applications, especially those having commercial significance, is now being considered by a working group of the International Standards Organization (ISO) common to the ISOISO, OIMLOIML and IECIEC, with the concurrence and cooperation of the CIPM,

requests that paragraph 4 of Recommendation INC-1 (1980) should be applied by all participants ingiving the results of all international comparisons or other work done under the auspices of the CIPM and the Comités Consultatifs and that the combined uncertainty of type A and type B uncertainties in terms of one standard deviation should be given.

clause.title: Source of definitions
clause.p:
The definitions of the general metrological terms relevant to this Guide that are given here have been taken from the International vocabulary of basic and general terms in metrology (abbreviated VIMVIM), second edition, 1993
Footnote to the 2008 version:
The third edition of the vocabulary was published in 2008, under the title JCGM 200:2008, International vocabulary of metrology — Basic and general concepts and associated terms (VIM). , published by the International Organization for Standardization (ISO), in the name of the seven organizations that supported its development and nominated the experts who prepared it: the Bureau International des Poids et Mesures (BIPM), the International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC), the International Federation of Clinical Chemistry (IFCC), ISO, the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPACIUPAC), the International Union of Pure and Applied Physics (IUPAPIUPAP), and the International Organization of Legal Metrology (OIML). The VIM should be the first source consulted for the definitions of terms not included either here or in the text.
Some basic statistical terms and concepts are given in , while the terms “true value”, “error”, and “uncertainty” are further discussed in .

terms.title: Definitions
terms.p: As in , in the definitions that follow, the use of parentheses around certain words of some terms means that the words may be omitted if this is unlikely to cause confusion.
The terms in boldface in some notes are additional metrological terms defined in those notes, either explicitly or implicitly (see Reference ).

verbal-definition.p: attribute of a phenomenon, body or substance that may be distinguished qualitatively and determined quantitatively

termnote.p: The term quantity may refer to a quantity in a general sense (see ) or to a particular quantityparticular quantity (see ).
Quantities that can be placed in order of magnitude relative to one another are called quantities of the same kind.
Quantities of the same kind may be grouped together into categories of quantities, for example:

li.p: work, heat, energy
thickness, circumference, wavelength.

termnote.p:
Symbols for quantities are given in ISO 31
Footnote to the 2008 version:
The ISO 31 series is under revision as a series of ISO 80000 and IEC 80000 documents. (Some of these documents have already been published.) .

termexample.p: Quantities in a general sense: length, time, mass, temperature, electrical resistance, amount-of-substance concentration.

li.p: length of a given rod
electrical resistance of a given specimen of wire
amount-of-substance concentration of ethanol in a given sample of wine.

expression.name: value (of a quantity)value of a quantity

verbal-definition.p: magnitude of a particular quantity generally expressed as a unit of measurement multiplied by a number

termnote.p: The value of a quantity may be positive, negative or zero.
The value of a quantity may be expressed in more than one way.
The values of quantities of dimension one are generally expressed as pure numbers.
A quantity that cannot be expressed as a unit of measurement multiplied by a number may be expressed by reference to a conventional reference scale or to a measurement procedure or to both.

termexample.p: Length of a rod: 5.34 ⁢ m 5.34 "unitsml(m)" or 534 ⁢ cm 534 "unitsml(cm)".
Mass of a body: 0.152 ⁢ kg 0.152 "unitsml(kg)" or 152 ⁢ g 152 "unitsml(g)".
Amount of substance of a sample of water (H2O): 0.012 ⁢ mol 0.012 "unitsml(mol)" or 12 ⁢ mmol 12 "unitsml(mmol)".

expression.name: true value (of a quantity)true value of a quantity

verbal-definition.p: value consistent with the definition of a given particular quantity

Guide Comment: See , in particular , for the reasons why the term “true value” is not used in this Guide and why the terms “true value of a measurand” (or of a quantity) and “value of a measurand” (or of aquantity) are viewed as equivalent.

termnote.p: This is a value that would be obtained by a perfect measurement.
True values are by nature indeterminate.

The indefinite article “a”, rather than the definite article “the”, is used in conjunction with “true value” because there may be many values consistent with the definition of a given particular quantity.

expression.name: conventional true value (of a quantity)conventional true value of a quantity

verbal-definition.p: value attributed to a particular quantity and accepted, sometimes by convention, as having an uncertainty appropriate for a given purpose
Guide Comment: See the Guide Comment to .

termnote.p: “Conventional true value” is sometimes called assigned value, best estimate of the value, conventional value or reference value. “Reference value”, in this sense, should not be confused with “reference value” in the senseused in the Note to VIM:1993, definition 5.7.
Frequently, a number of results of measurements of a quantity is used to establish a conventional true value.

termexample.p: At a given location, the value assigned to the quantity realized by a reference standard may be taken as a conventional true value.
The CODATA (1986) recommended value for the Avogadro constant: 6.0221367 × 10 23 ⁢ mol − 1 6.0221367 xx 10^{23} "unitsml(mol^(-1))".

verbal-definition.p: set of operations having the object of determining a value of a quantity

termnote.p: The operations may be performed automatically.

verbal-definition.p: scientific basis of a measurement

termexample.p: The thermoelectric effect applied to the measurement of temperature.
The Josephson effect applied to the measurement of electric potential difference.
The Doppler effect applied to the measurement of velocity.
The Raman effect applied to the measurement of the wave number of molecular vibrations.

verbal-definition.p: logical sequence of operations, described generically, used in the performance of measurements

termnote.p: Methods of measurement may be qualified in various ways such as:

li.p: substitution method
differential method
null method.

verbal-definition.p: set of operations, described specifically, used in the performance of particular measurements according to a given method

termnote.p: A measurement procedure is usually recorded in a document that is sometimes itself called a “measurement procedure” (or a measurement method) and is usually in sufficient detail to enable an operator to carry out a measurement without additional information.

verbal-definition.p: particular quantity subject to measurement

termnote.p: The specification of a measurand may require statements about quantities such as time, temperature and pressure.

termexample.p: Vapour pressure of a given sample of water at 20 ⁢ °C 20 "unitsml(degC)".

verbal-definition.p: quantity that is not the measurand but that affects the result of the measurement

Guide Comment: The definition of influence quantity is understood to include values associated with measurement standards, reference materials, and reference data upon which the result of a measurement may depend, as well as phenomena such as short-term measuring instrument fluctuations and quantities such as ambient temperature, barometric pressure and humidity.

termexample.p: Temperature of a micrometer used to measure length.
Frequency in the measurement of the amplitude of an alternating electric potential difference.
Bilirubin concentration in the measurement of haemoglobin concentration in a sample of human blood plasma.

expression.name: result of a measurementresult of a measurement

verbal-definition.p: value attributed to a measurand, obtained by measurement

termnote.p: When a result is given, it should be made clear whether it refers to:

li.p: the indication
the uncorrected result
the corrected result

termnote.p: and whether several values are averaged.
A complete statement of the result of a measurement includes information about the uncertainty of measurement.

verbal-definition.p: result of a measurement before correction for systematic error
result of a measurement after correction for systematic error
closeness of the agreement between the result of a measurement and a true value of the measurand
Guide Comment: See the Guide Comment to .

termnote.p: “Accuracy” is a qualitative concept.
The term precisionprecision should not be used for “accuracy”.

expression.name: repeatability (of results of measurements)repeatability of results of measurements

verbal-definition.p: closeness of the agreement between the results of successive measurements of the same measurand carried out under the same conditions of measurement

termnote.p: These conditions are called repeatability conditionsrepeatability conditions.
Repeatability conditions include:

li.p: the same measurement procedure
the same observer
the same measuring instrument, used under the same conditions
the same location
repetition over a short period of time.

termnote.p: Repeatability may be expressed quantitatively in terms of the dispersion characteristics of the results.

expression.name: reproducibility (of results of measurements)reproducibility of results of measurements

verbal-definition.p: closeness of the agreement between the results of measurements of the same measurand carried out under changed conditions of measurement

termnote.p: A valid statement of reproducibility requires specification of the conditions changed.
The changed conditions may include:

li.p: principle of measurement
method of measurement
observer
measuring instrument
reference standard
location
conditions of use
time.

termnote.p: Reproducibility may be expressed quantitatively in terms of the dispersion characteristics of the results.
Results are here usually understood to be corrected results.

verbal-definition.p: for a series of n n measurements of the same measurand, the quantity s ( q k ) s(q_k) characterizing the dispersion of the results and given by the formula:
q k q_k being the result of the k kth measurement and q ¯ bar(q) being the arithmetic mean of the n n results considered
Guide Comment: Some of the symbols used in the VIM have been changed in order to achieve consistency with the notation used in of this Guide.

termnote.p: Considering the series of n n values as a sample of a distribution, q ¯ bar(q) is an unbiased estimate of the mean μ q ii(mu)_q, and s 2 ( q k ) s^2(q_k) is an unbiased estimate of the variance σ 2 ii(sigma)^2, of that distribution.

The expression s ( q k ) / n s(q_k)//sqrt(n) is an estimate of the standard deviation of the distribution of q ¯ bar(q) and is called the experimental standard deviation of the mean.standard deviation of the meanexperimental
“Experimental standard deviation of the mean” is sometimes incorrectly called standard error of the mean.
Adapted from VIM:1993, definition 3.8.

verbal-definition.p: parameter, associated with the result of a measurement, that characterizes the dispersion of the values that could reasonably be attributed to the measurand
Guide Comment: It is pointed out in the VIM that this definition and the notes are identical to those in this Guide (see ).

termnote.p: The parameter may be, for example, a standard deviation (or a given multiple of it), or the half-width of an interval having a stated level of confidence.

Uncertainty of measurement comprises, in general, many components. Some of these components may be evaluated from the statistical distribution of the results of series of measurements and can be characterized by experimental standard deviations. The other components, which can also be characterized by standard deviations, are evaluated from assumed probability distributions based on experience or other information.

It is understood that the result of the measurement is the best estimate of the value of the measurand, and that all components of uncertainty, including those arising from systematic effects, such as components associated with corrections and reference standards, contribute to the dispersion.

verbal-definition.p: result of a measurement minus a true value of the measurandmeasurand

Guide Comment: If the result of a measurement depends on the values of quantities other than the measurand, the errors of the measured values of these quantities contribute to the error of the result of the measurement. Also see the Guide Comment to and to .

termnote.p: Since a true value cannot be determined, in practice a conventional true value is used [see VIM:1993, definitions 1.19 () and 1.20 ()].

When it is necessary to distinguish “error” from “relative error”, the former is sometimes called absolute error of measurementerror of measurement. This should not be confused with absolute value of error, which is the modulus of the error.

verbal-definition.p: error of measurement divided by a true value of the measurand
Guide Comment: See the Guide Comment to .

termnote.p: Since a true value cannot be determined, in practice a conventional true value is used [see VIM:1993, definitions 1.19 () and 1.20 ()].

verbal-definition.p: result of a measurement minus the mean that would result from an infinite number of measurements of the same measurand carried out under repeatability conditions
Guide Comment: See the Guide Comment to .

termnote.p: Random error is equal to error minus systematic error.
Because only a finite number of measurements can be made, it is possible to determine only an estimate of random error.

verbal-definition.p: mean that would result from an infinite number of measurements of the same measurand carried out under repeatability conditions minus a true value of the measurand

Guide Comment: The error of the result of a measurement (see ) may often be considered as arising from a number of random and systematic effects that contribute individual components of error to the error of the result. Also see the Guide Comment to and to .

termnote.p: Systematic error is equal to error minus random error.
Like true value, systematic error and its causes cannot be completely known.
For a measuring instrument, see “bias” (VIM:1993, definition 5.25).

verbal-definition.p: value added algebraically to the uncorrected result of a measurement to compensate for systematic error

termnote.p: The correction is equal to the negative of the estimated systematic error.
Since the systematic error cannot be known perfectly. the compensation cannot be complete.

verbal-definition.p: numerical factor by which the uncorrected result of a measurement is multiplied to compensate for systematic error

termnote.p: Since the systematic error cannot be known perfectly, the compensation cannot be complete.

clause.title: Source of definitions
clause.p:
The definitions of the basic statistical terms given in this annex are taken from International Standard ISO 3534-1ISO 3534-1:1993
Footnote to the 2008 version:
ISO 3534-1:1993 has been cancelled and replaced by ISO 3534-1:2006. Note that some of the terms and definitions have been revised. For further information, see the latest edition. . This should be the first source consulted for the definitions of terms not included here. Some of these terms and their underlying concepts are elaborated upon in following the presentation of their formal definitions in in order to facilitate further the use of this Guide. However, , which also includes the definitions of some related terms, is not based directly on ISO 3534-1:1993.

terms.title: Definitions
terms.p: As in and , the use of parentheses around certain words of some terms means that the words may be omitted if this is unlikely to cause confusion.
Terms to are defined in terms of the properties of populations. The definitions of terms to are related to a set of observations (see Reference ).

verbal-definition.p: a real number in the scale 0 to 1 attached to a random event

termnote.p: It can be related to a long-run relative frequency of occurrence or to a degree of belief that an event will occur. For a high degree of belief, the probability is near 1.

verbal-definition.p: a variable that may take any of the values of a specified set of values and with which is associated a probability distribution [ISO 3534-1:1993, definition 1.3 ()]
Guide Comment: The symbol Pr ( A ) "Pr"(ii(A)) is used in this Guide in place of the symbol Pr ( A ) "Pr"(ii(A)) used in ISO 3534-1:1993.

termnote.p: A random variable that may take only isolated values is said to be “discrete”. A random variable which may take any value within a finite or infinite interval is said to be “continuous”.
The probability of an event A is denoted by Pr ( A ) "Pr"(ii(A)) or P ( A ) ii(P)(ii(A)).

expression.name: probability distributionprobability distribution (of a random variable)

verbal-definition.p: a function giving the probability that a random variable takes any given value or belongs to a given set of values

termnote.p: The probability on the whole set of values of the random variable equals 1.

verbal-definition.p: a function giving, for every value x x, the probability that the random variable X ii(X) be less than or equal to x x:

expression.name: probability density functionprobability density function (for a continuous random variable)

verbal-definition.p: the derivative (when it exists) of the distribution function:

termnote.p: f ( x ) d x f(x)" d"x is the “probability element”:probability element

verbal-definition.p: a function giving, for each value x i x_i of a discrete random variable X ii(X), the probability p i p_i that the random variable equals x i x_i:
a quantity used in describing the probability distribution of a random variable
the relationship between two or several random variables within a distribution of two or more random variables

termnote.p: Most statistical measures of correlation measure only the degree of linear relationship.

expression.name: expectation (of a random variable or of a probability distribution)expectation (or expected value)

li.p: For a discrete random variable X ii(X) taking the values x i x_i with the probabilities p i p_i, the expectation, if it exists, is
the sum being extended over all the values x i x_i which can be taken by X ii(X).
For a continuous random variable X ii(X) having the probability density function f ( x ) f(x), the expectation, if it exists, is

stem.asciimath: ii(mu) = ii(E)(ii(X)) = int x f(x)" d"x

li.p: the integral being extended over the interval(s) of variation of X ii(X).

verbal-definition.p: a random variable the expectation of which equals zero

termnote.p: If the random variable X ii(X) has an expectation equal to μ ii(mu), the corresponding centred random variable is ( X − μ ii(X) - ii(mu)).

expression.name: variancevariance (of a random variable or of a probability distribution)

verbal-definition.p: the expectation of the square of the centred random variable [ISO 3534-1:1993, definition 1.21 ()]:

expression.name: standard deviationstandard deviation (of a random variable or of a probability distribution)

verbal-definition.p: the positive square root of the variance:

expression.name:
central moment
If, in the definition of the moments, the quantities X ii(X), X − a ii(X) - a, Y ii(Y), Y − b ii(Y) - b, etc. are replaced by their absolute values, i.e. | X | abs(ii(X)), | X − a | abs(ii(X) - a), | Y | abs(ii(Y)), | Y − b | abs(ii(Y) - b), etc., other moments called “absolute moments” are defined. of order
q
q

verbal-definition.p: in a univariate distribution, the expectation of the q qth power of the centred random variable ( X − μ ii(X) - ii(mu)):

termnote.p: The central moment of order 2 is the variance [ISO 3534-1:1993, definition 1.22 ()] of the random variable X ii(X).central moment of order q q

verbal-definition.p: the probability distribution of a continuous random variable X ii(X), the probability density function of which is
for − ∞ < x < + ∞ -oo < x < +oo.

termnote.p: μ ii(mu) is the expectation and σ ii(sigma) is the standard deviation of the normal distribution.

verbal-definition.p: a property which helps to identify or differentiate between items of a given population

termnote.p: The characteristic may be either quantitative (by variables) or qualitative (by attributes).

verbal-definition.p: the totality of items under consideration

termnote.p: In the case of a random variable, the probability distribution [ISO 3534-1:1993, definition 1.3 ()] is considered to define the population of that variable.

verbal-definition.p: the number of occurrences of a given type of event or the number of observations falling into a specified class
the empirical relationship between the values of a characteristic and their frequencies or their relative frequencies

termnote.p: The distribution may be graphically presented as a histogram (ISO 3534 -1:1993, definition 2.17), bar chart (ISO 3534 -1:1993, definition 2.18), cumulative frequency polygon (ISO 3534-1:1993, definition 2.19), or as a two-way table (ISO 3534-1:1993, definition 2.22).

verbal-definition.p: the sum of values divided by the number of values

termnote.p: The term “mean” is used generally when referring to a population parameter and the term “average” when referring to the result of a calculation on the data obtained in a sample.

The average of a simple random sample taken from a population is an unbiased estimator of the mean of this population. However, other estimators, such as the geometric or harmonic mean, or the median or mode, are sometimes used.

verbal-definition.p: a measure of dispersion, which is the sum of the squared deviations of observations from their average divided by one less than the number of observations

Guide Comment: The variance defined here is more appropriately designated the “sample estimate of thepopulation variance”. The variance of a sample is usually defined to be the central moment of order 2 of the sample (see and ).

termnote.p: The sample variance is an unbiased estimator of the population variance.
The variance is n / ( n − 1 ) n//(n - 1) times the central moment of order 2 (see note to ISO 3534-1:1993, definition 2.39).

termexample.p: For n n observations x 1 , x 2 , … , x n x_1, x_2,...,x_n with average

verbal-definition.p: the positive square root of the variance

termnote.p: The sample standard deviation is a biased estimator of the population standard deviation.

expression.name: central moment of order q q

verbal-definition.p: in a distribution of a single characteristic, the arithmetic mean of the q qth power of the difference between the observed values and their average x ¯ bar(x):
where n n is the number of observations

termnote.p: The central moment of order 1 is equal to zero.central moment of order q q

verbal-definition.p: a function of the sample random variables

termnote.p: A statistic, as a function of random variables, is also a random variable and as such it assumes different values from sample to sample. The value of the statistic obtained by using the observed values in this function may be used in a statistical test or as an estimate of a population parameter, such as a mean or a standard deviation.

verbal-definition.p: the operation of assigning, from the observations in a sample, numerical values to the parameters of a distribution chosen as the statistical model of the population from which this sample is taken

termnote.p: A result of this operation may be expressed as a single value [point estimate; see ISO 3534-1:1993, definition 2.51 ()] or as an interval estimate [see ISO 3534-1:1993, definitions 2.57 () and 2.58 ()].

verbal-definition.p: a statistic used to estimate a population parameter
the value of an estimator obtained as a result of an estimation

expression.name: two-sided confidence intervalconfidence intervaltwo-side confidence interval

verbal-definition.p: when T 1 ii(T)_1 and T 2 ii(T)_2 are two functions of the observed values such that, θ ii(theta) being a population parameter to be estimated, the probability Pr ( T 1 ≤ θ ≤ T 2 ) "Pr"(ii(T)_1 <= ii(theta) <= ii(T)_2) is at least equal to ( 1 − α 1 - ii(alpha)) [where ( 1 − α 1 - ii(alpha)) is a fixed number, positive and less than 1], the interval between T 1 ii(T)_1 and T 2 ii(T)_2 is a two-sided ( 1 − α 1 - ii(alpha)) confidence interval for θ ii(theta)

termnote.p: The limits T 1 ii(T)_1 and T 2 ii(T)_2 of the confidence interval are statistics [ISO 3534-1:1993, definition 2.45 ()] and as such will generally assume different values from sample to sample.

In a long series of samples, the relative frequency of cases where the true value of the population parameter θ ii(theta) is covered by the confidence interval is greater than or equal to ( 1 − α 1 - ii(alpha)).

verbal-definition.p: when T ii(T) is a function of the observed values such that, θ ii(theta) being a population parameter to be estimated, the probability Pr ( T ≥ θ ) "Pr"(ii(T) >= ii(theta)) [or the probability Pr ( T ≤ θ ) "Pr"(ii(T) <= ii(theta))] is at least equal to ( 1 − α 1 - ii(alpha)) [where ( 1 − α 1 - ii(alpha)) is a fixed number, positive and less than 1], the interval from the smallest possible value of θ ii(theta) up to T ii(T) (or the interval from T ii(T) up to the largest possible value of θ ii(theta)) is a one-sidedone-sided confidence interval ( 1 − α 1 - ii(alpha)) confidence interval for θ ii(theta)

termnote.p: The limit T ii(T) of the confidence interval is a statistic [ISO 3534-1:1993, definition 2.45 ()] and as such will generally assume different values from sample to sample.
See Note 2 of ISO 3534-1:1993, definition 2.57 ().

verbal-definition.p: the value ( 1 − α 1 - ii(alpha)) of the probability associated with a confidence interval or a statistical coverage interval [See ISO 3534-1:1993, definitions 2.57 (), 2.58 () and 2.61 ().]

termnote.p: ( 1 − α 1 - ii(alpha)) is often expressed as a percentage.

verbal-definition.p: an interval for which it can be stated with a given level of confidence that it contains at least a specified proportion of the population

termnote.p: When both limits are defined by statistics, the interval is two-sided. When one of the two limits is not finite or consists of the boundary of the variable, the interval is one-sided.
Also called “statistical tolerance interval”tolerance intervalstatistical. This term should not be used because it may cause confusion with “tolerance interval” which is defined in ISO 3534-2:1993.

verbal-definition.p: in general, the number of terms in a sum minus the number of constraints on the terms of the sum

clause.title: Standard deviationstandard deviation
clause.p: The standard deviation is the positive square root of the variance. Whereas a Type A standard uncertaintyType A standard uncertainty is obtained by taking the square root of the statistically evaluated variance, it is often more convenient when determining a Type B standard uncertaintyType B standard uncertainty to evaluate a nonstatistical equivalent standard deviation first and then to obtain the equivalent variance by squaring the standard deviation.

clause.title: Covariance matrixcovariance matrix
clause.p: For a multivariate probability distribution, the matrix V ii(bb(V)) with elements equal to the variances and covariances of the variables is termed the covariance matrix. The diagonal elements, υ ( z , z ) ≡ σ 2 ( z ) ii(upsilon)(z,z) -= ii(sigma)^2(z) or s ( z i , z i ) ≡ s 2 ( z i ) s(z_i,z_i) -= s^2(z_i), are the variances, while the off-diagonal elements, υ ( y , z ) ii(upsilon)(y, z) or s ( y i , z i ) s(y_i, z_i), are the covariances.

clause.title: Correlation coefficientcorrelation coefficient
clause.p: The correlation coefficient is a measure of the relative mutual dependence of two variables, equal to the ratio of their covariances to the positive square root of the product of their variances. Thus

Because ρ ii(rho) and r r are pure numbers in the range -1 to +1 inclusive, while covariances are usually quantities with inconvenient physical dimensions and magnitudes, correlation coefficients are generally more useful than covariances.
For multivariate probability distributions, the correlation coefficient matrixcorrelation coefficient matrix is usually given in place of the covariance matrix. Since ρ ( y , y ) = 1 ii(rho)(y,y) = 1 and r ( y i , y i ) = 1 r(y_i, y_i) = 1, the diagonal elements of this matrix are unity.

clause.title: Independenceindependence
clause.p:
Two random variablerandom variables are statistically independent if their joint probability distribution is the product of their individual probability distributions.
If two random variables are independent, their covariance and correlation coefficient are zero, but the converse is not necessarily true.

clause.title: The t t-distribution; Student’s distributionStudent’s distribution

As ν → ∞ ii(nu) -> oo, the t t-distribution approaches a normal distribution with μ = 0 ii(mu) = 0 and σ = 1 ii(sigma) = 1 (see ).

annex.title: “True” value, error, and uncertaintytrue value of a quantity
annex.p: The term true value () has traditionally been used in publications on uncertainty but not in this Guide for the reasons presented in this annex. Because the terms “measurand”, “error”, and “uncertainty” are frequently misunderstood, this annex also provides additional discussion of the ideas underlying them to supplement the discussion given in . Two figures are presented to illustrate why the concept of uncertainty adopted in this Guide is based on the measurement result and its evaluated uncertainty rather than on the unknowable quantities “true” value and error.error of measurement

clause.p: The first step in making a measurement is to specify the measurandmeasurand — the quantity to be measured; the measurand cannot be specified by a value but only by a description of a quantity. However, in principle, a measurand cannot be completely described without an infinite amount of information. Thus, to the extent that it leaves room for interpretation, incomplete definitionuncertainty due to incomplete definition of the measurand of the measurand introduces into the uncertainty of the result of a measurement a component of uncertainty that may or may not be significant relative to the accuracy required of the measurement.
Commonly, the definition of a measurandmeasurand specifies certain physical states and conditions.

example.p: The velocity of sound in dry air of composition (mole fraction) N 2 = 0.7808 "N"_2 = 0.7808, O 2 = 0.2095 "O"_2 = 0.2095, Ar = 0.00935 "Ar" = 0.00935, and CO 2 = 0.00035 "CO"_2 = 0.00035 at the temperature T = 273.15 ⁢ K ii(T) = 273.15 "unitsml(K)" and pressure p = 101325 ⁢ Pa p = 101325 "unitsml(Pa)".

clause.p: Ideally, the quantity realizedquantityrealized for measurement would be fully consistent with the definition of the measurand. Often, however, such a quantity cannot be realized and the measurement is performed on a quantity that is an approximation of the measurand.

clause.title: The “true” value and the corrected valuetrue value of a quantity

clause.p: The result of the measurement of the realized quantityquantityrealized is corrected for the difference between that quantity and the measurand in order to predict what the measurement result would have been if the realized quantity had in fact fully satisfied the definition of the measurand. The result of the measurement of the realized quantity is also corrected for all other recognized significant systematic effects. Although the final corrected resultcorrected result is sometimes viewed as the best estimate of the “true” valuetrue value of a quantity of the measurand, in reality the result is simply the best estimate of the value of the quantity intended to be measured.

As an example, suppose that the measurand is the thickness of a given sheet of material at as pecified temperature. The specimen is brought to a temperature near the specified temperature and its thickness at a particular place is measured with a micrometer. The thickness of the material at that place and temperature, under the pressure applied by the micrometer, is the realized quantityquantityrealized.

The temperature of the material at the time of the measurement and the applied pressure are determined. The uncorrected result of the measurement of the realized quantityquantityrealized is then corrected by taking into account the calibration curve of the micrometer, the departure of the temperature of the specimen from the specified temperature, and the slight compression of the specimen under the applied pressure.

The corrected resultcorrected result may be called the best estimate of the “true” value, “true” in the sense that it is the value of a quantity that is believed to satisfy fully the definition of the measurandmeasurand; but had the micrometer been applied to a different part of the sheet of material, the realized quantityquantityrealized would have been different with a different “true” valuetrue value of a quantity. However, that “true” value would be consistent with the definition of the measurand because the latter did not specify that the thickness was to be determined at a particular place on the sheet. Thus in this case, because of an incompleteuncertainty due to incomplete definition of the measurand definition of the measurand, the “true” value has an uncertainty that can be evaluated from measurements made at different places on the sheet. At some level, every measurand has such an “intrinsic” uncertaintyuncertaintyintrinsic that can in principle be estimated in some way. This is the minimum uncertaintyuncertaintyminimum with which a measurand can be determined, and every measurement that achieves such an uncertainty may be viewed as the best possible measurement of the measurand. To obtain a value of the quantity in question having a smaller uncertainty requires that the measurandmeasurand be more completely defined.measurandbest possible measurement of the
In the example, the measurand’s specification leaves many other matters in doubt that might conceivably affect the thickness: the barometric pressure, the humidity, the attitude of the sheet in the gravitational field, the way it is supported, etc.
Although a measurand should be defined in sufficient detail that any uncertainty arising from its incomplete definition is negligible in comparison with the required accuracy of the measurement, it must be recognized that this may not always be practicable. The definition may, for example, be incomplete because it does not specify parameters that may have been assumed, unjustifiably, to have negligible effect; or it may imply conditions that can never be fully met and whose imperfect realization is difficult to take into account. For instance, in the example of , the velocity of sound implies infinite plane waves of vanishingly small amplitude. To the extent that the measurement does not meet these conditions, diffraction and nonlinear effects need to be considered.
Inadequate specification of the measurand can lead to discrepancies between the results of measurements of ostensibly the same quantity carried out in different laboratories.

The term “true value of a measurand” or of a quantity (often truncated to “true value”) is avoided inthis Guide because the word “true” is viewed as redundant. “Measurand” (see ) means “particular quantity subject to measurement”, hence “value of a measurand” means “value of a particular quantity subject to measurement”. Since “particular quantity” is generally understood to mean a definite or specified quantity (see ), the adjective “true” in “true value of a measurand” (or in “true value of a quantity”)true value of a quantity is unnecessary — the “true” value of the measurand (or quantity) is simply the value of the measurand (or quantity). In addition, as indicated in the discussion above, a unique “true” value is only an idealized concept.

clause.title: Errorerror of measurement
clause.p: A corrected measurement result is not the value of the measurand — that is, it is in error — because of imperfect measurement of the realized quantityquantityrealized due to random variations of the observations (random effects), inadequate determination of the corrections for systematic effects, and incomplete knowledge of certain physical phenomena (also systematic effects). Neither the value of the realized quantity nor the value of the measurand can ever be known exactly; all that can be known is their estimated values. In the example above, the measured thickness of the sheet may be in error, that is, may differ from the value of the measurand (the thickness of the sheet), because each of the following may combine to contribute an unknown error to the measurement result:corrected result

li.p: slight differences between the indications of the micrometer when it is repeatedly applied to the same realized quantity;
imperfect calibration of the micrometer;
imperfect measurement of the temperature and of the applied pressure;
incomplete knowledge of the effects of temperature, barometric pressure, and humidity on the specimen or the micrometer or both.

clause.p: Whereas the exact values of the contributions to the error of a result of a measurement are unknown and unknowable, the uncertainties associated with the random and systematic effects that give rise to the error can be evaluated. But, even if the evaluated uncertainties are small, there is still no guarantee that the error in the measurement result is small; for in the determination of a correction or in the assessment of incomplete knowledge, a systematic effect may have been overlooked because it is unrecognized. Thus the uncertainty of a result of a measurementuncertainty of measurement is not necessarily an indication of the likelihood that the measurement result is near the value of the measurand; it is simply an estimate of the likelihood of nearness to the best value that is consistent with presently available knowledge.

Uncertainty of measurementuncertainty of measurement is thus an expression of the fact that, for a given measurand and a given result of measurement of it, there is not one value but an infinite number of values dispersed about the result that are consistent with all of the observations and data and one’s knowledge of the physical world, and that with varying degrees of credibility can be attributed to the measurand.

It is fortunate that in many practical measurement situations, much of the discussion of this annex does not apply. Examples are when the measurand is adequately well defined; when standards or instruments are calibrated using well-known reference standards that are traceable to national standards; and when the uncertainties of the calibration corrections are insignificant compared to the uncertainties arising from random effects on the indications of instruments, or from a limited number of observations (see ). Nevertheless, incomplete knowledge of influence quantities and their effects can often contribute significantly to the uncertainty of the result of a measurementuncertainty of measurement.

depicts some of the ideas discussed in of this Guide and in this annex. It illustrates why the focus of this Guide is uncertaintyuncertainty of measurement and not error. The exact error of a result of a measurement is, in general, unknown and unknowable. All one can do is estimate the values of input quantities, including corrections for recognized systematic effectsystematic effects, together with their standard uncertainties (estimated standard deviations), either from unknown probability distributions that are sampled by means of repeated observations, or from subjectiveprobabilitysubjective or a priori distributionsdistributiona priori based on the pool of available information; and then calculate the measurement result from the estimated values of the input quantities and the combined standard uncertaintycombined standard uncertainty of that result from the standard uncertainties of those estimated values. Only if there is a sound basis for believing that all of this has been done properly, with no significant systematic effectsystematic effects having been overlooked, can one assume that the measurement result is a reliable estimate of the value of the measurand and that its combined standard uncertaintystandard uncertainty is a reliable measure of its possible error.error of measurementuncertainty of a correction
In ), the observations are shown as a histogramhistogram for illustrative purposes [see and b)].
The correction for an error is equal to the negative of the estimate of the error. Thus in , and in as well, an arrow that illustrates the correction for an error is equal in length but points in the opposite direction to the arrow that would have illustrated the error itself, and vice versa. The text of the figure makes clear if a particular arrow illustrates a correction or an error.

depicts some of the same ideas illustrated in but in a different way. Moreover, it also depicts the idea that there can be many values of the measurand if the definition of the measurand is incompleteuncertainty due to incomplete definition of the measurand [entry g) of ]. The uncertaintyuncertainty of measurement arising from this incompleteness of definition as measured by the variance is evaluated from measurementserror of measurement of multiple realizations of the measurandmeasurandmany values of the, using the same method, instruments, etc. (see ).
In the column headed “Variance”, the variances are understood to be the variances u i 2 ( y ) u_i^2(y) defined in in ; hence they add linearly as shown.

annex.title: Motivation and basis for Recommendation INC-1 (1980Recommendation INC-1 (1980)
annex.p: This annex gives a brief discussion of both the motivation and statistical basis for Recommendation INC-1 (1980) of the Working Group on the Statement of Uncertainties upon which this Guide rests. For further discussion, see References , , , .

clause.p: This Guide presents a widely applicable method for evaluating and expressing uncertainty in measurement. It provides a realistic rather than a “safe” valueuncertaintysafe of uncertainty based on the concept that there is no inherent difference between an uncertainty component arising from a random effectrandom effect and one arising from a correctionuncertainty of a correction for a systematic effectsystematic effect (see and ). The method stands, therefore, in contrast to certain older methods that have the following two ideas in common.

The first idea is that the uncertainty reported should be “safe”uncertaintysafe or “conservative”, meaning that it must never err on the side of being too small. In fact, because the evaluation of the uncertainty of a measurement result is problematic, it was often made deliberately large.

The second idea is that the influences that give rise to uncertainty were always recognizable as either “random”random or “systematic”systematic with the two being of different natures; the uncertainties associated with each were to be combined in their own way and were to be reported separately (or when a single number was required, combined in some specified way). In fact, the method of combining uncertainties was often designed to satisfy the safety requirement.

clause.title: Justification for realistic uncertainty evaluationsuncertainty evaluationsjustification for realistic

clause.p: When the value of a measurand is reported, the best estimate of its value and the best evaluation of the uncertainty of that estimate must be given, for if the uncertainty is to err, it is not normally possible to decide in which direction it should err “safely”uncertaintysafe. An understatement of uncertainties might cause too much trust to be placed in the values reported, with sometimes embarrassing or even disastrous consequences. A deliberate overstatement of uncertainties could also have undesirable repercussions. It could cause users of measuring equipment to purchase instruments that are more expensive than they need, or it could cause costly products to be discarded unnecessarily or the services of a calibration laboratory to be rejected.uncertainty evaluationsjustification for realistic

That is not to say that those using a measurement result could not apply their own multiplicative factor to its stated uncertainty in order to obtain an expanded uncertainty that defines an interval having a specified level of confidence and that satisfies their own needs, nor in certain circumstances that institutions providing measurement results could not routinely apply a factor that provides a similar expanded uncertainty that meets the needs of a particular class of users of their results. However, such factors (always to be stated) must be applied to the uncertainty as determined by a realistic method, and only after the uncertainty has been so determined, so that the interval defined by the expanded uncertainty has the level of confidence required and the operation may be easily reversed.uncertainty evaluationsjustification for realistic

Those engaged in measurement often must incorporate in their analyses the results of measurements made by others, with each of these other results possessing an uncertainty of its own. In evaluating the uncertainty of their own measurement result, they need to have a best value, not a “safe” valueuncertaintysafe, of the uncertainty of each of the results incorporated from elsewhere. Additionally, there must be a logical and simple way in which these imported uncertainties can be combined with the uncertainties of their own observations to give the uncertainty of their own result. Recommendation INC-1 (1980Recommendation INC-1 (1980) provides such a way.uncertainty evaluationsjustification for realistic

clause.title: Justification for treating all uncertainty components identically
clause.p: The focus of the discussion of this subclause is a simple example that illustrates how this Guide treats uncertainty components arising from random effectrandom effects and from corrections for systematic effects in exactly the same way in the evaluation of the uncertainty of the result of a measurement. It thus exemplifies the viewpoint adopted in this Guide and cited in , namely, that all components of uncertainty are of the same nature and are to be treated identically. The starting point of the discussion is a simplified derivation of the mathematical expression for the propagation of standard deviationsstandard deviationspropagation of, termed in this Guide the law of propagation of uncertaintyuncertaintylaw of propagation of.uncertainty of a correction

Let the output quantity z = f ( w 1 , w 2 , … , w N ) z = f (w_1,w_2,...,w_N) depend on N ii(N) input quantities w 1 , w 2 , … , w N w_1, w_2,...,w_N, where each w i w_i is described by an appropriate probability distribution. Expansion of f f about the expectations of the w i w_i, E ( w i ) ≡ μ i ii(E)(w_i) -= ii(mu)_i, in a first-order Taylor seriesTaylor series yields for small deviations of z z about μ z ii(mu)_z in terms of small deviations of w i w_i about μ i ii(mu)_i,

stem.asciimath: z - ii(mu)_z = sum_{i=1}^ii(N) {del f}/{del w_i} (w_i - ii(mu)_i)

clause.p: where all higher-order termshigher-order terms are assumed to be negligible and μ z = f ( μ 1 , μ 2 , … , μ N ) ii(mu)_z = f(ii(mu)_1,ii(mu)_2,...,ii(mu)_N). The square of the deviation z − μ z z - ii(mu)_z is then given by
which may be written as

in [together with ], which is used to calculate combined standard uncertainty, is identical to except that is expressed in terms of estimates of the variances, standard deviations, and correlation coefficients.

In the traditional terminology, is often called the “general law of error propagation”error propagationgeneral law ofstandard deviationspropagation ofuncertaintylaw of propagation of, an appellation that is better applied to an expression of the form Δ z = ∑ i = 1 N ( ∂ f / ∂ w i ) Δ w i Delta z = sum_{i=1}^ii(N) (del f//del w_i)Delta w_i, where Δ z Delta z is the change in z z due to (small) changes Δ w i Delta w_i in the w i w_i [see ]. In fact, it is appropriate to call the law of propagation of uncertainty as is done in this Guide because it shows how the uncertainties of the input quantities w i w_i, taken equal to the standard deviations of the probability distributions of the w i w_i, combine to give the uncertainty of the output quantity z z if that uncertainty is taken equal to the standard deviation of the probability distribution of z z.
