The requirement of normality when propagating confidence intervalconfidence intervals using may be one of the reasons for the historic separation of the components of uncertainty derived from repeated observations, which were assumed to be normally distributed, from those that were evaluated simply as upper and lower bounds.

Consider the following example: z z depends on only one input quantity w w, z = f ( w ) z = f(w), where w w is estimated by averaging n n values w k w_k of w w; these n n values are obtained from n n independent repeated observations q k q_k of a random variable q q; and w k w_k and q k q_k are related by

Here α ii(alpha) is a constant “systematic”systematic offset or shift common to each observation, and β ii(beta) is a common scale factor. The offset and the scale factor, although fixed during the course of the observations, are assumed to be characterized by a priori probability distributionsdistributiona priori, with α ii(alpha) and β ii(beta) the best estimates of the expectations of these distributions.
The best estimate of w w is the arithmetic mean or average w ¯ bar(w) obtained from

stem.asciimath: bar(w) = 1/n sum_{k=1}^n w_k = 1/n sum_{k=1}^n (ii(alpha) + ii(beta) q_k)

clause.p: The quantity z z is then estimated by f ( w ¯ ) = f ( α , β , q 1 , q 2 , … , q n ) f(bar(w)) = f(ii(alpha), ii(beta), q_1, q_2,...,q_n) and the estimate u 2 ( z ) u^2(z) of its variance σ 2 ( z ) ii(sigma)^2(z) is obtained from . If for simplicity it is assumed that z = w z = w so that the best estimate of z z is z = f ( w ¯ ) = w ¯ z = f(bar(w)) = bar(w), then the estimate u 2 ( z ) u^2(z) can be readily found. Noting from that

stem.asciimath: {:({:{del f}/{del ii(alpha)} = 1,:}), ({:{del f}/{del ii(beta)} = 1/n sum_{k=1}^n q_k = bar(q):}):}

clause.p: and

denoting the estimated variances of α ii(alpha) and β ii(beta) by u 2 ( α ) u^2(ii(alpha)) and u 2 ( β ) u^2(ii(beta)), respectively, and assuming that the individual observations are uncorrelated, one finds from

where s 2 ( q k ) s^2(q_k) is the experimental variance of the observations q k q_k calculated according to , and s 2 ( q k ) / n = s 2 ( q ¯ ) s^2(q_k)//n = s^2(bar(q)) is the experimental variance of the mean q ¯ bar(q) [ in ].

In the traditional terminology, the third term on the right-hand side of is called a “random”random contribution to the estimated variance u 2 ( z ) u^2(z) because it normally decreases as the number of observations n n increases, while the first two terms are called “systematic”systematic contributions because they do not depend on n n.

Of more significance, in some traditional treatments of measurement uncertainty, is questioned because no distinction is made between uncertainties arising from systematic effects and those arising from randomrandom effects. In particular, combining variances obtained from a priori probabilityprobability distributionsdistributiona priori with those obtained from frequency-based distributions is deprecated because the concept of probability is considered to be applicable only to events that can be repeated a large number of times under essentially the same conditions, with the probability p p of an event ( 0 ≤ p ≤ 1 0 <= p <= 1) indicating the relative frequencyfrequencyrelative with which the event will occur.

In contrast to this frequency-based point of view of probability, an equally valid viewpoint is that probability is a measure of the degree of beliefdegree of belief that an event will occur , . For example, suppose one has a chance of winning a small sum of money D ii(D) and one is a rational bettor. One’s degree of beliefdegree of belief in event A ii(A) occurring is p =0.5 if one is indifferent to these two betting choices:

li.p: receiving D ii(D) if event A ii(A) occurs but nothing if it does not occur;
receiving D ii(D) if event A ii(A) does not occur but nothing if it does occur.

clause.p: Recommendation INC-1 (1980) upon which this Guide rests implicitly adopts such a viewpoint of probabilityprobability since it views expressions such as as the appropriate way to calculate the combined standard uncertainty of a result of a measurement.

There are three distinct advantages to adopting an interpretation of probabilityprobability based on degree of belief, the standard deviation (standard uncertainty), and the law of propagation of uncertaintyuncertaintylaw of propagation of [] as the basis for evaluating and expressing uncertainty in measurement, as has been done in this Guide:

li.p: the law of propagation of uncertainty allows the combined standard uncertaintycombined standard uncertainty of one result to be readily incorporated in the evaluation of the combined standard uncertainty of another result in which the first is used;
the combined standard uncertainty can serve as the basis for calculating intervals that correspond in a realistic way to their required levels of confidence; and

it is unnecessary to classify components as “random”random or “systematic”systematic (or in any other manner) when evaluating uncertainty because all components of uncertainty are treated in the same way.uncertaintycategorizing or classifying components of

clause.p: Benefit is highly advantageous because such categorization is frequently a source of confusion; an uncertainty component is not either “random”random or “systematic”.systematic Its nature is conditioned by the use made of the corresponding quantity, or more formally, by the context in which the quantity appears in the mathematical model that describes the measurement. Thus, when its corresponding quantity is used in a different context, a “random”random component may become a “systematic”systematic component, and vice versa.

For the reason given in above, Recommendation INC- 1 (1980) does not classify components of uncertainty as either “random”random or “systematic”.systematic In fact, as far as the calculation of the combined standard uncertainty of a measurement result is concerned, there is no need to classify uncertainty components and thus no real need for any classificational scheme. Nonetheless, since convenient labels can sometimes be helpful in the communication and discussion of ideas, Recommendation INC-1 (1980Recommendation INC-1 (1980) does provide a scheme for classifying the two distinct methods by which uncertainty components may be evaluated, “A” and “B” (see ,, and ).Type A evaluation of uncertaintyType B evaluation of uncertainty

Classifying the methods used to evaluate uncertainty components avoids the principal problem associated with classifying the componentsuncertaintycategorizing or classifying components of themselves, namely, the dependence of the classification of a component on how the corresponding quantity is used. However, classifying the methods rather than the components does not preclude gathering the individual components evaluated by the two methods into specific groups for a particular purpose in a given measurement, for example, when comparing the experimentally observed and theoretically predicted variability of the output values of a complex measurement system (see ).uncertaintygrouping components of

clause.title: Standard deviations as measures of uncertainty

clause.p: requires that no matter how the uncertainty of the estimate of an input quantity is obtained, it must be evaluated as a standard uncertaintystandard uncertainty, that is, as an estimated standard deviation. If some “safe”uncertaintysafe alternative is evaluated instead, it cannot be used in . In particular, if the “maximum error bound”error boundmaximum (the largest conceivable deviation from the putative best estimate) is used in , the resulting uncertainty will have an ill-defined meaning and will be unusable by anyone wishing to incorporate it into subsequent calculations of the uncertainties of other quantities (see ).uncertaintystandard deviations as measures of

When the standard uncertainty of an input quantity cannot be evaluated by an analysis of the results of an adequate number of repeated observationsobservationsrepeated, a probability distributionprobability distribution must be adopted based on knowledge that is much less extensive than might be desirable. That does not, however, make the distribution invalid or unreal; like all probability distributions, it is an expression of what knowledge exists.uncertaintystandard deviations as measures of

Evaluations based on repeated observationsobservationsrepeated are not necessarily superior to those obtained by other means. Consider s ( q ¯ ) s(bar(q)), the experimental standard deviation of the meanuncertainty of the experimental standard deviation of the mean of n n independent observations q k q_k of a normally distributed random variable q q [see in ]. The quantity s ( q ¯ ) s(bar(q)) is a statistic (see ) that estimates σ ( q ¯ ) ii(sigma)(bar(q)), the standard deviation of the probability distribution of q ¯ bar(q), that is, the standard deviation of the distribution of the values of q ¯ bar(q) that would be obtained if the measurement were repeated an infinite number of times. The variance σ 2 [ s ( q ¯ ) ] ii(sigma)^2[s(bar(q))] of s ( q ¯ ) s(bar(q)) is given, approximately, byuncertaintystandard deviations as measures of

where ν = n − 1 ii(nu) = n - 1 is the degrees of freedomdegrees of freedom of s ( q ¯ ) s(bar(q)) (see ). Thus the relative standard deviation of s ( q ) s(q), which is given by the ratio σ [ s ( q ¯ ) ] σ ( q ¯ ) ii(sigma) [s(bar(q))]ii(sigma)(bar(q)) and which can be taken as a measure of the relative uncertainty of s ( q ¯ ) s(bar(q)), is approximately [ 2 ( n − 1 ) ] − 1 / 2 [2(n -1)]^{-1//2}. This “uncertainty of the uncertainty” of q ¯ bar(q), which arises from the purely statistical reason of limited samplinguncertainty due to limited sampling, can be surprisingly large; for n = 10 n = 10 observations it is 24 percent. This and other values are given in , which shows that the standard deviation of a statistically estimated standard deviation is not negligible for practical values of n n. One may therefore conclude that Type A evaluations of standard uncertainty are not necessarily more reliable than Type B evaluations, and that in many practical measurement situations where the number of observations is limited, the components obtained from Type B evaluations may be better known than the components obtained from Type A evaluations.

It has been argued that, whereas the uncertainties associated with the application of a particular method of measurement are statistical parameters characterizing random variables, there are instances of a “truly systematic effect”systematic effect whose uncertainty must be treated differently. An example is an offset having an unknown fixed value that is the same for every determination by the method due to a possible imperfection in the very principle of the method itself or one of its underlying assumptions. But if the possibility of such an offset is acknowledged to exist and its magnitude is believed to be possibly significant, then it can be described by a probability distribution, however simply constructed, based on the knowledge that led to the conclusion that it could exist and be significant. Thus, if one considers probability to be a measure of the degree of beliefdegree of belief that an event will occur, the contribution of such a systematic effectsystematic effect can be included in the combined standard uncertainty of a measurement result by evaluating it as a standard uncertainty of an a priori probability distribution and treating it in the same manner as any other standard uncertainty of an input quantity.uncertaintystandard deviations as measures of

example.p: The specification of a particular measurement procedure requires that a certain input quantity be calculated from a specific power-series expansion whose higher-order terms are inexactly known. The systematic effect due to not being able to treat these terms exactly leads to an unknown fixed offset that cannot be experimentally sampled by repetitions of the procedure. Thus the uncertainty associated with the effect cannot be evaluated and included in the uncertainty of the final measurement result if a frequency-based interpretation of probability is strictly followed. However, interpreting probability on the basis of degree of belief allows the uncertainty characterizing the effect to be evaluated from an a priori probability distribution (derived from the available knowledge concerning the inexactly known terms) and to be included in the calculation of the combined standard uncertainty of the measurement result like any other uncertainty.

clause.title: A comparison of two views of uncertaintyuncertaintycomparison of two views of

clause.p: The focus of this Guide is on the measurement result and its evaluated uncertainty rather than on the unknowable quantities “true” valuetrue value of a quantity and error (see ). By taking the operational views that the result of a measurement is simply the value attributed to the measurand and that the uncertainty of that result is a measure of the dispersion of the values that could reasonably be attributed to the measurand, this Guide in effect uncouples the often confusing connection between uncertainty and the unknowable quantities “true” value and error.error of measurement

This connection may be understood by interpreting the derivation of , the law of propagation of uncertainty, from the standpoint of “true” valuetrue value of a quantity and error. In this case, μ i ii(mu)_i is viewed as the unknown, unique “true” value of input quantity w i w_i and each w i w_i is assumed to be related to its “true” value μ i ii(mu)_i by w i = μ i + ε i w_i = ii(mu)_i + ii(epsilon)_i, where ε i ii(epsilon)_i is the error in w i w_i. The expectation of the probability distribution of each ε i ii(epsilon)_i is assumed to be zero, E ( ε i ) = 0 ii(E)(ii(epsilon)_i) = 0, with variance E ( ε i 2 ) = σ i 2 ii(E)(ii(epsilon)_i^2) = ii(sigma)_i^2. becomes then

It is assumed that probability is viewed as a measure of the degree of belief that an event will occur, implying that a systematic error may be treated in the same way as a random error and that ε i ii(epsilon)_i represents either kind.
In practice, the difference in point of view does not lead to a difference in the numerical value of the measurement result or of the uncertainty assigned to that result.

First, in both cases, the best available estimates of the input quantities w i w_i are used to obtain the best estimate of z z from the function f f; it makes no difference in the calculations if the best estimates are viewed as the values most likely to be attributed to the quantities in question or the best estimates of their “true” valuestrue value of a quantity.

Second, because

Again, it makes no difference in the calculations if a standard uncertainty is viewed as a measure of the dispersion of the probability distribution of an input quantity or as a measure of the dispersion of the probability distribution of the error of that quantity.
If the assumption of the had not been made, then the discussion of this subclause would not apply unless all of the estimates of the input quantities and the uncertainties of those estimates were obtained from the statistical analysis of repeated observations, that is, from Type A evaluations.

While the approach based on “true” valuetrue value of a quantity and error yields the same numerical results as the approach taken in this Guide (provided that the assumption of the is made), this Guide‘s concept of uncertainty eliminates the confusion between error and uncertaintyerror and uncertaintyconfusion between (see ). Indeed, this Guide‘s operational approach, wherein the focus is on the observed (or estimated) value of a quantity and the observed (or estimated) variability of that value, makes any mention of error entirely unnecessary.

annex.title: Practical guidance on evaluating uncertainty components
annex.p: This annex gives additional suggestions for evaluating uncertainty components, mainly of a practical nature, that are intended to complement the suggestions already given in .

clause.title: Components evaluated from repeated observations: Type A evaluation of standard uncertaintyobservationsrepeatedType A evaluation of uncertainty

clause.p: Uncertainties determined from repeated observations are often contrasted with those evaluated by other means as being “objective”, “statistically rigorous”, etc. That incorrectly implies that they can be evaluated merely by the application of statistical formulae to the observations and that their evaluation does not require the application of some judgement.observationsrepeatedType A evaluation of uncertainty

It must first be asked, “To what extent are the repeated observations completely independent repetitionsindependent repetitions of the measurement proceduremeasurement procedure?” If all of the observations are on a single sample, and if sampling is part of the measurement procedure because the measurand is the property of a material (as opposed to the property of a given specimen of the material), then the observations have not been independently repeated; an evaluation of a component of variance arising from possible differences among samples must be added to the observed variance of the repeated observations made on the single sample.observationsrepeatedType A evaluation of uncertainty

If zeroing an instrument is part of the measurement procedure, the instrument ought to be rezeroed as part of every repetition, even if there is negligible drift during the period in which observations are made, for there is potentially a statistically determinable uncertainty attributable to zeroing.

Similarly, if a barometer has to be read, it should in principle be read for each repetition of the measurement (preferably after disturbing it and allowing it to return to equilibrium), for there may be a variation both in indication and in reading, even if the barometric pressure is constant.

Second, it must be asked whether all of the influencesinfluence quantitiesrandom that are assumed to be random really are randomrandomness. Are the means and variances of their distributions constant, or is there perhaps a drift in the value of an unmeasured influence quantity during the period of repeated observations? If there is a sufficient number of observations, the arithmetic means of the results of the first and second halves of the period and their experimental standard deviations may be calculated and the two means compared with each other in order to judge whether the difference between them is statistically significant and thus if there is an effect varying with time.Type A evaluation of uncertainty

If the values of “common services” in the laboratory (electric-supply voltage and frequency, water pressure and temperature, nitrogen pressure, etc.) are influence quantities, there is normally a strongly nonrandom element in their variations that cannot be overlooked.influence quantitiesrandomrandomnessType A evaluation of uncertainty

If the least significant figure of a digital indication varies continually during an observation due to “noise”, it is sometimes difficult not to select unknowingly personally preferred values of that digit. It is better to arrange some means of freezing the indication at an arbitrary instant and recording the frozen result.randomness

clause.title: Correlations
clause.p: Much of the discussion in this subclause is also applicable to Type B evaluations of standard uncertainty.
The covariancecovariance associated with the estimates of two input quantities X i ii(X)_i and X j ii(X)_j may be taken to bezero or treated as insignificant if

li.p: X i ii(X)_i and X j ii(X)_j are uncorrelated (the random variablerandom variables, not the physical quantities that are assumed to be invariants — see ), for example, because they have been repeatedly but not simultaneously measured in different independent experiments or because they represent resultant quantities of different evaluations that have been made independently, or if
either of the quantities X i ii(X)_i or X j ii(X)_j can be treated as a constant, or if
there is insufficient information to evaluate the covariance associated with the estimates of X i ii(X)_i and X j ii(X)_j.

note.p: On the other hand, in certain cases, such as the reference-resistance of , it is apparent that the input quantities are fully correlated and that the standard uncertainties of their estimates combine linearly.
Different experiments may not be independent if, for example, the same instrument is used in each (see ).

clause.p: Whether or not two repeatedly and simultaneouslyobservationsindependent pairs of simultaneous observed input quantities are correlated maybe determined by means of in . For example, if the frequency of an oscillator uncompensated or poorly compensated for temperature is an input quantity, if ambient temperature is also an input quantity, and if they are observed simultaneously, there may be a significant correlationcorrelation revealed by the calculated covariancecovariance of the frequency of the oscillator and the ambient temperature.

In practice, input quantities are often correlated because the same physical measurement standard, measuring instrument, reference datum, or even measurement method having a significant uncertainty is used in the estimation of their values. Without loss of generality, suppose two input quantities X 1 ii(X)_1 and X 2 ii(X)_2 estimated by x 1 x_1 and x 2 x_2 depend on a set of uncorrelated variables Q 1 , Q 2 , … , Q L ii(Q)_1, ii(Q)_2,..., ii(Q)_L. Thus X 1 = F ( Q 1 , Q 2 , … , Q L ) ii(X)_1 = F(ii(Q)_1, ii(Q)_2,..., ii(Q)_L) and X 2 = G ( Q 1 , Q 2 , … , Q L ) ii(X)_2 = G(ii(Q)_1, ii(Q)_2,..., ii(Q)_L), although some of these variables may actually appear onlyin one function and not in the other. If u 2 ( q l ) u^2(q_l) is the estimated variance associated with the estimate q l q_l of Q l ii(Q)_l, then the estimated variance associated with x 1 x_1 is, from in ,
with a similar expression for u 2 ( x 2 ) u^2(x_2). The estimated covariance associated with x 1 x_1 and x 2 x_2 is given by

stem.asciimath: u(x_1,x_2) = sum_{l=1}^ii(L) {del ii(F)}/{del q_l} {del ii(G)}/{del q_l} u^2(q_l)

clause.p: Because only those terms for which ∂ F / ∂ q l ≠ 0 del ii(F)// del q_l != 0 and ∂ G / ∂ q l ≠ 0 del ii(G)// del q_l != 0 for a given l l contribute to the sum, the covariance is zero if no variable is common to both F ii(F) and G ii(G).

The estimated correlation coefficientcorrelation coefficient r ( x 1 , x 2 ) r(x_1, x_2) associated with the two estimates x 1 x_1 and x 2 x_2 is determined from u ( x 1 , x 2 ) u(x_1, x_2) [] and in , with u ( x 1 ) u(x_1) calculated from and u ( x 2 ) u(x_2) from asimilar expression. [See also .] It is also possible for the estimated covariance associated with two input estimates to have both a statistical component [see ] and a component arising as discussed in this subclause.

example.p: A standard resistor R S ii(R)_{"S"} is used in the same measurement to determine both a current I ii(I) and a temperature t t. The current is determined by measuring, with a digital voltmeter, the potential difference across the terminals of the standard; the temperature is determined by measuring, with a resistance bridge and the standard, the resistance R t ( t ) ii(R)_t(t) of a calibrated resistive temperature sensor whose temperature-resistance relation in the range 15 ⁢ °C ≤ t ≤ 30 ⁢ °C 15 "unitsml(degC)" <= t <= 30 "unitsml(degC)" is t = a R t 2 ( t ) − t 0 t = a ii(R)_t^2 (t) - t_0, where a a and t 0 t_0 are known constants. Thus the current is determined through the relation I = V S / R S ii(I) = ii(V)_{"S"}//ii(R)_{"S"} and the temperature through the relation t = a β 2 ( t ) R S 2 − t 0 t = a ii(beta)^2(t) ii(R)_{"S"}^2 - t_0, where β ( t ) ii(beta)(t) is the measured ratio R t ( t ) / R S ii(R)_t(t)//ii(R)_{"S"} provided by the bridge.
Since only the quantity R S ii(R)_{"S"} is common to the expression for I ii(I) and t t, yields for the covariance of I ii(I) and t t
(For simplicity of notation, in this example the same symbol is used for both the input quantity and its estimate.)

To obtain the numerical value of the covariancecovariance, one substitutes into this expression the numerical values of the measured quantities I ii(I) and t t, and the values of R S ii(R)_{"S"} and u ( R S ) u(ii(R)_{"S"}) given in the standard resistor’s calibration certificate. The unit of u ( I , t ) u(ii(I),t) is clearly A ⋅ ⁢ °C ii(A) * "unitsml(degC)" since the dimension of the relative variance [ u ( R S ) / R S ] 2 [u(ii(R)_{"S"})//ii(R)_{"S"}]^2 is one (that is, the latter is a so-called dimensionless quantity).

Further, let a quantity P ii(P) be related to the input quantities I ii(I) and t t by P = C 0 I 2 / ( T 0 + t ) ii(P) = ii(C)_0 I^2//(ii(T)_0 + t), where C 0 ii(C)_0 and T 0 ii(T)_0 are known constants with negligible uncertainties [ u 2 ( C 0 ) ≈ 0 , u 2 ( T 0 ) ≈ 0 u^2(ii(C)_0) ~~ 0, u^2(ii(T)_0) ~~ 0]. in then yields for the variance of P ii(P) in terms of the variances of I ii(I) and t t and their covariance

The variances u 2 ( I ) u^2(I) and u 2 ( t ) u^2(t) are obtained by the application of of to the relations I = V S / R S ii(I) = ii(V)_{"S"}//ii(R)_{"S"} and t = a β ( t ) R S − t 0 t = a ii(beta)(t) ii(R)_"S" − t_0. The results are

where for simplicity it is assumed that the uncertainties of the constants t 0 t_0 and a a are also negligible. These expressions can be readily evaluated since u 2 ( V S ) u^2(ii(V)_{"S"}) and u 2 ( β ) u^2(ii(beta)) may be determined, respectively, from the repeated readings of the voltmeter and of the resistance bridge. Of course, any uncertainties inherent in the instruments themselves and in the measurement procedures employed must also be taken into account when u 2 ( V S ) u^2(ii(V)_{"S"}) and u 2 ( β ) u^2(ii(beta)) are determined.

In the of , let the calibration of each resistor be represented by R i = α i R S ii(R)_i = ii(alpha)_i ii(R)_{"S"}, with u ( α i ) u(ii(alpha)_i) the standard uncertainty of the measured ratio α i ii(alpha)_i as obtained from repeated observations. Further, let α i ≈ 1 ii(alpha)_i ~~ 1 for eachresistor, and let u ( α i ) u(ii(alpha)_i) be essentially the same for each calibration so that u ( α i ) ≈ u ( α ) u(ii(alpha)_i) ~~ u(ii(alpha)). Then and yield u 2 ( R i ) = R S 2 u 2 ( α ) + u 2 ( R S ) u ^2(ii(R)_i) = ii(R)_{"S"}^2 u^2(ii(alpha)) + u^2(ii(R)_{"S"}) and u ( R i , R j ) = u 2 ( R S ) u(ii(R)_i, ii(R)_j) = u^2(ii(R)_{"S"}). This implies through in that the correlation coefficientcorrelation coefficient of any two resistors ( i ≠ j i != j) iscovariance

note.p: In general, in comparison calibrations such as this example, the estimated values of the calibrated items are correlated, with the degree of correlationcorrelation depending upon the ratio of the uncertainty of the comparison to the uncertainty of the reference standard. When, as often occurs in practice, the uncertainty of the comparison is negligible with respect to the uncertainty of the standard, the correlation coefficientcorrelation coefficients are equal to +1+1 and the uncertainty of each calibrated item is the same as that of the standard.calibrationcomparison

clause.p: The need to introduce the covariancecovariance u ( x i , x j ) u(x_i,x_j) can be bypassed if the original set of input quantities X 1 , X 2 , … , X N ii(X)_1, ii(X)_2,...,ii(X)_N upon which the measurand Y ii(Y) depends [see in ] is redefined in such a way as to include as additional independent input quantities those quantities Q l ii(Q)_l that are common to two or more of the original X i ii(X)_i. (It may be necessary to perform additional measurements to establish fully the relationship between Q l ii(Q)_l and the affected X i ii(X)_i.) Nonetheless, in some situations it may be more convenient to retain covariances rather than to increase the number of input quantities. A similar process can be carried out on the observed covariances of simultaneous repeated observations [see in ], but the identification of the appropriate additional input quantities is often ad hoc and nonphysical.correlationelimination of

example.p: If, in of , the expressions for I ii(I) and t t in terms of R S ii(R)_{"S"} are introduced into the expression for P ii(P), the result is

and the correlationcorrelation between I ii(I) and t t is avoided at the expense of replacing the input quantities I ii(I) and t t with the quantities V S ii(V)_{"S"}, R S ii(R)_{"S"}, and β ii(beta). Since these quantities are uncorrelated, the variance of P ii(P) can be obtained from in .

clause.title: Components evaluated by other means: Type B evaluation of standard uncertaintyType B evaluation of uncertainty
The need for Type B evaluationsType B evaluationsneed for
clause.p: If a measurement laboratory had limitless time and resources, it could conduct an exhaustive statistical investigation of every conceivable cause of uncertainty, for example, by using many different makes and kinds of instruments, different methods of measurement, different applications of the method, and different approximations in its theoretical models of the measurement. The uncertainties associated with all of these causes could then be evaluated by the statistical analysis of series of observations and the uncertainty of each cause would be characterized by a statistically evaluated standard deviation. In other words, all of the uncertainty components would be obtained from Type A evaluations. Since such an investigation is not an economic practicality, many uncertainty components must be evaluated by whatever other means is practical.uncertaintystatistical evaluation ofby varying input quantities

clause.title: The resolution of a digital indication
clause.p: One source of uncertainty of a digital instrumentuncertainty due to resolution of a digital indication is the resolution of its indicating device. For example, even if the repeated indications were all identical, the uncertainty of the measurement attributable to repeatability would not be zero, for there is a range of input signals to the instrument spanning a known interval that would give the same indication. If the resolution of the indicating device is δ x ii(delta) x, the value of the stimulus that produces a given indication X X can lie with equal probability anywhere in the interval X − δ x / 2 ii(X) - ii(delta) x//2 to X + δ x / 2 X + ii(delta) x//2. The stimulus is thus described by a rectangular probability distributiondistributionrectangular (see and ) of width δ x ii(delta) x with variance u 2 = ( δ x ) 2 / 12 u^2 = (ii(delta) x)^2//12, implying a standard uncertainty of u = 0.29 δ x u = 0.29 ii(delta) x for any indication.

Thus a weighing instrument with an indicating device whose smallest significant digit is 1 ⁢ g 1 "unitsml(g)" has a variance due to the resolution of the device of u 2 = ( 1 / 12 ) unitsml(g)^2 u^2 = (1//12) "unitsml(g)^2" and a standard uncertainty of u = ( 1 / 12 ) ⁢ g = 0.29 ⁢ g u = (1//sqrt(12)) "unitsml(g)" = 0.29 "unitsml(g)".

clause.title: Hysteresis
clause.p: Certain kinds of hysteresisuncertainty due to hysteresis can cause a similar kind of uncertainty. The indication of an instrument may differ by a fixed and known amount according to whether successive readings are rising or falling. The prudent operator takes note of the direction of successive readings and makes the appropriate correction. But the direction of the hysteresis is not always observable: there may be hidden oscillations within the instrument about an equilibrium point so that the indication depends on the direction from which that point is finally approached. If the range of possible readings from that cause is δ x ii(delta) x, the variance is again u 2 = ( δ x ) 2 / 12 u^2 = (ii(delta) x)^2//12, and the standard uncertainty due to hysteresis is u = 0.29 δ x u = 0.29 ii(delta) x.

clause.title: Finite-precision arithmetic
clause.p:
The rounding or truncation of numbers arising in automated data reduction by computer can also be a source of uncertainty. Consider, for example, a computer with a word length of 16 bits. If, in the course of computation, a number having this word length is subtracted from another from which it differs only in the 16th bit, only one significant bit remains. Such events can occur in the evaluation of “ill-conditioned” algorithms, and they can be difficult to predict. One may obtain an empirical determination of the uncertainty by increasing the most important input quantity to the calculation (there is frequently one that is proportional to the magnitude of the output quantity) by small increments until the output quantity changes; the smallest change in the output quantity that can be obtained by such means may be taken as a measure of the uncertainty; if it is
δ

One may check the uncertainty evaluation by comparing the result of the computation carried out on the limited word-length machine with the result of the same computation carried out on a machine with a significantly larger word length.

clause.title: Imported input valuesimported input value or quantity

clause.p:
An imported value for an input quantityimported input value or quantity is one that has not been estimated in the course of a given measurement but has been obtained elsewhere as the result of an independent evaluation. Frequently such an imported value is accompanied by some kind of statement about its uncertainty. For example, the uncertainty may be given as a standard deviation, a multiple of a standard deviation, or the half-width of an interval having a stated level of confidence. Alternatively, upper and lower bounds may be given, or no information may be provided about the uncertainty. In the latter case, those who use the value must employ their own knowledge about the likely magnitude of the uncertainty, given the nature of the quantity, the reliability of the source, the uncertainties obtained in practice for such quantities, etc.
The discussion of the uncertainty of imported input quantities is included in this subclause on Type B evaluation of standard uncertainty for convenience; the uncertainty of such a quantity could be composed of components obtained from Type A evaluations or components obtained from both Type A and Type B evaluations. Since it is unnecessary to distinguish between components evaluated by the two different methods in order to calculate a combined standard uncertainty, it is unnecessary to know the composition of the uncertainty of an imported quantity.distributionrectangular

Some calibration laboratories have adopted the practice of expressing “uncertainty” in the form of upper and lower limits that define an interval having a “minimum” level of confidencelevel of confidenceminimum, for example, “at least” 95 percent. This may be viewed as an example of a so-called “safe” uncertainty (see ), and it cannot be converted to a standard uncertainty without a knowledge of how it was calculated. If sufficient information is given, it may be recalculated in accordance with the rules of this Guide; otherwise an independent assessment of the uncertainty must be made by whatever means are available.uncertaintysafe

Some uncertainties are given simply as maximum boundsbounds on an input quantity within which all values of the quantity are said to lie. It is a common practice to assume that all values within those bounds are equally probable (a rectangular probability distribution)distributionrectangular, but such a distribution should not be assumed if there is reason to expect that values within but close to the bounds are less likely than those nearer the centre of the bounds. A rectangular distribution of half-width a a has a variance of a 2 / 3 a^2//3; a normal distributionnormal distribution for which a a is the half -width of an interval having a level of confidence of 99.7399.73 percent has a variance of a 2 / 9 a^2//9. It may be prudent to adopt a compromise between those values, for example, by assuming a triangular distributiondistributiontriangular for which the variance is a 2 / 6 a^2//6 (see and ).

clause.title: Single observation, calibrated instrumentsuncertainty of a single observation of a calibrated instrument
clause.p: If an input estimate has been obtained from a single observation with a particular instrument that has been calibrated against a standard of small uncertainty, the uncertainty of the estimate is mainly one of repeatability. The variance of repeated measurements by the instrument may have been obtained on an earlier occasion, not necessarily at precisely the same value of the reading but near enough to be useful, and it may be possible to assume the variance to be applicable to the input value in question. If no such information is available, an estimate must be made based on the nature of the measuring apparatus or instrument, the known variances of other instruments of similar construction, etc.

clause.title: Single observation, verified instrumentsuncertainty of a single observation of a verified instrument
clause.p: Not all measuring instruments are accompanied by a calibration certificate or a calibration curvecalibration curve. Most instruments, however, are constructed to a written standard and verified, either by the manufacturer or by an independent authority, to conform to that standard. Usually the standard contains metrological requirements, often in the form of “maximum permissible errors”, to which the instrument is required to conform. The compliance of the instrument with these requirements is determined by comparison with a reference instrument whose maximum allowed uncertainty is usually specified in the standard. This uncertainty is then a component of the uncertainty of the verified instrument.error curve of a verified instrumentuncertaintymaximum allowed

If nothing is known about the characteristic error curve of the verified instrument it must be assumed that there is an equal probability that the error has any value within the permitted limits, that is, a rectangular probability distribution. However, certain types of instruments have characteristic curves such that the errors are, for example, likely always to be positive in part of the measuring range and negative in other parts. Sometimes such information can be deduced from a study of the written standard.errormaximum permissible

clause.title: Controlled quantitiesuncertainty of a controlled quantity
clause.p: Measurements are frequently made under controlledquantitycontrolled reference conditions that are assumed to remain constant during the course of a series of measurements. For example, measurements may be performed on specimens in a stirred oil bath whose temperature is controlled by a thermostat. The temperature of the bath may be measured at the time of each measurement on a specimen, but if the temperature of the bath is cycling, the instantaneous temperature of the specimen may not be the temperature indicated by the thermometer in the bath. The calculation of the temperature fluctuations of the specimen based on heat-transfer theory, and of their variance, is beyond the scope of this Guide, but it must start from a known or assumed temperature cycle for the bath. That cycle may be observed by a fine thermocouple and a temperature recorder, but failing that, an approximation of it may be deduced from a knowledge of the nature of the controls.

clause.title: Asymmetric distributions of possible valuesdistributionasymmetric
clause.p: There are occasions when all possible values of a quantity lie to one side of a single limiting value. For example, when measuring the fixed vertical height h h (the measurand) of a column of liquid in a manometer, the axis of the height-measuring device may deviate from verticality by a small angle β ii(beta). The distance l l determined by the device will always be larger than h h; no values less than h h are possible. This is because h h is equal to the projection l cos β l cos ii(beta), implying l = h / cos β l = h//cos ii(beta), and all values of cos β cos ii(beta) are less than one; no values greater than one are possible. This so-called “cosine error” can also occur in such a way that the projection h ′ cos β h' cos ii(beta) of a measurand h ′ h' is equal to the observed distance l l, that is, l = h ′ cos β l = h' cos ii(beta), and the observed distance is always less than the measurand.

If a new variable δ = 1 − cos β ii(delta) = 1 - cos ii(beta) is introduced, the two different situations are, assuming β ≈ 0 ii(beta) ~~ 0 or δ << 1 ii(delta) " << " 1 as is usually the case in practice,

Here l l, the best estimate of l l, is the arithmetic mean or average of n n independent repeated observations l k l_k of l l with estimated variance u 2 ( l ¯ ) u^2(bar(l)) [see and in ]. Thus it follows from and that to obtain an estimate of h h or h ′ h' requires an estimate of the correction factor δ ii(delta), while to obtain the combined standard uncertainty of the estimate of h h or h ′ h' requires u 2 ( δ ) u^2(ii(delta)), the estimated variance of δ ii(delta). More specifically, application of in to and yields for u c 2 ( h ) u_{"c"}^2(h) and u c 2 ( h ′ ) u_{"c"}^2(h') ( − - and + + signs, respectively)

To obtain estimates of the expected value of δ ii(delta) and the variance of δ ii(delta), assume that the axis of the device used to measure the height of the column of liquid in the manometer is constrained to be fixed in a vertical plane and that the distribution of the values of the angle of inclination β ii(beta) about its expected value of zero is a normal distribution with variance σ 2 ii(sigma)^2. Although β ii(beta) can have both positive and negative values, δ = 1 − cos β ii(delta) = 1 - cos ii(beta) is positive for all values of β ii(beta). If the misalignment of the axis of the device is assumed to be unconstrained, the orientation of the axis can vary over a solid angle since it is capable of misalignment in azimuth as well, but β ii(beta) is then always a positive angle.

In the constrained or one-dimensional case, the probability elementprobability element p ( β ) d β p(ii(beta)) d ii(beta) () is proportional to { exp [ − β 2 / ( 2 σ 2 ) ] } d β {exp[-ii(beta)^2//(2ii(sigma)^2)]}dii(beta); in the unconstrained or two-dimensional case, the probability element is proportional to { exp [ − β 2 2 σ 2 ] } sin β d β {exp[-ii(beta)^2/(2ii(sigma)^2)]}sin ii(beta) dii(beta). The probability density functionprobability density functions p ( δ ) p(ii(delta)) in the two cases are the expressions required to determine the expectation and variance of δ ii(delta) for use in and . They may readily be obtained from these probability elements because the angle β ii(beta) may be assumed small, and hence δ = 1 − cos β ii(delta) = 1 - cos ii(beta) and sin β sin ii(beta) may be expanded to lowest order in β ii(beta). This yields δ ≈ β 2 / 2 ii(delta) ~~ ii(beta)^2//2, sin β ≈ β = 2 δ sin ii(beta) ~~ ii(beta) = sqrt(2 ii(delta)), and d β = d δ / 2 δ d ii(beta) = d ii(delta) // sqrt(2ii(delta)). This is a situation where the expansion of the function Y = f ( X 1 , X 2 , … , X N ) ii(Y) = f(ii(X)_1, ii(X)_2,..., ii(X)_N) in a first-order Taylor series to obtain u c 2 ( y ) u_{"c"}^2(y), in , is inadequate because of the nonlinearity of f f: cos β ¯ ≠ cos β ¯ bar(cos ii(beta)) != cos bar(ii(beta)) (see , and ). Although the analysis can be carried out entirely in terms of β ii(beta), introducing the variable δ ii(delta) simplifies the problem.functional relationshiplinearization of afunctional relationshipnonlinear

Another example of a situation where all possible values of a quantity lie to one side of a single limiting value is the determination by titration of the concentration of a component in a solution where the end point is indicated by the triggering of a signal; the amount of reagent added is always more than that necessary to trigger the signal; it is never less. The excess titrated beyond the limit point is a required variable in the data reduction, and the procedure in this (and in similar) cases is to assume an appropriate probability distribution for the excess and to use it to obtain the expected value of the excess and its variance.

example.p: If a rectangular distribution of lower bound zero and upper bound C 0 ii(C)_0 is assumed for the excess z z, then the expected value of the excess is C 0 / 2 ii(C)_0//2 with associated variance C 0 2 / 12 ii(C)_0^2//12. If the probability density function of the excess is taken as that of a normal distribution with 0 ≤ z < ∞ 0 <= z < oo, that is, p ( z ) = ( σ π / 2 ) − 1 exp [ − z 2 / ( 2 σ 2 ) ] p(z) = (ii(sigma) sqrt(pi//2))^{-1} exp[-z^2//(2 ii(sigma)^2)], then the expected value is σ 2 / π ii(sigma) sqrt(2//pi) with variance σ 2 ( 1 − 2 / π ) ii(sigma)^2(1 - 2//pi).

clause.title: Uncertainty when corrections from a calibration curvecalibration curve are not applieduncertainty when a correction is not applied
clause.p: The discusses the case where a known correction b b for a significant systematic effect is not applied to the reported result of a measurement but instead is taken into account by enlarging the “uncertainty” assigned to the result. An example is replacement of an expanded uncertainty U ii(U) with U + b ii(U) + b, where U ii(U) is an expanded uncertainty obtained under the assumption b = 0 b = 0. This practice is sometimes followed in situations where all of the following conditions apply: the measurand Y ii(Y) is defined over a range of values of a parameter t t, as in the case of a calibration curve for a temperature sensor; U ii(U) and b b also depend on t t; and only a single value of “uncertainty” is to be given for all estimates y ( t ) y(t) of the measurand over the range of possible values of t t. In such situations the result of the measurement is often reported as Y ( t ) = y ( t ) ± [ U max + b max ] ii(Y)(t) = y(t) pm [ii(U)_{"max"} + b_{"max"}], where the subscript “max” indicates that the maximum value of U ii(U) and the maximum value of the known correction b b over the range of values of t t are used.

Although this Guide recommends that corrections be applied to measurement results for known significant systematic effects, this may not always be feasible in such a situation because of the unacceptable expense that would be incurred in calculating and applying an individual correction, and in calculating and using an individual uncertainty, for each value of y ( t ) y(t).
A comparatively simple approach to this problem that is consistent with the principles of this Guide is as follows:
Compute a single mean correction b ¯ bar(b) from

where t 1 t_1 and t 2 t_2 define the range of interest of the parameter t t, and take the best estimate of Y ( t ) Y(t) to be y ′ ( t ) = y ( t ) + b ¯ y'(t) = y(t) + bar(b), where y ( t ) y(t) is the best uncorrected estimate of Y ¯ ( t ) bar(Y)(t). The variance associated with the mean correction b ¯ bar(b) over the range of interest is given by

where u 2 [ y ( t ) ] u^2[y(t)] is the variance of y ( t ) y(t) due to all uncertainty sources other than b ( t ) b(t). The single value of standard uncertainty to be used for all estimates y ′ ( t ) = y ( t ) + b ¯ y'(t) = y(t) + bar(b) of the measurand Y ( t ) ii(Y)(t) is then the positive square root of

An expanded uncertainty U ii(U) may be obtained by multiplying u c ( y ′ ) u_{"c"}(y') by an appropriately chosen coverage factor k k, U = k u c ( y ′ ) ii(U) = k u_{"c"}(y'), yielding Y ( t ) = y ′ ( t ) ± U = y ( t ) + b ¯ ± U ii(Y)(t) = y'(t) pm ii(U) = y(t) + bar(b) pm ii(U). However, the use of the same average correction for allvalues of t t rather than the correction appropriate for each value of t t must be recognized and a clear statement given as to what U ii(U) represents.

clause.title: Uncertainty of the method of measurementuncertainty of the method of measurement

clause.p:
Perhaps the most difficult uncertainty component to evaluate is that associated with the method of measurement, especially if the application of that method has been shown to give results with less variability than those of any other that is known. But it is likely that there are other methods, some of them as yet unknown or in some way impractical, that would give systematically different results of apparently equal validity. This implies an a priori probability distribution, not a distribution from which samples can be readily drawn and treated statistically. Thus, even though the uncertainty of the method may be the dominant one, the only information often available for evaluating its standard uncertainty is one’s existing knowledge of the physical world. (See also .)uncertainty of the method of measurement
Determining the same measurand by different methods, either in the same laboratory or in different laboratories, or by the same method in different laboratories, can often provide valuable information about the uncertainty attributable to a particular method. In general, the exchange of measurement standards or reference materials between laboratories for independent measurement is a useful way of assessing the reliability of evaluations of uncertainty and of identifying previously unrecognized systematic effects.

clause.title: Uncertainty of the sampleuncertainty of the sample

clause.p: Many measurements involve comparing an unknown object with a known standard having similar characteristics in order to calibrate the unknown. Examples include end gauges, certain thermometers, sets of masses, resistors, and high purity materials. In most such cases, the measurement methods are not especially sensitive to, or adversely affected by, sample selection (that is, the particular unknown being calibrated), sample treatment, or the effects of various environmental influence quantities because the unknown and standard respond in generally the same (and often predictable) way to such variables.

In some practical measurement situations, sampling and specimen treatment play a much larger role. This is often the case for the chemical analysis of natural materials. Unlike man-made materials, which may have proven homogeneity to a level beyond that required for the measurement, natural materials are often very inhomogeneous. This inhomogeneity leads to two additional uncertainty components. Evaluation of the first requires determining how adequately the sample selected represents the parent material being analysed. Evaluation of the second requires determining the extent to which the secondary (unanalysed) constituents influence the measurement and how adequately they are treated by the measurement method.

In some cases, careful design of the experiment may make it possible to evaluate statistically the uncertainty due to the sample (see and ). Usually, however, especially when the effects of environmental influence quantities on the sample are significant, the skill and knowledge of the analyst derived from experience and all of the currently available information are required for evaluating the uncertainty.

annex.title: Degrees of freedom and levels of confidencedegrees of freedomlevel of confidence

clause.p: This annex addresses the general question of obtaining from the estimate y y of the measurand Y ii(Y), and from the combined standard uncertainty u c ( y ) u_{"c"}(y) of that estimate, an expanded uncertaintyexpanded uncertainty U p = k p u c ( y ) ii(U)_p = k_p u_{"c"}(y) that defines an interval y − U p ≤ Y ≤ y + U p y - ii(U)_p <= ii(Y) <= y + ii(U)_p that has a high, specified coverage probabilitycoverage probability or level of confidence p p. It thus deals with the issue of determining the coverage factor k p k_p that produces an interval about the measurement result y y that may be expected to encompass a large, specified fraction p p of the distribution of values that could reasonably be attributed to the measurand Y ii(Y) (see ).level of confidence

In most practical measurement situations, the calculation of intervals having specified levels of confidence — indeed, the estimation of most individual uncertainty components in such situations — is at best only approximate. Even the experimental standard deviation of the mean of as many as 30 repeated observations of a quantity described by a normal distribution has itself an uncertainty of about 13 percent (see in ).

In most cases, it does not make sense to try to distinguish between, for example, an interval having a level of confidencelevel of confidence of 95 percent (one chance in 20 that the value of the measurand Y ii(Y) lies outside the interval) and either a 94 percent or 96 percent interval (1 chance in 17 and 25, respectively). Obtaining justifiable intervals with levels of confidence of 99 percent (1 chance in 100) and higher is especially difficult, even if it is assumed that no systematic effects have been overlooked, because so little information is generally available about the most extreme portions or “tails” of the probability distributions of the input quantities.

To obtain the value of the coverage factorcoverage factor k p k_p that produces an interval corresponding to a specified level of confidencelevel of confidence p p requires detailed knowledge of the probability distribution characterized by the measurement result and its combined standard uncertainty. For example, for a quantity z z described by a normal distributionnormal distribution with expectation μ z ii(mu)_z and standard deviation σ ii(sigma), the value of k p k_p that produces an interval μ z ± k p σ ii(mu)_z pm k_p ii(sigma) that encompasses the fraction p p of the distribution, and thus has a coverage probabilitycoverage probability or level ofconfidence p p, can be readily calculated. Some examples are given in .

note.p: By contrast, if z z is described by a rectangular probability distribution with expectation μ z ii(mu)_z and standard deviation σ = a / 3 ii(sigma) = a//sqrt(3), where a a is the half-width of the distribution, the level of confidence p p is 57.7457.74 percent for k p = 1 k_p = 1; 95 percent for k p = 1.65 k_p = 1.65; 99 percent for k p = 1.71 k_p = 1.71; and 100 percent for k p ≥ 3 ≈ 1.73 k_p >= sqrt(3) ~~ 1.73; the rectangular distribution is “narrower” than the normal distribution in the sense that it is of finite extent and has no “tails”.

clause.p: If the probability distributionprobability distributions of the input quantities X 1 , X 2 , … , X N ii(X)_1,ii(X)_2,...,ii(X)_N upon which the measurand Y ii(Y) depends are known [their expectations, variances, and higher moments (see and ) if the distributions are not normal distributionnormal distributions], and if Y ii(Y) is a linear function of the input quantities, Y = c 1 X 1 + c 2 X 2 … c N X N ii(Y) = c_1 ii(X)_1 + c_2 ii(X)_2 ... c_N ii(X)_N, then the probability distributionprobability distribution of Y ii(Y) may be obtained by convolving the individual probability distributionsprobability distributions, convolving . Values of k p k_p that produce intervals corresponding to specified levels of confidence p p may then be calculated from the resulting convolved distribution.

If the functional relationship between Y ii(Y) and its input quantities is nonlinearfunctional relationshipnonlinear and a first-order Taylor seriesTaylor series expansion of the relationship is not an acceptable approximation (see and ), then the probability distributionprobability distribution of Y ii(Y) cannot be obtained by convolving the distributionsprobability distributions, convolving of the input quantities. In such cases, other analytical or numerical methods are required.

In practice, because the parameters characterizing the probability distributions of input quantities are usually estimates, because it is unrealistic to expect that the level of confidence to be associated with a given interval can be known with a great deal of exactness, and because of the complexity of convolving probability distributionsprobability distributions, convolving, such convolutions are rarely, if ever, implemented when intervals having specified levels of confidence need to be calculated. Instead, approximations are used that take advantage of the Central Limit TheoremCentral Limit Theorem.

If Y = c 1 X 1 + c 2 X 2 + … + c N X N = ∑ i = 1 N c i X i ii(Y) = c_1 ii(X)_1 + c_2 ii(X)_2 + ... + c_N ii(X)_N = sum_{i=1}^ii(N) c_i ii(X)_i and all the X i ii(X)_i are characterized by normal distributionnormal distributions, then the resulting convolved distribution of Y ii(Y) will also be normal. However, even if the distributions of the X i ii(X)_i are not normal, the distribution of Y ii(Y) may often be approximated by a normal distributionnormal distribution because of the Central Limit Theorem Central Limit Theorem. This theorem states that the distribution of Y ii(Y) will be approximately normal with expectation E ( Y ) = ∑ i = 1 N c i E ( X i ) ii(E)(ii(Y))= sum_{i=1}^ii(N) c_i E(ii(X)_i) and variance σ 2 ( Y ) = ∑ i = 1 N c i 2 σ 2 ( X i ) ii(sigma)^2 (Y) = sum_{i=1}^ii(N) c_i^2 ii(sigma)^2(ii(X)_i), where E ( X i ) ii(E)(ii(X)_i) is the expectation of X i ii(X)_i and σ 2 ( X i ) ii(sigma)^2(ii(X)_i) is the variance of X i ii(X)_i, if the X i ii(X)_i are independent and σ 2 ( Y ) ii(sigma)^2(ii(Y)) is much larger than any single component c i 2 σ 2 ( X ¯ i ) c_i^2 ii(sigma)^2(bar(ii(X))_i) from a non-normally distributed X i ii(X)_i.

The Central Limit TheoremCentral Limit Theorem is significant because it shows the very important role played by the variances of the probability distributions of the input quantities, compared with that played by the higher moments of the distributions, in determining the form of the resulting convolved distribution of Y ii(Y). Further, it implies that the convolved distribution converges towards the normal distributionnormal distribution as the number of input quantities contribut ing to σ 2 ( Y ) ii(sigma)^2(ii(Y)) increases; that the convergence will be more rapid the closer the values of c i 2 σ 2 ( X ¯ i ) c_i^2 ii(sigma)^2(bar(X)_i) are to each other (equivalent in practice to each input estimate x i x_i contribut ing a comparableuncertainty to the uncertainty of the estimate y y of the measurand Y ii(Y)); and that the closer the distributions of the X i ii(X)_i are to being normal, the fewer X i ii(X)_i are required to yield a normal distributionnormal distribution for Y ii(Y).

example.p: The rectangular distribution (see and ) is an extreme example of a non-normal distributionnormal distribution, but the convolution of even as few as three such distributions of equal width is approximately normal. If the half-width of each of the three rectangular distributions is a a so that the variance of each is a 2 / 3 a^2//3, the variance of the convolved distribution is σ 2 = a 2 ii(sigma)^2 = a^2. The 95 percent and 99 percent intervals of the convolved distribution are defined by 1.937 σ 1.937 ii(sigma) and 2.379 σ 2.379 ii(sigma), respectively, while the corresponding intervals for a normal distributionnormal distribution with the same standard deviation σ ii(sigma) are defined by 1.960 σ 1.960 ii(sigma) and 2.576 σ 2.576 ii(sigma) (see ) .probability distributions, convolving

note.p: For every interval with a level of confidence p p greater than about 91.791.7 percent, the value of k p k_p for a normal distributionnormal distribution is larger than the corresponding value for the distribution resulting from the convolution of any number and size of rectangular distributionsdistributionrectangular.

It follows from the Central Limit TheoremCentral Limit Theorem that the probability distribution of the arithmetic mean q ¯ bar(q) of n n observations q k q_k of a random variable q q with expectation μ q ii(mu)_q and finite standard deviation σ ii(sigma) approaches a normal distributionnormal distribution with mean μ q ii(mu)_q and standard deviation σ / n ii(sigma)//sqrt(n) as n → ∞ n -> oo, whatever may be the probability distribution of q q.

clause.p: A practical consequence of the Central Limit TheoremCentral Limit Theorem is that when it can be established that its requirements are approximately met, in particular, if the combined standard uncertainty u c ( y ) u_{"c"}(y) is not dominated by a standard uncertainty component obtained from a Type A evaluation based on just a few observations, or by a standard uncertainty component obtained from a Type B evaluation based on an assumed rectangular distribution, a reasonable first approximation to calculating an expanded uncertaintyexpanded uncertainty U p = k p u c ( y ) ii(U)_p = k_p u_{"c"}(y) that provides an interval with level of confidencelevel of confidence p p is to use for k p k_p a value from the normal distributionnormal distribution. The values most commonly used for this purpose are given in .coverage factor

The degrees of freedomdegrees of freedom ν ii(nu) is equal to n − 1 n-1 for a single quantity estimated by the arithmetic mean of n n independent observations, as in . If n n independent observations are used to determine both the slopeand intercept of a straight line by the method of least squaresleast squaresmethod of, the degrees of freedom of their respective standard uncertainties is ν = n − 2 ii(nu) = n - 2. For a least-squares fit of m m parameters to n n data points, the degrees of freedom of the standard uncertainty of each parameter is ν = n − m ii(nu) = n-m. (See Reference for a further discussion of degrees of freedom.)degrees of freedom of a Type A standard uncertainty

example.p: Consider that Y = f ( X 1 , X 2 , X 3 ) = b X 1 X 2 X 3 ii(Y) = f (ii(X)_1, ii(X)_2, ii(X)_3) = b ii(X)_1 ii(X)_2 ii(X)_3 and that the estimates x 1 x_1, x 2 x_2, x 3 x_3 of the normally distributed input quantities X 1 ii(X)_1, X 2 ii(X)_2, X 3 ii(X)_3 are the arithmetic means of x 1 = 10 x_1 = 10, x 2 = 5 x_2 = 5, and x 3 = 15 x_3 = 15 independent repeated observations, respectively, with relative standard uncertainties u ( x 1 ) / x 1 = 0.25 u(x_1)//x_1 = 0.25 percent, u ( x 2 ) / x 2 = 0.57 u(x_2)//x_2= 0.57 percent, and u ( x 3 ) / x 3 = 0.82 u(x_3)//x_3 = 0.82 percent. In this case, c i = ∂ f / ∂ X i = Y / X i c_i = del f // del ii(X)_i = Y // ii(X)_i (to be evaluated at x 1 x_1, x 2 x_2, x 3 x_3 — see , ), [ u c ( y ) / y ] 2 = ∑ i = 1 3 [ u ( x i ) / x i ] 2 = ( 1.03 p e r c e n t ) 2 [u_{"c"}(y)//y]^2 = sum_{i=1}^3 [u(x_i)//x_i]^2 = (1.03" "rm(percent))^2 (see ), and becomes

It may then be stated that Y = y ± U 95 = y ( 1 ± 0.022 ) ii(Y) = y pm ii(U)_{95} = y(1 pm 0.022) ( y y to be determined from y = b x 1 x 2 x 3 y = b x_1 x_2 x_3), or that 0.978 y ≤ Y ≤ 1.022 y 0.978 y <= ii(Y) <= 1.022 y, and that the level of confidence to be associated with the interval is approximately 95 percent.

clause.p: In practice, u c ( y ) u_{"c"}(y) depends on standard uncertainties u ( x i ) u(x_i) of input estimates of both normally and non-normally distributed input quantities, and the u ( x i ) u(x_i) are obtained from both frequency-based and a priori probability distributionsdistributiona priori (that is, from both Type A and Type B evaluations). A similar statement applies to the estimate y y and input estimates x i x_i upon which y y depends. Nevertheless, the probability distribution of the function t = ( y − Y ) / u c ( y ) t = (y - Y)//u_{"c"}(y) can be approximated by the t t-distribution t t-distribution if it is expanded in a Taylor seriesTaylor series about its expectation. In essence, this is what is achieved, in the lowest order approximation, by the Welch-Satterthwaite formulaWelch-Satterthwaite formula, or .

The question arises as to the degrees of freedom to assign to a standard uncertainty obtained from a Type B evaluation when ν eff ii(nu)_{"eff"} is calculated from . Since the appropriate definition of degrees of freedom recognizes that ν ii(nu) as it appears in the t t-distribution t t-distribution is a measure of the uncertainty of the variance s 2 ( z ¯ ) s^2(bar(z)), in may be used to define the degrees of freedom ν i ii(nu)_i,

The quantity in large brackets is the relative uncertainty of u ( x i ) u(x_i); for a Type B evaluation of standard uncertainty it is a subjective quantity whose value is obtained by scientific judgement based on the pool of available information.

example.p: Consider that one’s knowledge of how input estimate x i x_i was determined and how its standard uncertainty u ( x i ) u(x_i) was evaluated leads one to judge that the value of u ( x i ) u(x_i) is reliable to about 25 percent. This may be taken to mean that the relative uncertainty is Δ u ( x i ) / u ( x i ) = 0.25 Delta u(x_i)//u(x_i) = 0.25, and thus from , ν i = ( 0.25 ) − 2 / 2 = 8 ii(nu)_i = (0.25)^{-2}//2 = 8. If instead one had judged the value of u ( x i ) u(x_i) to be reliable to only about 50 percent, then ν i = 2 ii(nu)_i = 2. (See also in .)degrees of freedom of a Type B standard uncertainty

clause.p: In the discussion in and of Type B evaluation of standard uncertainty from an a priori probability distributiondistributiona priori, it was implicitly assumed that the value of u ( x i ) u(x_i) resulting from such an evaluation is exactly known. For example, when u ( x i ) u(x_i) is obtained from a rectangular probability distributiondistributionrectangular of assumed half-width a = ( a + − a − ) / 2 a = (a_+ - a_"−")//2 as in and , u ( x i ) = a / 3 u(x_i) = a//sqrt(3) is viewed as a constant with no uncertainty because a + a_+ and a − a_"−", and thus a a, are so viewed (but see ). This implies through that ν i → ∞ ii(nu)_i -> oo or 1 / ν i → 0 1//ii(nu)_i -> 0, but it causes no difficulty in evaluating . Further, assuming that ν i → ∞ ii(nu)_i -> oo is not necessarily unrealistic; it is common practice to choose a − a_"−" and a + a_+ in such a way that the probability of the quantity in question lying outside the interval a − a_"−" to a + a_+ is extremely small.degrees of freedom of a Type B standard uncertainty

In the limits u 2 / s 2 → ∞ u^2//s^2 -> oo and ν eff → ∞ ii(nu)_{"eff"} -> oo, U ′ 95 → 1.732 u ii(U)'_{95} -> 1.732u while U 95 → 1.960 u ii(U)_{95} -> 1.960u. In this case, U ′ 95 ii(U)'_{95} provides an interval having only a 91.791.7 percent level of confidence, while U 95 ii(U)_{95} provides a 95 percent interval. This case is approximated in practice when the components obtained from estimates of upper and lower bounds are dominant, large in number, and have values of u j 2 ( y ) = c j 2 a j 2 / 3 u_j^2(y) = c_j^2 a_j^2//3 that are of comparable size.
For a normal distributionnormal distribution, the coverage factorcoverage factor k = 3 ≈ 1.732 k = sqrt(3) ~~ 1.732 provides an interval with a level of confidence p = 91.673 p = 91.673…​ percent. This value of p p is robust in the sense that it is, in comparison with that of any other value, optimally independent of small deviations of the input quantities from normality.

Occasionally an input quantity X i ii(X)_i is distributed asymmetricallydistributionasymmetric — deviations about its expected value of one sign are more probable than deviations of the opposite sign (see ). Although this makes no difference in the evaluation of the standard uncertainty u ( x i ) u(x_i) of the estimate x i x_i of X i ii(X)_i, and thus in the evaluation of u c ( y ) u_{"c"}(y), it may affect the calculation of U ii(U).

It is usually convenient to give a symmetric interval, Y = y ± U ii(Y) = y pm ii(U), unless the interval is such that there is a cost differential between deviations of one sign over the other. If the asymmetry of X i ii(X)_i causes only a small asymmetry in the probability distribution characterized by the measurement result y y and its combined standard uncertainty u c ( y ) u_{"c"}(y), the probability lost on one side by quoting a symmetric interval is compensated by the probability gained on the other side. The alternative is to give an interval that is symmetric in probability (and thus asymmetric in U ii(U)): the probability that Y ii(Y) lies below the lower limit y − U − y - ii(U)_"−" is equal to the probability that Y ii(Y) lies above the upper limit y + U + y + ii(U)_+. But in order to quote such limits, more information than simply theestimates y y and u c ( y ) u_{"c"}(y) [and hence more information than simply the estimates x i x_i and u ( x i ) u(x_i) of each input quantity X i ii(X)_i] is needed.expanded uncertaintyexpanded uncertainty for an asymmetric distribution

The evaluation of the expanded uncertaintyexpanded uncertainty U p ii(U)_p given here in terms of u c ( y ) u_{"c"}(y), ν eff ii(nu)_{"eff"}, and the factor t p ( ν eff ) t _p (ii(nu)_{"eff"}) from the t t-distribution t t-distribution is only an approximation, and it has its limitations. The distribution of ( y − Y ) / u c ( y ) (y - ii(Y))//u_{"c"}(y) is given by the t t-distribution t t-distribution only if the distribution of Y ii(Y) is normal, the estimate y y and its combined standard uncertainty u c ( y ) u_{"c"}(y) are independent, and if the distribution of u c 2 ( y ) u_{"c"}^2(y) is a χ 2 ii(chi)^2 distribution. The introduction of ν eff ii(nu)_{"eff"}, , deals only with the latter problem, and provides an approximately χ 2 ii(chi)^2 distribution for u c 2 ( y ) u_{"c"}^2(y); the other part of the problem, arising from the non-normality of the distribution of Y ii(Y), requires the consideration of higher moments in addition to the variance.degrees of freedomeffective t t-factor

The coverage factorcoverage factor k p k_p that provides an interval having a level of confidencelevel of confidence p p close to a specified level can only be found if there is extensive knowledge of the probability distribution of each input quantity and if these distributions are combined to obtain the distribution of the output quantity. The input estimates x i x_i and their standard uncertainties u ( x i ) u(x_i) by themselves are inadequate for this purpose.

Because the extensive computations required to combine probability distributions are seldom justified by the extent and reliability of the available information, an approximation to the distribution of the output quantity is acceptable. Because of the Central Limit TheoremCentral Limit Theorem, it is usually sufficient to assume that the probability distribution of ( y − Y ) / u c ( y ) (y-ii(Y))//u_{"c"}(y) is the t t-distribution t t-distribution and take k p = t p ( ν eff ) k_p = t_p(ii(nu)_{"eff"}), with the t t-factor t t-factor based on an effective degrees of freedom ν eff ii(nu)_{"eff"} of u c ( y ) u_{"c"}(y) obtained from the Welch-Satterthwaite formulaWelch-Satterthwaite formula, .degrees of freedomeffective

To obtain ν eff ii(nu)_{"eff"} from requires the degrees of freedomdegrees of freedom ν i ii(nu)_i for each standard uncertainty component. For a component obtained from a Type A evaluation, ν i ii(nu)_i is obtained from the number of independent repeated observations upon which the corresponding input estimate is based and the number of independent quantities determined from those observations (see ). For a component obtained from a Type B evaluation, ν i ii(nu)_i is obtained from the judged reliability of the value of that component [see and ].degrees of freedom of a Type A standard uncertaintydegrees of freedom of a Type B standard uncertainty

Thus the following is a summary of the preferred method of calculating an expanded uncertaintyexpanded uncertainty U p = k p u c ( y ) ii(U)_p = k_p u_{"c"}(y) intended to provide an interval Y = y ± U p ii(Y) = y pm ii(U)_p that has an approximate level of confidencelevel of confidence p p:

li.p: Obtain y y and u c ( y ) u_{"c"}(y) as described in and .
Compute ν eff ii(nu)_{"eff"} from the Welch-Satterthwaite formulaWelch-Satterthwaite formula, (repeated here for easy reference)

If u ( x i ) u(x_i) is obtained from a Type A evaluation, determine ν i ii(nu)_i as outlined in . If u ( x i ) u(x_i) is obtained from a Type B evaluation and it can be treated as exactly known, which is often the case in practice, ν i → ∞ ii(nu)_i -> oo; otherwise, estimate ν i ii(nu)_i from .

Obtain the t t-factor t t-factor t p ( ν eff ) t_p (ii(nu)_{"eff"}) for the desired level of confidence p p from . If ν eff ii(nu)_{"eff"} is not an integer, either interpolate or truncate ν eff ii(nu)_{"eff"} to the next lower integer.

Take k p = t p ( ν eff ) k_p = t_p (ii(nu)_{"eff"}) and calculate U p = k p u c ( y ) ii(U)_p = k_p u_{"c"}(y).degrees of freedomdegrees of freedom of a Type A standard uncertaintydegrees of freedom of a Type B standard uncertaintyexpanded uncertainty

clause.p: In certain situations, which should not occur too frequently in practice, the conditions required by the Central Limit TheoremCentral Limit Theorem may not be well met and the approach of may lead to an unacceptable result. For example, if u c ( y ) u_{"c"}(y) is dominated by a component of uncertainty evaluated from a rectangular distribution whose bounds are assumed to be exactly known, it is possible [if t p ( ν eff ) > 3 t_p (ii(nu)_{"eff"}) > sqrt(3)] that y + U p y + ii(U)_p and y − U p y - ii(U)_p, the upper and lower limits of the interval defined by U p ii(U)_p, could lie outside the bounds of the probability distribution of the output quantity Y ii(Y). Such cases must be dealt with on an individual basis but are often amenable to an approximate analytic treatment (involving, for example, the convolution of a normal distribution with a rectangular distribution ).expanded uncertaintyprobability distributions, convolving t t-factor
For many practical measurements in a broad range of fields, the following conditions prevail:

li.p: the estimate y y of the measurand Y ii(Y) is obtained from estimates x i x_i of a significant number of input quantities X i ii(X)_i that are describable by well-behaved probability distributions, such as the normal and rectangulardistributions;

the standard uncertainties u ( x i ) u(x_i) of these estimates, which may be obtained from either Type A or Type B evaluations, contribute comparable amounts to the combined standard uncertainty u c ( y ) u_{"c"}(y) of the measurement result y y;
the linear approximation implied by the law of propagation of uncertaintyuncertaintylaw of propagation of is adequate (see and );
the uncertainty of u c ( y ) u_{"c"}(y) is reasonably small because its effective degrees of freedom ν eff ii(nu)_{"eff"} has a significant magnitude, say greater than 10.

clause.p: Under these circumstances, the probability distribution characterized by the measurement result and its combined standard uncertainty can be assumed to be normal because of the Central Limit TheoremCentral Limit Theorem; and u c ( y ) u_{"c"}(y) can be taken as a reasonably reliable estimate of the standard deviation of that normal distribution because of the significant size of ν eff ii(nu)_{"eff"}. Then, based on the discussion given in this annex, including that emphasizing the approximate nature of the uncertainty evaluation process and the impracticality of trying to distinguish between intervals having levels of confidence that differ by one or two percent, one may do the following:

li.p: adopt k = 2 k = 2 and assume that U = 2 u c ( y ) ii(U) = 2 u_{"c"}(y) defines an interval having a level of confidencelevel of confidence of approximately 95 percent;

clause.p: or, for more critical applications,

li.p: adopt k = 3 k = 3 and assume that U = 3 u c ( y ) ii(U) = 3 u_{"c"}(y) defines an interval having a level of confidence of approximately 99 percent.

clause.p: Although this approach should be suitable for many practical measurements, its applicability to any particular measurement will depend on how close k = 2 k = 2 must be to t 95 ( ν eff ) t_{95}(ii(nu)_{"eff"}) or k = 3 k = 3 must be to t 99 ( ν eff ) t_{99}(ii(nu)_{"eff"}); that is, on how close the level of confidence of the interval defined by U = 2 u c ( y ) ii(U) = 2 u_{"c"}(y) or U = 3 u c ( y ) ii(U) = 3 u_{"c"}(y) must be to 95 percent or 99 percent, respectively. Although for ν eff = 11 ii(nu)_{"eff"} = 11, k = 2 k = 2 and k = 3 k = 3 underestimate t 95 ( 11 ) t_{95}(11) and t 99 ( 11 ) t_{99}(11) by only about 10 percent and 4 percent, respectively (see ), this may not be acceptable in some cases. Further, for all values of ν eff ii(nu)_{"eff"} somewhat larger than 13, k = 3 k = 3 produces an interval having a level of confidence larger than 99 percent. (See , which also shows that for ν eff → ∞ ii(nu)_{"eff"} -> oo the levels of confidence of the intervals produced by k = 2 k= 2 and k = 3 k = 3 are 95.4595.45 percent and 99.73 percent, respectively). Thus, in practice, the size of ν eff ii(nu)_{"eff"} and what is required of the expanded uncertaintyexpanded uncertainty will determine whether this approach can be used. t t-factor

annex.title: Examples
annex.p: This annex gives six examples, to , which are worked out in considerable detail in order to illustrate the basic principles presented in this Guide for evaluating and expressing uncertainty in measurement. Together with the examples included in the main text and in some of the other annexes, they should enable the users of this Guide to put these principles into practice in their own work.

Because the examples are for illustrative purposes, they have by necessity been simplified. Moreover, because they and the numerical data used in them have been chosen mainly to demonstrate the principles of this Guide, neither they nor the data should necessarily be interpreted as describing real measurements. While the data are used as given, in order to prevent rounding errors, more digits are retained in intermediate calculations than are usually shown. Thus the stated result of a calculation involving several quantities may differ slightly from the result implied by the numerical values given in the text for these quantities.

It is pointed out in earlier portions of this Guide that classifying the methods used to evaluate components of uncertainty as Type A or Type B is for convenience only; it is not required for the determination of the combined standard uncertainty or expanded uncertainty of a measurement result because all uncertainty components, however they are evaluated, are treated in the same way (see , , and ). Thus, in the examples, the method used to evaluate a particular component of uncertainty is not specifically identified as to its type. However, it will be clear from the discussion whether a component is obtained from a Type A or a Type B evaluation.

clause.title: End-gauge calibration
clause.p: This example demonstrates that even an apparently simple measurement may involve subtle aspects of uncertainty evaluation.

clause.title: The measurement problem
clause.p: The length of a nominally 50 ⁢ mm 50 "unitsml(mm)" end gauge is determined by comparing it with a known standard of the same nominal length. The direct output of the comparison of the two end gauges is the difference d d in their lengths:

stem.asciimath: d = l(1 + ii(alpha) ii(theta)) - l_{"S"}(1 + ii(alpha)_{"S"}ii(theta)_{"S"})

(If the measurand were expressed in terms of the variables θ ii(theta), θ S ii(theta)_{"S"}, α ii(alpha), and α S ii(alpha)_{"S"}, it would be necessary to include the correlation between θ ii(theta) and θ S ii(theta)_{"S"}, and between α ii(alpha) and α S ii(alpha)_{"S"}.)
