clause.p: The calibration certificate for the standard end gauge gives l S = 50.000623 ⁢ mm l_{"S"} = 50.000623 "unitsml(mm)" as its length at 20 ⁢ °C 20 "unitsml(degC)". The arithmetic mean d ¯ bar(d) of the five repeated observations of the difference in lengths between the unknown end gauge and the standard gauge is 215 ⁢ nm 215 "unitsml(nm)". Thus, since l = l S + d ¯ l = l_{"S"} + bar(d) (see ), the length l l of the unknown end gauge at 20 ⁢ °C 20 "unitsml(degC)" is 50.000838 ⁢ mm 50.000838 "unitsml(mm)". clause.p: Suppose that one is required to obtain an expanded uncertainty U 99 = k 99 u c ( l ) ii(U)_{99} = k_{99} u_{"c"}(l) that provides an interval having a level of confidence of approximately 99 percent. The procedure to use is that summarized in , and the required degrees of freedom are indicated in . These were obtained as follows:

li.p: Uncertainty of the calibration of the standard, u ( l S ) u(l_{"S"}) []. The calibration certificate states that theeffective degrees of freedom of the combined standard uncertainty from which the quoted expanded uncertainty was obtained is ν eff ( l S ) = 18 ii(nu)_{"eff"}(l_{"S"}) = 18.

The degrees of freedom of u ( d 1 ) u(d_1), the uncertainty due to random effects on the comparator, is ν ( d 1 ) = 6 − 1 = 5 ii(nu)(d_1) = 6 - 1 = 5 because d 1 d_1 was obtained from six repeated measurements. The ± 0.02 ⁢ µm pm 0.02 "unitsml(um)" uncertainty for systematic effects on the comparator may be assumed to be reliable to 25 percent, and thus the degrees of freedom from in is ν ( d 2 ) = 8 ii(nu)(d_2) = 8 (see the of ). This gives, from in , ν ( δ α ) = 50 ii(nu) (delta ii(alpha)) = 50.

clause.title: Second-order terms
clause.p: The points out that , which is used in this example to obtain the combined standard uncertainty u c ( l ) u_{"c"}(l), must be augmented when the nonlinearity of the functionfunctional relationshipnonlinear Y = f ( X 1 , X 2 , … , X N ) ii(Y) = f (ii(X)_1, ii(X)_2,..., ii(X)_N) is so significant that the higher-order termshigher-order terms in the Taylor seriesTaylor series expansion cannot be neglected. Such is the case in this example, and therefore the evaluation of u c ( l ) u_{"c"}(l) as presented up to this point is not complete. Application to of the expression given in the yields in fact two distinct non-negligible second-order terms to be added to . These terms, which arise from the quadratic term in the expression of the , are

stem.asciimath: l_{"S"}^2 u^2(delta ii(alpha)) u^2(ii(theta)) + l_{"S"}^2 u^2(ii(alpha)_{"S"}) u^2(delta ii(theta))

clause.p: but only the first of these terms contributes significantly to u c ( l ) u_{"c"}(l):
The second-order terms increase u c ( l ) u_{"c"}(l) from 32 ⁢ nm 32 "unitsml(nm)" to 34 ⁢ nm 34 "unitsml(nm)".

clause.title: Simultaneous resistance and reactance measurement
clause.p: This example demonstrates the treatment of multiple measurands or output quantities determined simultaneously in the same measurement and the correlation of their estimates. It considers only the random variations of the observations; in actual practice, the uncertainties of corrections for systematic effects would also contribute to the uncertainty of the measurement results. The data are analysed in two different ways with each yielding essentially the same numerical values.

clause.title: The measurement problem
clause.p: The resistance R ii(R) and the reactance X ii(X) of a circuit element are determined by measuring the amplitude V ii(V) of a sinusoidally-alternating potential difference across its terminals, the amplitude I ii(I) of the alternating current passing through it, and the phase-shift angle ϕ ii(phi) of the alternating potential difference relative to the alternating current. Thus the three input quantities are V ii(V), I ii(I), and ϕ ii(phi) and the three output quantities — the measurands — are the three impedance components R ii(R), X ii(X), and Z ii(Z). Since Z 2 = R 2 + X 2 ii(Z)^2 = ii(R)^2 + ii(X)^2, there are only two independent output quantities.

clause.title: Mathematical model and data
clause.p: The measurands are related to the input quantities by Ohm’s law:

stem.asciimath: ii(R) = ii(V)/ii(I) cos ii(phi); " " " " ii(X) = ii(V)/ii(I) sin ii(phi): " " " " ii(Z) = ii(V)/ii(I)

clause.p: Consider that five independent sets of simultaneous observations of the three input quantities V ii(V), I ii(I), and ϕ ii(phi) are obtained under similar conditions (see ), resulting in the data given in . The arithmetic means of the observations and the experimental standard deviations of those means calculated from and are also given. The means are taken as the best estimates of the expected values of the input quantities, and the experimental standard deviations are the standard uncertainties of those means.

Because the means V ¯ bar(ii(V)), I ¯ bar(ii(I)), and ϕ ¯ bar(ii(phi)) are obtained from simultaneous observationsobservationsindependent pairs of simultaneous, they are correlated and the correlations must be taken into account in the evaluation of the standard uncertainties of the measurands R ii(R), X ii(X), and Z ii(Z). The required correlation coefficientcorrelation coefficients are readily obtained from in using values of s ( V ¯ , I ¯ ) s(bar(ii(V)),bar(ii(I))), s ( V ¯ , ϕ ¯ ) s(bar(ii(V)),bar(ii(phi))), and s ( I ¯ , ϕ ¯ ) s(bar(ii(I)),bar(ii(phi))) calculated from in . The results are included in , where it should be recalled that r ( x i , x j ) = r ( x j , x i ) r(x_i,x_j) = r(x_j,x_i) and r ( x i , x i ) = 1 r(x_i,x_i) = 1.

clause.title: Results: approach 1
clause.p: Approach 1 is summarized in .

The values of the three measurands R ii(R), X ii(X), and Z ii(Z) are obtained from the relations given in using the mean values V ¯ bar(ii(V)), I ¯ bar(ii(I)), and ϕ ¯ bar(ii(phi)) of for V ii(V), I ii(I), and ϕ ii(phi). The standard uncertainties of R ii(R), X ii(X), and Z ii(Z) are obtained from in since, as pointed out above, the input quantities V ¯ bar(ii(V)), I ¯ bar(ii(I)), and ϕ ¯ bar(ii(phi)) are correlatedcorrelated output estimates or quantities. As an example, consider Z = V ¯ / I ¯ ii(Z) = bar(ii(V))//bar(ii(I)). Substitution of the appropriate values from into then gives u c ( Z ) = 0.236 ⁢ Ω u_{"c"}(ii(Z)) = 0.236 "unitsml(Ohm)".

Because the three measurands or output quantities depend on the same input quantities, they too are correlated. The elements of the covariance matrixcovariance matrix that describes this correlation may be written in general as

stem.asciimath: u(y_l,y_m) = sum_{i=1}^ii(N) sum_{j=1}^ii(N) {del y_l}/{del x_i} {del y_m}/{del x_j} u(x_i) u(x_j) r(x_i,x_j)

clause.p: where y l = f l ( x 1 , x 2 , … , x N ) y_l = f_l(x_1, x_2,..., x_N) and y m = f m ( x 1 , x 2 , … , x N ) y_m = f_m(x_1, x_2,..., x_N). is a generalization of in when the q l q_l in that expression are correlated. The estimated correlation coefficientcorrelation coefficients of the output quantities are given by r ( y l , y m ) = u ( y l , y m ) / u ( y l ) u ( y m ) r(y_l, y_m) = u(y_l, y_m)//u(y_l)u(y_m), as indicated in in . It should be recognized that the diagonal elements of the covariance matrixcovariance matrix, u ( y l , y l ) ≡ u 2 ( y l ) u(y_l,y_l) -= u^2(y_l), are the estimated variances of the output quantities y l y_l (see ) and that for m = l m = l, is identical to in .

clause.title: Results: approach 2
clause.p: Approach 2 is summarized in .

Since the data have been obtained as five sets of observationsobservationsindependent pairs of simultaneous of the three input quantities V ii(V), I ii(I), and ϕ ii(phi), it is possible to compute a value for R ii(R), X ii(X), and Z ii(Z) from each set of input data, and then take the arithmetic meancovariance of two arithmetic means of the five individual values to obtain the best estimates of R ii(R), X ii(X), and Z ii(Z). The experimental standard deviation of each mean (which is its combined standard uncertainty) is then calculated from the five individual values in the usual way [ in ]; and the estimated covariances of the three means are calculated by applying in directly to the five individual values from which each mean is obtained. There are no differences in the output values, standard uncertainties, and estimated covarianc es provided by the two approaches except for second-order effects associated with replacing terms such as V ¯ / I ¯ bar(ii(V))//bar(ii(I)) and cos { ϕ ¯ } cos{bar(ii(phi))} by V / I ¯ bar(ii(V)//ii(I)) and cos ϕ ¯ bar(cos ii(phi)).

To demonstrate this approach, gives the values of R ii(R), X ii(X) and Z ii(Z) calculated from each of the five sets of observations. The arithmetic means, standard uncertainties, and estimated correlation coefficientcorrelation coefficients are then directly computed from these individual values. The numerical results obtained in this way are negligibly different from the results given in .correlated output estimates or quantities

clause.p: In the terminology of the , approach 2 is an example of obtaining the estimate y y from Y ¯ = ( ∑ k = 1 n Y k ) / n bar(ii(Y)) = (sum_{k=1}^n ii(Y)_k) // n, while approach 1 is an example of obtaining y y from y = f ( X ¯ 1 , X ¯ 2 , … , X ¯ N ) y = f(bar(ii(X))_1, bar(ii(X))_2,...,bar(ii(X))_N). As pointed out in that note, in general, the two approaches will give identical results if f f is a linear function of its input quantities (provided that the experimentally observed correlation coefficientcorrelation coefficients are taken into account when implementing approach 1). If f f is not a linear function, then the results of approach 1 will differ from those of approach 2 depending on the degree of nonlinearityfunctional relationshipnonlinear and the estimated variances and covariances of the X i ii(X)_i. This may be seen from the expression

stem.asciimath: y = f(bar(ii(X))_1, bar(ii(X))_2,...,bar(ii(X))_N) + 1/2 sum_{i=1}^ii(N) sum_{j=1}^ii(N) {del^2 f}/{del bar(ii(X))_i del bar(ii(X))_j} u(bar(ii(X))_i, bar(ii(X))_j) + ...

clause.p: where the second term on the right-hand side is the second-order term in the Taylor seriesTaylor series expansion of f f in terms of the X ¯ i bar(ii(X))_i (see also ). In the present case, approach 2 is preferred because it avoids the approximation y = f ( X ¯ 1 , X ¯ 2 , … , X ¯ N ) y = f(bar(ii(X))_1, bar(ii(X))_2,..., bar(ii(X))_N) and better reflects the measurement procedure used — the data were in fact collected in sets.

On the other hand, approach 2 would be inappropriate if the data of represented n 1 = 5 n_1 = 5 observations of the potential difference V ii(V), followed by n 2 = 5 n_2 = 5 observations of the current I ii(I), and then followed by n 3 = 5 n_3 = 5 observations of the phase ϕ ii(phi), and would be impossible if x = n 1 ≠ n 2 ≠ n 3 x = n_1 != n_2 != n_3. (It is in fact poor measurement procedure to carry out the measurements in this way since the potential difference across a fixed impedance and the current through it are directly related.)

If the data of are reinterpreted in this manner so that approach 2 is inappropriate, and if correlations among the quantities V ii(V), I ii(I), and ϕ ii(phi) are assumed to be absent, then the observed correlation coefficientcorrelation coefficients have no significance and should be set equal to zero. If this is done in , reduces to the equivalent of in , namely,

stem.asciimath: u(y_l,y_m) = sum_{i=i}^ii(N) {del y_l}/{del x_i} {del y_m}/{del x_i} u^2(x_i)

clause.title: Calibration of a thermometer
clause.p: This example illustrates the use of the method of least squaresleast squaresmethod of to obtain a linear calibration curvecalibration curvelinear and how the parameters of the fit, the intercept and slope, and their estimated variances and covariance, are used to obtain from the curve the value and standard uncertainty of a predicted correction.

clause.title: The measurement problem
clause.p: A thermometer is calibrated by comparing n = 11 n = 11 temperature readings t k t_k of the thermometer, each having negligible uncertainty, with corresponding known reference temperatures t R , k t_{"R",k} in the temperature range 21 ⁢ °C 21 "unitsml(degC)" to 27 ⁢ °C 27 "unitsml(degC)" to obtain the corrections b k = t R , k − t k b_k = t_{"R",k} - t_k to the readings. The measured corrections b k b_k and measured temperatures t k t_k are the input quantities of the evaluation. A linear calibration curve

is fitted to the measured corrections and temperatures by the method of least squaresleast squaresmethod of. The parameters y 1 y_1 and y 2 y_2, which are respectively the intercept and slope of the calibration curve, are the two measurands or outputquantities to be determined. The temperature t 0 t_0 is a conveniently chosen exact reference temperature; it is not an independent parameter to be determined by the least-squares fit. Once y 1 y_1 and y 2 y_2 are found, along with their estimated variances and covariance, can be used to predict the value and standard uncertainty of the correction to be applied to the thermometer for any value t t of the temperature.

clause.title: Least-squares fitting
clause.p: Based on the method of least squaresleast squaresmethod of and under the assumptions made in above, the output quantities y 1 y_1 and y 2 y_2 and their estimated variances and covariance are obtained by minimizing the sum

This leads to the following equations for y 1 y_1, y 2 y_2, their experimental variances s 2 ( y 1 ) s^2(y_1) and s 2 ( y 2 ) s^2(y_2), and their estimated correlation coefficientcorrelation coefficient r ( y 1 , y 2 ) = s ( y 1 , y 2 ) / s ( y 1 ) s ( y 2 ) r(y_1, y_2) = s(y_1, y_2)//s(y_1)s(y_2), where s ( y 1 , y 2 ) s(y_1, y_2) is their estimated covariance:

The variance s 2 s^2 is a measure of the overall uncertainty of the fit, where the factor n − 2 n - 2 reflects the fact that because two parameters, y 1 y_1 and y 2 y_2, are determined by the n n observations, the degrees of freedom of s 2 s^2 is ν = n − 2 ii(nu) = n - 2 (see ).correlated output estimates or quantities

clause.p: The calibration curve may then be written as

where the numbers in parentheses are the numerical values of the standard uncertainties referred to the corresponding last digits of the quoted results for the intercept and slope (see ). This equation gives the predicted value of the correction b ( t ) b(t) at any temperature t t, and in particular the value b ( t k ) b(t_k) at t = t k t = t_k. These values are given in the fourth column of the table while the last column gives the differences between the measured and predicted values, b k − b ( t k ) b_k - b(t_k). An analysis of these differences can be used to check the validity of the linear model; formal tests exist (see Reference ), but are not considered in this example.

clause.title: Uncertainty of a predicted value
clause.p: The expression for the combined standard uncertainty of the predicted value of a correction can be readily obtained by applying the law of propagation of uncertainty, in , to . Noting that b ( t ) = f ( y 1 , y 2 ) b(t) = f (y_1, y_2) and writing u ( y 1 ) = s ( y 1 ) u(y_1) = s (y_1) and u ( y 2 ) = s ( y 2 ) u(y_2) = s (y_2), one obtains

clause.title: Other considerations
clause.p: The least-squares method can be used to fit higher-order curves to data points, and is also applicable to cases where the individual data points have uncertainties. Standard texts on the subject should be consulted for details . However, the following examples illustrate two cases where the measured corrections b k b_k are not assumed to be exactly known.

clause.title: Measurement of activity
clause.p: This example is similar to example , the simultaneous measurement of resistance and reactance, in that the data can be analysed in two different ways but each yields essentially the same numerical result. The first approach illustrates once again the need to take the observed correlations between input quantities into account.

clause.title: The measurement problem
clause.p: The unknown radon (222Rn) activity concentration in a water sample is determined by liquid-scintillation counting against a radon-in-water standard sample having a known activity concentration. The unknown activity concentration is obtained by measuring three counting sources consisting of approximately 5 ⁢ g 5 "unitsml(g)" of water and 12 ⁢ g 12 "unitsml(g)" of organic emulsion scintillator in vials of volume 22 ⁢ ml 22 "unitsml(ml)":

dd.p: a standard consisting of a mass m S m_{"S"} of the standard solution with a known activity concentration;
a matched blank water sample containing no radioactive material, used to obtain the background counting rate;
the sample consisting of an aliquot of mass m x m_x with unknown activity concentration.

clause.p: Six cycles of measurement of the three counting sources are made in the order standard — blank — sample; and each dead-time-corrected counting interval T 0 ii(T)_0 for each source during all six cycles is 60 minutes. Although the background counting rate cannot be assumed to be constant over the entire counting interval (65 hours), it is assumed that the number of counts obtained for each blank may be used as representative of the background counting rate during the measurements of the standard and sample in the same cycle. The data are given in , where

dl.dt: t S t_{"S"}, t B t_{"B"}, t x t_x

dd.p: are the number of counts recorded in the dead-time-corrected counting intervals T 0 = 60 ⁢ min ii(T)_0 = 60 "unitsml(min)" for the standard, blank, and sample vials, respectively.

clause.title: Calculation of final results
clause.p: To obtain the unknown activity concentration A x ii(A)_x and its combined standard uncertainty u c ( A x ) u_{"c"}(ii(A)_x) from requires A S ii(A)_{"S"}, m x m_x, and m S m_{"S"} and their standard uncertainties. These are given as
Other possible sources of uncertainty are evaluated to be negligible:

(The significant quantity is the decay factor exp [ λ ( t x − t S ) ] exp[ii(lambda)(t_x - t_{"S"})], which varies from 1.015631.01563 for cycles k = 4 k = 4 and 6 to 1.015701.01570 for cycle k = 1 k = 1. The standard uncertainty of these values is u = 1.2 × 10 − 5 u = 1.2 xx 10^{-5});
uncertainty associated with the possible dependence of the detection efficiency of the scintillation counter on the source used (standard, blank, and sample);
uncertainty of the correction for counter dead-time and of the correction for the dependence of counting efficiency on activity level.

clause.title: Results: approach 1
clause.p: As indicated earlier, A x ii(A)_x and u c ( A x ) u_{"c"}(ii(A)_x) may be obtained in two different ways from ). In the first approach, A x ii(A)_x is calculated using the arithmetic means R ¯ x bar(ii(R))_x and R ¯ S bar(ii(R))_{"S"}, which leads to
Application of in to this expression yields for the combined variance u c 2 ( A x ) u_{"c"}^2(ii(A)_x)

where, as noted in , the last three terms give u 2 ( R ¯ x / R ¯ S ) / ( R ¯ x / R ¯ S ) 2 u^2(bar(ii(R))_x//bar(ii(R))_{"S"})//(bar(ii(R))_x//bar(ii(R))_{"S"})^2, the estimated relative variance of R ¯ x / R ¯ S bar(ii(R))_x//bar(ii(R))_{"S"}. Consistent with the discussion of , the results in show that R ¯ bar(ii(R)) is not exactly equal to R ¯ x / R ¯ S bar(ii(R))_x//bar(ii(R))_{"S"}; and that the standard uncertainty u ( R ¯ x / R ¯ S ) u(bar(ii(R))_x//bar(ii(R))_{"S"}) of R ¯ x / R ¯ S bar(ii(R))_x // bar(ii(R))_{"S"} is not exactly equal to the standard uncertainty s ( R ¯ ) s(bar(ii(R))) of R ¯ bar(ii(R)).
Substitution of the values of the relevant quantities into and yields
The result of the measurement may then be stated as:
A x = 0.4300 ⁢ Bq ⋅ g − 1 ii(A)_x = 0.4300 "unitsml(Bq/g)" with a combined standard uncertainty of u c = 0.0083 ⁢ Bq ⋅ g − 1 u_{"c"} = 0.0083 "unitsml(Bq/g)".

clause.title: Results: approach 2
clause.p: In the second approach, which avoids the correlation between R ¯ x bar(ii(R))_x and R ¯ S bar(ii(R))_{"S"}, A x ii(A)_x is calculated using the arithmetic mean R ¯ bar(ii(R)). Thus
The expression for u c 2 ( A x ) u_{"c"}^2(ii(A)_x) is simply
which yields
The result of the measurement may then be stated as:

quote.p: A x = 0.4304 ⁢ Bq ⋅ g − 1 ii(A)_x = 0.4304 "unitsml(Bq/g)" with a combined standard uncertainty of u c = 0.0084 ⁢ Bq ⋅ g − 1 u_{"c"} = 0.0084 "unitsml(Bq/g)".

clause.p: The effective degrees of freedom of u c u_{"c"} can be evaluated using the Welch-Satterthwaite formula in the manner illustrated in .

As in , of the two results, the second is preferred because it avoids approximating the mean of a ratio of two quantities by the ratio of the means of the two quantities; and it better reflects the measurement procedure used — the data were in fact collected in separate cycles.

Nevertheless, the difference between the values of A x ii(A)_x resulting from the two approaches is clearly small compared with the standard uncertainty ascribed to either one, and the difference between the two standard uncertainties is entirely negligible. Such agreement demonstrates that the two approaches are equivalent when the observed correlations are properly included.

clause.title: Analysis of variance
clause.p: This example provides a brief introduction to analysis of variance (ANOVA) methods. These statistical techniques are used to identify and quantify individual random effects in a measurement so that they may be properly taken into account when the uncertainty of the result of the measurement is evaluated. Although ANOVAANOVA methods are applicable to a wide range of measurements, for example, the calibration of reference standards, such as Zener voltage standards and standards of mass, and the certification of reference materialsreference materialscertification of, ANOVA methods by themselves cannot identify systematic effects that might be present.

There are many different models included under the general name of ANOVA. Because of its importance, the specific model discussed in this example is the balanced nested design. The numerical illustration of this model involves the calibration of a Zener voltage standard; the analysis should be relevant to a variety of practical measurement situations.

ANOVA methods are of special importance in the certification of reference materials (RMs) by interlaboratory testing, a topic covered thoroughly in ISO Guide 35 (see for a brief description of such RM certification). Since much of the material contained in ISO Guide 35 is in fact broadly applicable, that publication may be consulted for additional details concerning ANOVA, including unbalanced nested designs. References and may be similarly consulted.

clause.title: The measurement problem
clause.p: Consider a nominally 10 ⁢ V 10 "unitsml(V)" Zener voltage standard that is calibrated against a stable voltage reference over a two-week period. On each of J ii(J) days during the period, K ii(K) independent repeated observations of the potential difference V S ii(V)_{"S"} of the standard are made. If V j k ii(V)_{jk} denotes the k kth observation of V S ii(V)_{"S"}( k = 1 , 2 , … , K k = 1, 2,...,ii(K)) on the j jth day ( j = 1 , 2 , … , J j = 1, 2,..., ii(J)), the best estimate of the potential difference of the standard is the arithmetic mean V ¯ bar(ii(V)) of the J K ii(JK) observations [see in ],

The experimental standard deviation of the mean s ( V ¯ ) s(bar(ii(V))), which is a measure of the uncertainty of V ¯ bar(ii(V)) as an estimate of the potential difference of the standard, is obtained from [see in ]

note.p: It is assumed throughout this example that all corrections applied to the observations to compensate for systematic effects have negligible uncertainties or their uncertainties are such that they can be taken into account at the end of the analysis. A correction in this latter category, and one that can itself be applied to the mean of the observations at the end of the analysis, is the difference between the certified value (assumed to have a given uncertainty) and the working value of the stable voltage reference against which the Zener voltage standard is calibrated. Thus the estimate of the potential difference of the standard obtained statistically from the observations is not necessarily the final result of the measurement; and the experimental standard deviation of that estimate is not necessarily the combined standard uncertainty of the final result.

clause.p: The experimental standard deviation of the mean s ( V ¯ ) s(bar(ii(V))) as obtained from is an appropriate measure of the uncertainty of V ¯ bar(ii(V)) only if the day-to-day variability of the observations is the same as the variability of the observations made on a single day. If there is evidence that the between-day variability is significantly larger than can be expected from the within-day variability, use of this expression could lead to a considerable understatement of the uncertainty of V ¯ bar(ii(V)). Two questions thus arise: How should one decide if the between-day variability (characterized by a between-day component of variance) is significant in comparison with the within-day variability (characterized by a within-day component of variance) and, if it is, how should one evaluate the uncertainty of the mean?
Data which allow the above questions to be addressed are given in , where
J = 10 ii(J) = 10 is the number of days on which potential-difference observations were made;
K = 5 ii(K) = 5 is the number of potential-difference observations made on each day;
is the arithmetic mean of the K = 5 ii(K) = 5 potential-difference observations made on the j jth day (there are J = 10 ii(J) = 10 such daily means);

stem.asciimath: bar(ii(V)) = 1/ii(J) sum_{j=1}^ii(J) bar(ii(V))_j = 1/{ii(JK)} sum_{j=1}^ii(J) sum_{k=1}^ii(K) ii(V)_{jk}

clause.p: is the arithmetic mean of the J = 10 ii(J) = 10 daily means and thus the overall mean of the J K = 50 ii(JK) = 50 observations;
is the experimental variance of the K = 5 ii(K) = 5 observations made on the j jth day (there are J = 10 ii(J) = 10 such estimates of variance); and
is the experimental variance of the J = 10 ii(J) = 10 daily means (there is only one such estimate of variance).

The consistency of the within-day variability and between-day variability of the observations canbe investigated by comparing two independent estimates of σ W 2 ii(sigma)_W^2, the within-day component of variance (that is, the variance of observations made on the same day).variancepooled estimate of (or pooled experimental standard deviation)

The first estimate of σ W 2 ii(sigma)_W^2, denoted by s a 2 s_a^2, is obtained from the observed variation of the daily means V ¯ j bar(ii(V))_j. Since V ¯ j bar(ii(V))_j is the average of K ii(K) observations, its estimated variance s 2 ( V ¯ j ) s^2 (bar(ii(V))_j), under the assumption that the between-day component of variance is zero, estimates σ W 2 / K ii(sigma)_W^2//ii(K). It then follows from that

clause.p: which is an estimate of σ W 2 ii(sigma)_W^2 having ν a = J − 1 = 9 ii(nu)_a = ii(J) - 1 = 9 degrees of freedom.

The second estimate of σ W 2 ii(sigma)_W^2, denoted by s b 2 s_{"b"}^2, is the pooled estimate of variance obtained from the J = 10 ii(J) = 10 individual values of s 2 ( V j k ) s^2(ii(V)_{jk}) using the equation of the , where the ten individual values are calculated from . Because the degrees of freedom of each of these values is ν i = K − 1 ii(nu)_i = ii(K) - 1, the resulting expression for s b 2 s_{"b"}^2 is simply their average. Thus

stem.asciimath: s_{"b"}^2 = bar(s^2(ii(V)_{jk})) = 1/ii(J) sum_{j=1}^ii(J) s^2 (ii(V)_{jk}) = 1/{ii(J)(ii(K) - 1)} sum_{j=1}^ii(J) sum_{k=1}^ii(K) (ii(V)_{jk} - bar(ii(V))_j)^2

clause.p: which is an estimate of σ W 2 ii(sigma)_W^2 having ν b = J ( K − 1 ) = 40 ii(nu)_{"b"} = ii(J)(ii(K) - 1) = 40 degrees of freedom.

The estimates of σ W 2 ii(sigma)_W^2 given by and are s a 2 = ( 128 ⁢ µV ) 2 s_{"a"}^2 = (128 "unitsml(uV)")^2 and s b 2 = ( 85 ⁢ µV ) 2 s_{"b"}^2 = (85 "unitsml(uV)")^2, respectively (see ). Since the estimate s a 2 s_{"a"}^2 is based on the variability of the daily means while the estimate s b 2 s_{"b"}^2 is based on the variability of the daily observations, their difference indicates the possible presence of an effect that varies from one day to another but that remains relatively constant when observations are made on any single day. The F ii(F)-test F ii(F)-test is used to test this possibility, and thus the assumption that the between-day component of variance is zero.

The F ii(F)-distribution F ii(F)-distribution is the probability distribution of the ratio F ( ν a , ν b ) = s a 2 ( ν a ) / s b 2 ( ν b ) ii(F)(ii(nu)_{"a"},ii(nu)_{"b"}) = s_{"a"}^2(ii(nu)_{"a"})//s_{"b"}^2(ii(nu)_{"b"}) of two independent estimates, s a 2 ( ν a ) s_{"a"}^2 (ii(nu)_{"a"}) and s b 2 ( ν b ) s_{"b"}^2 (ii(nu)_{"b"}), of the variance σ 2 ii(sigma)^2 of a normally distributed random variable . The parameters ν a ii(nu)_{"a"} and ν b ii(nu)_{"b"} are the respective degrees of freedom of the two estimates and 0 ≤ F ( ν a , ν b ) < ∞ 0 <= ii(F)(ii(nu)_{"a"},ii(nu)_{"b"}) < oo. Values of F ii(F) are tabulated for different values of ν a ii(nu)_{"a"} and ν b ii(nu)_{"b"} and various quantiles of the F ii(F)-distribution. A value of F ( ν a , ν b ) > F 0.95 F(ii(nu)_{"a"}, ii(nu)_{"b"}) > ii(F)_{0.95} or F ( ν a , ν b ) > F 0.975 F(ii(nu)_{"a"}, ii(nu)_{"b"}) > ii(F)_{0.975} (the critical value) is usually interpreted as indicating that s a 2 ( ν a ) s_{"a"}^2 (ii(nu)_{"a"}) is larger than s b 2 ( ν b ) s_{"b"}^2 (ii(nu)_{"b"}) by a statistically significant amount; and that the probability of a value of F ii(F) as large as that observed, if the two estimates were estimates of the same variance, is less than 0.050.05 or 0.0250.025, respectively. (Other critical values may also be chosen, such as F 0.99 ii(F)_{0.99}.)

The best estimate of the potential difference of the voltage standard is then V S = V ¯ = 10.000097 ⁢ V ii(V)_{"S"} = bar(ii(V)) = 10.000097 "unitsml(V)", with s ( V ¯ ) = u c = 18 ⁢ µV s(bar(ii(V))) = u_{"c"} = 18 "unitsml(uV)" as given in . This value of u c u_{"c"} and its 9 degreesof freedom are to be compared with u c = 13 ⁢ µV u_{"c"} = 13 "unitsml(uV)" and its 49 degrees of freedom, the result obtained in [] when the existence of a between-day effect was rejected.

In a real measurement an apparent between-day effect should be further investigated, if possible, in order to determine its cause and whether a systematic effect is present that would negate the use of ANOVA methods. As pointed out at the beginning of this example, ANOVA techniques are designed to identify and evaluate components of uncertainty arising from random effects; they cannot provide information about components arising from systematic effects.

clause.title: The role of ANOVA in measurementmeasurementrole of ANOVA in

clause.p: This voltage standard example illustrates what is generally termed a balanced, one-stage nested designdesignbalanced nested. It is a one-stage nested design because there is one level of “nesting” of the observations with one factor, the day on which observations are made, being varied in the measurement. It is balanced because the same number of observations is made on each day. The analysis presented in the example can be used to determine if there is an “operator effect”, an “instrument effect”, a “laboratory effect”, a “sample effect”, or even a “method effect” in a particular measurement. Thus in the example, one might imagine replacing the observations made on the J ii(J) different days by observations made on the same day but by J ii(J) different operators; the between-day component of variance becomes then a component of variance associated with different operators.

As noted in , ANOVA methods are widely used in the certification of reference materialsreference materialscertification of (RMs) by interlaboratory testing. Such certification usually involves having a number of independent, equally competent laboratories measure samples of a material for the property for which the material is to be certified. It is generally assumed that the differences between individual results, both within and between laboratories, are statistical in nature regardless of the causes. Each laboratory mean is considered an unbiased estimate of the property of the material, and usually the unweighted mean of the laboratory means is assumed to be the best estimate of that property.

An RM certification might involve I ii(I) different laboratories, each of which measures the requisite property of J ii(J) different samples of the material, with each measurement of a sample consisting of K ii(K) independent repeatedobservations. Thus the total number of observations is I J K ii(IJK) and the total number of samples is I J ii(IJ). This is an example of a balanced, two- stage nested design analogous to the one-stage voltage-standard example above. In this case, there are two levels of “nesting” of the observations with two different factors, sample and laboratory, being varied in the measurement. The designdesignbalanced nested is balanced because each sample is observed the same number of times ( K ii(K)) in each laboratory and each laboratory measures the same number of samples ( J ii(J)). In further analogy with the voltage-standard example, in the RM case the purpose of the analysis of the data is to investigate the possible existence of a between-samples effect and a between-laboratories effect, and to determine the proper uncertainty to assign to the best estimate of the value of the property to be certified. In keeping with the previous paragraph, that estimate is assumed to be the mean of the I ii(I) laboratory means, which is also the mean of the I J K ii(IJK) observations.

The importance of varying the input quantities upon which a measurement result depends so that its uncertainty is based on observed data evaluated statistically is pointed out in . Nested designs and the analysis of the resulting data by ANOVA methods can be successfully used in many measurement situations encountered in practice.

Nonetheless, as indicated in , varying all input quantities is rarely feasible due to limited time and resources; at best, in most practical measurement situations, it is only possible to evaluate a few components of uncertainty using ANOVA methods. As pointed out in , many components must be evaluated by scientific judgement using all of the available information on the possible variability of the input quantities in question; in many instances an uncertainty component, such as arises from a between-samples effect, a between-laboratories effect, a between-instruments effect, or a between-operators effect, cannot be evaluated by the statistical analysis of series of observations but must be evaluated from the available pool of information.

clause.title: Measurements on a reference scale: hardness
clause.p: Hardness is an example of a physical concept that cannot be quantified without reference to a method of measurementmethod of measurementunit dependent on the; it has no unit that is independent of such a method. The quantity “hardness” is unlike classical measurable quantities in that it cannot be entered into algebraic equations to define other measurable quantities (though it is sometimes used in empirical equations that relate hardness to another property for a category of materials). Its magnitude is determined by a conventional measurement, that of a linear dimension of an indentation in a block of the material of interest, or sample block. The measurement is made according to a written standard, which includes a description of the “indentor”, the construction of the machine by which the indentor is applied, and the way in which the machine is to be operated. There is more than one written standard, so there is more than one scale of hardness.

The hardness reported is a function (depending on the scale) of the linear dimension that is measured. In the example given in this subclause, it is a linear function of the arithmetic mean or average of the depths of five repeated indentations, but for some other scales the function is nonlinear.

Realizations of the standard machine are kept as national standards (there is no international standard realization); a comparison between a particular machine and the national standard machine is made using a transfer-standard block.

clause.title: The measurement problem
clause.p: In this example, the hardness of a sample block of material is determined on the scale “Rockwell C” using a machine that has been calibrated against the national standard machine. The scale unit of Rockwell-C hardness is 0.002 ⁢ mm 0.002 "unitsml(mm)", with hardness on that scale defined as 100 × ( 0.002 ⁢ mm ) 100 xx (0.002 "unitsml(mm)") minus the average of the depths, measured in mm "unitsml(mm)", of five indentations. The value of that quantity divided by the Rockwell scale unit 0.002 ⁢ mm 0.002 "unitsml(mm)" is called the “HRC hardness index”. In this example, the quantity is called simply “hardness”,
symbol h Rockwell C h_{"Rockwell C"}, and the numerical value of hardness expressed in Rockwell units of length is called the “hardness index”, H Rockwell C ii(H)_{"Rockwell C"}.

clause.title: Mathematical model
clause.p: To the average of the depths of the indentations made in the sample block by the machine used to determine its hardness, or calibration machine, must be added corrections to determine the average of the depths of the indentations that would have been made in the same block by the national standard machine. Thus

dd.p: is the average of the depths of five indentations made by the calibration machine in the sample block;

is the correction obtained from a comparison of the calibration machine with the national standard machine using a transfer-standard block, equal to the average of the depths of 5 m 5m indentations made by the national standard machine in this block, minus the average of the depths of 5 n 5n indentations made in the same block by the calibration machine;

is the difference in hardness (expressed as a difference of average depth of indentation) between the two parts of the transfer-standard block used respectively for indentations by the two machines, assumed zero; and

is the error due to the lack of repeatability of the national standard machine and the incomplete definition of the quantity hardness. Although Δ S ii(Delta)_{"S"} must be assumed to be zero, it has a standard uncertainty associated with it of u ( Δ S ) u(ii(Delta)_{"S"}).

clause.p: Since the partial derivatives, ∂ f / ∂ d ¯ del f//del bar(d), ∂ f / ∂ Δ c del f//del ii(Delta)_{"c"}, ∂ f / ∂ Δ b del f//del ii(Delta)_{"b"}, and ∂ f / ∂ Δ S del f//del ii(Delta)_{"S"} of the function of are all equal to -1-1, the combined standard uncertainty u c ( h ) u_{"c"}(h) of the hardness of the sample block as measured by the calibration machine is simply given by
where for simplicity of notation h ≡ h Rockwell C h -= h_{"Rockwell C"}.

clause.title: Uncertainty of the average depth of indentation d ¯ bar(d) of the sample block, u ( d ¯ ) u(bar(d))
clause.p: Uncertainty of repeated observations. Strict repetition of an observation is not possible because a new indentation cannot be made on the site of an earlier one. Since each indentation must be made on a different site, any variation in the results includes the effect of variations in hardness between different sites. Thus u ( d ¯ ) u(bar(d)), the standard uncertainty of the average of the depths of five indentations in the sample block by the calibration machine, is taken as s p ( d k ) / 5 s_{"p"}(d_k)// sqrt(5), where s p ( d k ) s_{"p"}(d_k) is the pooled experimental standard deviation of the depths of indentations determined by “repeated” measurements on a block known to have very uniform hardness (see ).variancepooled estimate of (or pooled experimental standard deviation)

Uncertainty of indication. Although the correction to d ¯ bar(d) due to the display of the calibration machine is zero, there is an uncertainty in d ¯ bar(d) due to the uncertainty of the indication of depth due to the resolution δ ii(delta) of the display given by u 2 ( δ ) = δ 2 / 12 u^2(ii(delta)) = ii(delta)^2//12 (see ). The estimated variance of d ¯ bar(d) is thus

clause.title: Uncertainty of the correction for the difference between the two machines, u ( Δ c ) u(ii(Delta)_{"c"})
clause.p: As indicated in , Δ c ii(Delta)_c is the correction for the difference between the national standard machine and the calibration machine. This correction may be expressed as Δ c = z ′ S − z ′ ii(Delta)_c = z'_{"S"} - z', where z ′ S = ( ∑ i = 1 m z ¯ S , i ) / m z'_{"S"} = (sum_{i=1}^m bar(z)_{"S",i})//m is the average depth of the 5 m 5m indentations made by the national standard machine in the transfer-standard block; and z ′ = ( ∑ i = 1 n z ¯ i ) / n z' = (sum_{i=1}^n bar(z)_i)//n is the average depth of the 5 n 5n indentations made in the same block by the calibration machine. Thus, assuming that for the comparison the uncertainty due to the resolution of the display of each machine is negligible, the estimated variance of Δ c ii(Delta)_"c" is

dd.p: is the average of the experimental variances of the means of each of the m m series of indentations z S , i k z_{"S",ik} made by the standard machine;

dl.dt: s av 2 ( z ¯ ) = [ ∑ i = 1 n s 2 ( z ¯ i ) s_{"av"}^2(bar(z)) = [sum_{i=1}^n s^2 (bar(z)_i)//n]

dd.p: is the average of the experimental variances of the means of each of the n n series of indentations z i k z_{ik} made by the calibration machine.

note.p: The variances s av 2 ( z ¯ S ) s_{"av"}^2(bar(z)_{"S"}) and s av 2 ( z ¯ ) s_{"av"}^2(bar(z)) are pooled estimates of variance — see the discussion of .variancepooled estimate of (or pooled experimental standard deviation)

clause.title: Uncertainty of the correction due to variations in the hardness of the transfer-standard block, u ( Δ b ) u(ii(Delta)_{"b"})
clause.p: OIML International Recommendation R 12, Verification and calibration of Rockwell C hardness standardized blocks, requires that the maximum and minimum depths of indentation obtained from five measurements onthe transfer-standard block shall not differ by more than a fraction x x of the average depth of indentation, where x x is a function of the hardness level. Let, therefore, the maximum difference in the depths of indentation over the entire block be x z ′ xz', where z ′ z' is as defined in with n = 5 n = 5. Also let the maximum difference be described by a triangular probability distribution about the average value x z ′ / 2 xz'//2 (on the likely assumption that values near the central value are more probable than extreme values — see ). Then, if in in a = x z ′ / 2 a = xz'//2, the estimated variance of the correction to the average depth of indentation due to differences of the hardnesses presented respectively to the standard machine and the calibration machine is
As indicated in , it is assumed that the best estimate of the correction Δ b ii(Delta)_{"b"} itself is zero.

clause.title: Uncertainty of the national standard machine and the definition of hardness, u ( Δ S ) u(ii(Delta)_{"S"})
clause.p: The uncertainty of the national standard machine together with the uncertainty due to incomplete definition of the quantity hardness is reported as an estimated standard deviation u ( Δ S ) u(ii(Delta)_{"S"}) (a quantity of dimension length).

clause.title: The combined standard uncertainty, u c ( h ) u_{"c"}(h)
clause.p: Collection of the individual terms discussed in to and their substitution into yields for the estimated variance of the measurement of hardness
and the combined standard uncertainty is u c ( h ) u_{"c"}(h).

clause.title: Numerical example
clause.p: The data for this example are summarized in .

clause.p: The scale is Rockwell C, designated HRC. The Rockwell scale unit is 0.002 ⁢ mm 0.002 "unitsml(mm)", and thus in and in the following, it is understood that (for example) “36.036.0 Rockwell scale unit” means 36.0 × ( 0.002 ⁢ mm ) = 0.072 ⁢ mm 36.0 xx (0.002 "unitsml(mm)") = 0.072 "unitsml(mm)" and is simply a convenient way of expressing the data and results.
If the values for the relevant quantities given in are substituted into , one obtains the following two expressions:

where for the purpose of the calculation of uncertainty it is adequate to take z ′ = d ¯ = 36.0 Rockwell scale unit z' = bar(d) = 36.0" Rockwell scale unit". Thus, if it is assumed that Δ c = 0 ii(Delta)_"c" = 0, the hardness of the sample block is

h Rockwell C = 64.0 Rockwell scale unit h_{"Rockwell C"} = 64.0" Rockwell scale unit" or 0.1280 ⁢ mm 0.1280 "unitsml(mm)" with a combined standard uncertainty of u c = 0.55 Rockwell scale unit u_{"c"} = 0.55" Rockwell scale unit" or 0.0011 ⁢ mm 0.0011 "unitsml(mm)".
The hardness index of the block is h Rockwell C / ( 0.002 ⁢ mm ) = ( 0.1280 ⁢ mm ) / ( 0.002 ⁢ mm ) h_{"Rockwell C"}//(0.002 "unitsml(mm)") = (0.1280 "unitsml(mm)")//(0.002 "unitsml(mm)"), or
H Rockwell C = 64.0 ii(H)_{"Rockwell C"} = 64.0 HRC with a combined standard uncertainty of u c = 0.55 u_{c} = 0.55 HRC.

In addition to the component of uncertainty due to the national standard machine and the definition of hardness, u ( Δ S ) = 0.5 u(ii(Delta)_{"S"}) = 0.5 Rockwell scale unit, the significant components of uncertainty are those of the repeatability of the machine, s p ( d k ) / 5 = 0.20 s_{"p"} (d_k)//sqrt(5) = 0.20 Rockwell scale unit; and the variation of the hardness of the transfer-standard block, which is ( x z ′ ) 2 / 24 = 0.11 (x z')^2//24 = 0.11 Rockwell scale unit. The effective degrees of freedom of u c u_{"c"} can be evaluated using the Welch-Satterthwaite formula in the manner illustrated in .

note.p: The English translation of Recommendation INC-1 (1980) given in the to this Guide (see ) is that of the final version of the Recommendation and is taken from a BIPM internal report. It is consisfent with the authoritative French text of the Recommendation given in BIPM Proc.-Verb. Com. Int. Poids et Mesures 49 and reproduced in , of this Guide. The English translation of Recommendation INC-1 (1980) given in Metrologia 17 is that of a draft and differs slightly from the translation given in the BIPM internal report and thus in .

ISO 5725:1986, Precision of test methods — Determination of repeatability and reproducibility for a standard test method by inter-laboratory tests, International Organization for Standardization (Geneva,Switzerland)

note.p:
This standard is currently
Footnote to the 2008 version:
ISO 5725:1986 has been replaced by a series of six parts. ISO 5725 consists of the following parts, under the general title Accuracy (trueness and precision) of measurement methods and results:
Part 1: General principles and definitions
Part 2: Basic method for the determination of repeatability and reproducibility of a standard measurement method
Part 3: Intermediate measures of the precision of a standard measurement method
Part 4: Basic methods for the determination of the trueness of a standard measurement method
Part 5: Alternative methods for the determination of the precision of a standard measurement method
Part 6: Use in practice of accuracy values being revised. The revision has a new title, “Accuracy (trueness and precision) of measurement methods and results”, and it is composed of six parts.

bibitem.formattedref:
International vocabulary of basic and general terms in metrology, second edition, 1993,
Footnote to the 2008 version:*
The third edition of the vocabulary was published in 2008, under the title JCGM 200:2008, International vocabulary of metrology — Basic and general concepts and associated terms (VIM). * International Organization for Standardization (Geneva, Switzerland)

note.p: The abbreviation of the title of this vocabulary is VIM.
The definitions of terms given in are taken from the revised English text of the VIM in its final form prior to publication.

The second edition of the VIM was published by the International Organization for Standardization (ISO) in the name of the following seven organizations that participate in the work of ISO Technical Advisory Group 4 (TAG 4), the group that supported the development of the VIM: the Bureau International des Poids et Mesures (BIPM), the International Electrotechnical Commission (IEC), the International Federation of Clinical Chemistry (IFCC), ISO, the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC), the International Union of Pure and Applied Physics (IUPAP), and the International Organization of Legal Metrology (OIML).
The first edition of the VIM was published by ISO in 1984 in the name of the BIPM, IEC, ISO, and OIML.
